title: A Level Pure Mathematics 2 & 3 with R
text:
1 Preface
This book is an illustrative companion for Cambridge A Level Pure Mathematics 2 & 3.
The aim is to help students visualise mathematical ideas using R. The focus is not only on symbolic manipulation, but also on graphs, numerical checks, approximations, simulations and mathematical interpretation.
Each chapter contains:

key mathematical ideas
visualisations using R
worked examples
short investigations
exam-style practice
reflection questions







Note



This is a visual and computational companion. Exact algebraic methods remain essential for written examination work.

title: 2  Algebra
text:
2.1 Overview
This chapter develops six connected algebraic ideas:
The central aim is to connect symbolic algebra, graphical interpretation and numerical checking.

title: 2  Algebra
2  Algebra
text:
2.2 1.1 The modulus function

2.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
The modulus of a real number is its distance from zero on the number line.
\[
|x| =
\begin{cases}
x, & x \geq 0,\\
-x, & x < 0.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore, \(|x|\) is always non-negative.
For example,
\[
|5|=5,\qquad |-5|=5.
\]
A useful equation rule is:
\[
|A|=k,\quad k\geq 0
\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad
A=k \ \text{or}\ A=-k.
\]
If \(k<0\), then the equation \(|A|=k\) has no real solution.


2.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
)

df$y <- abs(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title = "Graph of y = |x|",
    subtitle = "The rule changes at x = 0.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.1: The graph of y = |x|.





The sharp corner at the origin occurs because the function changes from \(y=-x\) to \(y=x\).


2.2.3 Worked example
Solve
\[
|3x-2|=10.
\]
Since \(|A|=10\) means \(A=10\) or \(A=-10\), we solve
\[
3x-2=10
\]
or
\[
3x-2=-10.
\]
From the first equation,
\[
3x=12,
\]
so
\[
x=4.
\]
From the second equation,
\[
3x=-8,
\]
so
\[
x=-\dfrac{8}{3}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
x=4
\quad \text{or} \quad
x=-\dfrac{8}{3}.
\]


2.2.4 Short investigation
Change the value of \(a\) in the code below and observe the graph of \(y=|x-a|\).


Code
a <- 2

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-5, 7, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- abs(df$x - a)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = a, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Graph of y = |x - ", a, "|"),
    subtitle = "The turning point occurs at x = a.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.2: Exploring the family y = |x - a|.







2.2.5 Exercises 1.1

2.2.5.1 Bronze

Solve \(|x|=7\).
Solve \(|x|=0\).
Solve \(|x|=-3\).
Solve \(|2x|=8\).
Solve \(|x+1|=5\).



2.2.5.2 Silver

Solve \(|3x-1|=8\).
Solve \(|5-2x|=9\).
Solve \(|4x+7|=3\).
Solve \(|6-3x|=12\).
Solve \(|2x+5|=|x-1|\).



2.2.5.3 Gold

Solve \(|3x-4|=2x+1\).
Solve \(|x+6|=3x-2\).
Solve \(|2x-5|=|x+4|\).
Find all real values of \(x\) such that \(|x-2|=x^2-4\).
Find all real values of \(x\) such that \(|2x+1|=x^2+x-2\).

title: 2  Algebra
text:
2.3 1.2 Graphs involving modulus functions

2.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
For a linear expression \(ax+b\), the graph
\[
y=|ax+b|
\]
has a turning point where
\[
ax+b=0.
\]
Solving,
\[
x=-\dfrac{b}{a}.
\]
The graph is V-shaped because the output is reflected above the \(x\)-axis.


2.3.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-2, 5, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- abs(2 * df$x - 3)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 3/2, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("point", x = 3/2, y = 0, size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = 3/2, y = 0.8, label = "turning point") +
  labs(
    title = "Graph of y = |2x - 3|",
    subtitle = "The turning point occurs where 2x - 3 = 0.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.3: The graph of y = |2x - 3|.







2.3.3 Worked example
Sketch
\[
y=|3x+6|.
\]
The expression inside the modulus is zero when
\[
3x+6=0.
\]
Therefore,
\[
x=-2.
\]
So the turning point is
\[
(-2,0).
\]
For \(x\geq -2\),
\[
|3x+6|=3x+6.
\]
For \(x<-2\),
\[
|3x+6|=-(3x+6)=-3x-6.
\]
Hence the graph is made from two straight-line pieces meeting at \((-2,0)\).


2.3.4 Short investigation
The following code plots several graphs of the form \(y=|mx+c|\).


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = rep(seq(-5, 5, length.out = 600), 3),
  case = rep(c("m = 1, c = 0", "m = 2, c = -3", "m = -1, c = 2"), each = 600)
)

df$y <- ifelse(
  df$case == "m = 1, c = 0",
  abs(df$x),
  ifelse(
    df$case == "m = 2, c = -3",
    abs(2 * df$x - 3),
    abs(-df$x + 2)
  )
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = case)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Graphs of y = |mx + c|",
    subtitle = "Changing m and c changes the steepness and turning point.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Function"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.4: A family of graphs y = |mx + c|.







2.3.5 Exercises 1.2

2.3.5.1 Bronze

State the turning point of \(y=|x-4|\).
State the turning point of \(y=|x+3|\).
State the turning point of \(y=|2x-6|\).
State the turning point of \(y=|5x+10|\).
State the turning point of \(y=|-3x+9|\).



2.3.5.2 Silver

Sketch \(y=|x-2|\).
Sketch \(y=|2x+4|\).
Sketch \(y=|3x-6|\).
Find the coordinates where \(y=|x-1|\) meets \(y=3\).
Find the coordinates where \(y=|2x+1|\) meets \(y=5\).



2.3.5.3 Gold

Solve graphically and algebraically: \(|x-3|=2\).
Solve graphically and algebraically: \(|2x+1|=3\).
Find the coordinates of intersection of \(y=|x+2|\) and \(y=x+4\).
Find the coordinates of intersection of \(y=|2x-1|\) and \(y=x+2\).
Determine the values of \(k\) for which \(|x-1|=k\) has exactly two distinct real solutions.

title: 2  Algebra
text:
2.4 1.3 Modulus inequalities

2.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
Modulus inequalities can often be interpreted using distance.
For example,
\[
|x-a|<r
\]
means that the distance from \(x\) to \(a\) is less than \(r\).
So
\[
a-r<x<a+r.
\]
Similarly,
\[
|x-a|>r
\]
means that the distance from \(x\) to \(a\) is greater than \(r\).
So
\[
x<a-r
\quad \text{or} \quad
x>a+r.
\]


2.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-4, 8, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- abs(df$x - 2)
df$inside <- df$y < 3

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = subset(df, inside),
    aes(ymin = y, ymax = 3),
    alpha = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 3, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 5, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = 1.4, label = "-1 < x < 5") +
  labs(
    title = "Graph of y = |x - 2| and y = 3",
    subtitle = "The shaded region shows where |x - 2| < 3.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.5: The shaded solution region for |x - 2| < 3.







2.4.3 Worked example
Solve
\[
|x-5|\leq 4.
\]
This means the distance from \(x\) to \(5\) is at most \(4\).
Therefore,
\[
-4\leq x-5\leq 4.
\]
Adding \(5\) throughout gives
\[
1\leq x\leq 9.
\]
So the solution is
\[
x\in[1,9].
\]


2.4.4 Short investigation
Change the value of \(r\) below and observe how the solution interval changes.


Code
r <- 2.5

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-4, 6, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- abs(df$x - 1)
df$inside <- df$y < r

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = subset(df, inside),
    aes(ymin = y, ymax = r),
    alpha = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = r, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1 - r, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1 + r, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 1,
    y = r / 2,
    label = paste0(1 - r, " < x < ", 1 + r)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Graph of y = |x - 1| and y = ", r),
    subtitle = "The shaded region changes as r changes.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.6: Exploring the shaded region for |x - 1| < r.







2.4.5 Exercises 1.3

2.4.5.1 Bronze

Solve \(|x|<4\).
Solve \(|x|>6\).
Solve \(|x-2|<5\).
Solve \(|x+1|\leq 3\).
Solve \(|x-4|\geq 7\).



2.4.5.2 Silver

Solve \(|2x|<10\).
Solve \(|3x-6|\leq 9\).
Solve \(|5-2x|<7\).
Solve \(|4x+8|>12\).
Solve \(|6-3x|\geq 15\).



2.4.5.3 Gold

Solve \(|2x-1|<5\).
Solve \(|3x+4|\leq 2\).
Solve \(|x-2|<|x+4|\).
Solve \(|2x+1|\geq |x-5|\).
Find all values of \(k\) such that \(|x-3|<k\) has no real solution.

title: 2  Algebra
text:
2.5 1.4 Polynomial division

2.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
Polynomial division is based on the division algorithm.
If \(P(x)\) is divided by \(D(x)\), then
\[
P(x)=D(x)Q(x)+R(x),
\]
where \(Q(x)\) is the quotient and \(R(x)\) is the remainder.
If the divisor is linear, then the remainder is constant.
If the divisor is quadratic, then the remainder is at most linear.
For examination work, the safest written method is long division. Synthetic division is a useful checking method for divisors of the form \(x-a\), but it should not replace the full written method unless it is explicitly allowed.


2.5.2 Worked example using long division
Divide
\[
x^3-2x^2-5x+6
\]
by
\[
x-1.
\]
We divide term by term.
First,
\[
\dfrac{x^3}{x}=x^2.
\]
Multiply \(x-1\) by \(x^2\):
\[
x^2(x-1)=x^3-x^2.
\]
Subtract this from the original polynomial:
\[
\begin{aligned}
x^3-2x^2-5x+6-(x^3-x^2)
&=x^3-2x^2-5x+6-x^3+x^2\\
&=-x^2-5x+6.
\end{aligned}
\]
Next,
\[
\dfrac{-x^2}{x}=-x.
\]
Multiply \(x-1\) by \(-x\):
\[
-x(x-1)=-x^2+x.
\]
Subtract:
\[
\begin{aligned}
-x^2-5x+6-(-x^2+x)
&=-x^2-5x+6+x^2-x\\
&=-6x+6.
\end{aligned}
\]
Finally,
\[
\dfrac{-6x}{x}=-6.
\]
Multiply \(x-1\) by \(-6\):
\[
-6(x-1)=-6x+6.
\]
Subtract:
\[
(-6x+6)-(-6x+6)=0.
\]
Therefore,
\[
x^3-2x^2-5x+6=(x-1)(x^2-x-6).
\]
So the quotient is
\[
x^2-x-6
\]
and the remainder is
\[
0.
\]


2.5.3 Synthetic division check
Synthetic division gives a quick check when the divisor has the form \(x-a\).
Here, the divisor is \(x-1\), so \(a=1\).
The coefficients of
\[
x^3-2x^2-5x+6
\]
are
\[
1,\ -2,\ -5,\ 6.
\]
The synthetic division check is
\[
\begin{array}{c|rrrr}
1 & 1 & -2 & -5 & 6\\
  &   & 1 & -1 & -6\\
\hline
  & 1 & -1 & -6 & 0
\end{array}
\]
This gives quotient coefficients
\[
1,\ -1,\ -6,
\]
so the quotient is
\[
x^2-x-6.
\]
The final number is \(0\), so the remainder is \(0\).
This agrees with the long division result.






Warning



Synthetic division is included here as a checking method. For examination working, present the long division method unless the examination explicitly allows synthetic division.




2.5.4 Visualisation using R


Code
f <- function(x) x^3 - 2*x^2 - 5*x + 6

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-3.5, 4, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- f(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  annotate("point", x = 1, y = 0, size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = 1.2, y = 6, label = "x = 1") +
  labs(
    title = expression(f(x) == x^3 - 2*x^2 - 5*x + 6),
    subtitle = "Since f(1) = 0, division by x - 1 leaves remainder 0.",
    x = "x",
    y = "f(x)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.7: The graph of f(x) = x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6.







2.5.5 Short investigation
Use R to check the identity
\[
P(x)=D(x)Q(x)+R(x).
\]


Code
P <- function(x) x^3 - 2*x^2 - 5*x + 6
D <- function(x) x - 1
Q <- function(x) x^2 - x - 6
R <- function(x) 0

test_values <- c(-2, 0, 1, 3)

data.frame(
  x = test_values,
  P_x = P(test_values),
  DQ_plus_R = D(test_values) * Q(test_values) + R(test_values)
)


   x P_x DQ_plus_R
1 -2   0         0
2  0   6         6
3  1   0         0
4  3   0         0




2.5.6 Synthetic division in R
The following code performs the synthetic division check.


Code
root <- 1
coefficients <- c(1, -2, -5, 6)

bottom_row <- numeric(length(coefficients))
bottom_row[1] <- coefficients[1]

for (i in 2:length(coefficients)) {
  bottom_row[i] <- coefficients[i] + root * bottom_row[i - 1]
}

data.frame(
  coefficient = coefficients,
  synthetic_bottom_row = bottom_row
)


  coefficient synthetic_bottom_row
1           1                    1
2          -2                   -1
3          -5                   -6
4           6                    0


The last entry is the remainder. The previous entries are the quotient coefficients.


2.5.7 Exercises 1.4

2.5.7.1 Bronze

Divide \(x^2+5x+6\) by \(x+2\).
Divide \(x^2-9\) by \(x-3\).
Divide \(x^2-4x+3\) by \(x-1\).
Divide \(x^3-1\) by \(x-1\).
Divide \(x^3+8\) by \(x+2\).



2.5.7.2 Silver

Divide \(x^3+2x^2-x-2\) by \(x+2\).
Divide \(x^3-4x^2+x+6\) by \(x-2\).
Divide \(2x^3+3x^2-8x+3\) by \(x+3\).
Divide \(x^4-1\) by \(x-1\).
Divide \(x^3+3x^2+3x+1\) by \(x+1\).



2.5.7.3 Gold

Divide \(x^4-3x^3+2x^2+x-1\) by \(x-1\).
Divide \(2x^4-x^3-7x^2+5x+3\) by \(x+1\).
Divide \(x^4+2x^3-3x^2-4x+4\) by \(x^2-1\).
Divide \(2x^4+3x^3-x^2+5x-2\) by \(x^2+x-1\).
Given that \(P(x)=x^3+ax^2+bx+6\) is divisible by \(x-2\), find a relation between \(a\) and \(b\).

title: 2  Algebra
text:
2.6 1.5 The factor theorem

2.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
The factor theorem states:
If
\[
P(a)=0,
\]
then
\[
x-a
\]
is a factor of \(P(x)\).
Conversely, if \(x-a\) is a factor of \(P(x)\), then
\[
P(a)=0.
\]


2.6.2 Worked example
Show that \(x-2\) is a factor of
\[
P(x)=x^3-5x^2+2x+8.
\]
By the factor theorem, \(x-2\) is a factor if \(P(2)=0\).
Now,
\[
P(2)=2^3-5(2)^2+2(2)+8.
\]
So
\[
P(2)=8-20+4+8=0.
\]
Therefore,
\[
x-2
\]
is a factor of \(P(x)\).
To factorise fully, divide \(P(x)\) by \(x-2\).
Using long division,
\[
x^3-5x^2+2x+8=(x-2)(x^2-3x-4).
\]
Then
\[
x^2-3x-4=(x-4)(x+1).
\]
Therefore,
\[
P(x)=(x-2)(x-4)(x+1).
\]


2.6.3 Synthetic division check
For the divisor \(x-2\), we use \(a=2\).
The coefficients of
\[
x^3-5x^2+2x+8
\]
are
\[
1,\ -5,\ 2,\ 8.
\]
The synthetic division check is
\[
\begin{array}{c|rrrr}
2 & 1 & -5 & 2 & 8\\
  &   & 2 & -6 & -8\\
\hline
  & 1 & -3 & -4 & 0
\end{array}
\]
This confirms that the quotient is
\[
x^2-3x-4
\]
and the remainder is \(0\).


2.6.4 Visualisation using R


Code
P <- function(x) x^3 - 5*x^2 + 2*x + 8

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-2, 5, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- P(df$x)

roots <- data.frame(
  x = c(-1, 2, 4),
  y = c(0, 0, 0)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  geom_point(data = roots, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = expression(P(x) == x^3 - 5*x^2 + 2*x + 8),
    subtitle = "The x-intercepts correspond to linear factors.",
    x = "x",
    y = "P(x)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.8: The graph of P(x) = x^3 - 5x^2 + 2x + 8.







2.6.5 Short investigation
Change the roots \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\) below.


Code
a <- -2
b <- 1
c <- 3

f <- function(x) (x - a) * (x - b) * (x - c)

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-5, 5, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- f(df$x)

root_df <- data.frame(
  x = c(a, b, c),
  y = c(0, 0, 0)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  geom_point(data = root_df, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Graph of f(x) = (x - a)(x - b)(x - c)",
    subtitle = "The chosen values of a, b and c become the roots.",
    x = "x",
    y = "f(x)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.9: Exploring f(x) = (x - a)(x - b)(x - c).







2.6.6 Exercises 1.5

2.6.6.1 Bronze

Show that \(x-1\) is a factor of \(x^2-3x+2\).
Show that \(x+2\) is a factor of \(x^2+x-2\).
Show that \(x-3\) is a factor of \(x^2-5x+6\).
Show that \(x+1\) is a factor of \(x^3+1\).
Show that \(x-2\) is a factor of \(x^3-8\).



2.6.6.2 Silver

Show that \(x-1\) is a factor of \(x^3-4x^2+x+2\).
Show that \(x+2\) is a factor of \(x^3+3x^2-4x-12\).
Factorise \(x^3-6x^2+11x-6\) fully.
Factorise \(x^3+x^2-4x-4\) fully.
Factorise \(2x^3-3x^2-8x+12\) fully.



2.6.6.3 Gold

Find \(k\) if \(x-2\) is a factor of \(x^3+kx^2-4x+4\).
Find \(k\) if \(x+1\) is a factor of \(2x^3+kx^2-5x+6\).
Given that \(x-3\) is a factor of \(x^3+ax^2+bx-9\), find a relation between \(a\) and \(b\).
The polynomial \(x^3+px^2+qx-6\) has roots \(1\), \(2\) and \(r\). Find \(p\), \(q\) and \(r\).
The polynomial \(2x^3+ax^2+bx-6\) has \(x-1\) and \(x+2\) as factors. Find \(a\) and \(b\).

title: 2  Algebra
text:
2.7 1.6 The remainder theorem

2.7.1 Key mathematical ideas
The remainder theorem states:
When \(P(x)\) is divided by \(x-a\), the remainder is
\[
P(a).
\]
More generally, when \(P(x)\) is divided by \(ax-b\), the remainder is
\[
P\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right).
\]
This is connected to polynomial division because if
\[
P(x)=(x-a)Q(x)+R,
\]
then substituting \(x=a\) gives
\[
P(a)=(a-a)Q(a)+R.
\]
Therefore,
\[
P(a)=R.
\]


2.7.2 Worked example
Find the remainder when
\[
P(x)=x^3-2x^2-5x+6
\]
is divided by \(x-2\).
By the remainder theorem, the remainder is \(P(2)\).
Now,
\[
P(2)=2^3-2(2)^2-5(2)+6.
\]
So
\[
P(2)=8-8-10+6=-4.
\]
Therefore, the remainder is
\[
-4.
\]


2.7.3 Long division check
Dividing
\[
x^3-2x^2-5x+6
\]
by
\[
x-2
\]
using long division gives:
First,
\[
\dfrac{x^3}{x}=x^2.
\]
Subtract
\[
x^2(x-2)=x^3-2x^2.
\]
Then
\[
x^3-2x^2-5x+6-(x^3-2x^2)=-5x+6.
\]
Next,
\[
\dfrac{-5x}{x}=-5.
\]
Subtract
\[
-5(x-2)=-5x+10.
\]
Then
\[
(-5x+6)-(-5x+10)=-4.
\]
Therefore,
\[
x^3-2x^2-5x+6=(x-2)(x^2-5)-4.
\]
The remainder is again
\[
-4.
\]


2.7.4 Synthetic division check
For the divisor \(x-2\), we use \(a=2\).
The coefficients are
\[
1,\ -2,\ -5,\ 6.
\]
The synthetic division check is
\[
\begin{array}{c|rrrr}
2 & 1 & -2 & -5 & 6\\
  &   & 2 & 0 & -10\\
\hline
  & 1 & 0 & -5 & -4
\end{array}
\]
This gives quotient
\[
x^2-5
\]
and remainder
\[
-4.
\]


2.7.5 Visualisation using R


Code
P <- function(x) x^3 - 2*x^2 - 5*x + 6

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-3.5, 4, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- P(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("point", x = 2, y = P(2), size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = 2.45, y = P(2), label = "P(2) = -4") +
  labs(
    title = expression(P(x) == x^3 - 2*x^2 - 5*x + 6),
    subtitle = "The y-value at x = 2 gives the remainder.",
    x = "x",
    y = "P(x)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 2.10: The value P(2) gives the remainder when P(x) is divided by x - 2.







2.7.6 Short investigation
Evaluate the same polynomial at different values of \(a\).


Code
P <- function(x) x^3 - 2*x^2 - 5*x + 6

a_values <- c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3)

data.frame(
  divisor = paste0("x - (", a_values, ")"),
  a = a_values,
  remainder = P(a_values)
)


   divisor  a remainder
1 x - (-2) -2         0
2 x - (-1) -1         8
3  x - (0)  0         6
4  x - (1)  1         0
5  x - (2)  2        -4
6  x - (3)  3         0




2.7.7 Exercises 1.6

2.7.7.1 Bronze

Find the remainder when \(x^2+3x+1\) is divided by \(x-1\).
Find the remainder when \(x^2-5x+6\) is divided by \(x-2\).
Find the remainder when \(x^3+1\) is divided by \(x+1\).
Find the remainder when \(x^3-8\) is divided by \(x-2\).
Find the remainder when \(2x^2+x-4\) is divided by \(x+2\).



2.7.7.2 Silver

Find the remainder when \(x^3-4x^2+x+6\) is divided by \(x-3\).
Find the remainder when \(2x^3+x^2-5x+1\) is divided by \(x+1\).
Find the remainder when \(x^4-3x^2+2\) is divided by \(x-2\).
Find the remainder when \(3x^3-2x+5\) is divided by \(2x-1\).
Find the remainder when \(x^3+2x^2-x+4\) is divided by \(3x+1\).



2.7.7.3 Gold

Find \(k\) if \(x-2\) leaves remainder \(5\) when it divides \(x^3+kx^2-3x+1\).
Find \(k\) if \(x+1\) leaves remainder \(4\) when it divides \(2x^3+kx^2-x+5\).
The polynomial \(P(x)=x^3+ax^2+bx+2\) leaves remainder \(4\) when divided by \(x-1\) and remainder \(-10\) when divided by \(x+2\). Find two equations in \(a\) and \(b\).
Find the remainder when \(P(x)=x^{50}+x^{25}+1\) is divided by \(x-1\).
Find the remainder when \(P(x)=2x^3-5x^2+7x-3\) is divided by \(2x+3\).

title: 2  Algebra
text:
2.8 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require more than routine application.

2.8.1 STEP-style Question 1
Let
\[
f(x)=|x-a|+|x-b|,
\]
where \(a<b\).
Show that the minimum value of \(f(x)\) is \(b-a\).


2.8.2 STEP-style Question 2
Find all real numbers \(x\) satisfying
\[
|x-1|+|x+2|=7.
\]


2.8.3 STEP-style Question 3
Let
\[
P(x)=x^3+ax^2+bx+c.
\]
Suppose \(P(1)=P(2)=P(3)=0\).
Find \(P(x)\) in factorised form and express \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\).


2.8.4 STEP-style Question 4
Let
\[
P(x)=x^4+ax^3+bx^2+cx+d.
\]
Suppose that \(P(x)\) is divisible by both \(x-1\) and \(x+1\).
Find two equations involving \(a\), \(b\), \(c\) and \(d\).


2.8.5 STEP-style Question 5
Let
\[
P(x)=x^3+px+q.
\]
Suppose that the remainder when \(P(x)\) is divided by \(x-1\) is \(4\), and the remainder when \(P(x)\) is divided by \(x+2\) is \(-5\).
Find \(p\) and \(q\).

title: 2  Algebra
text:
2.9 Reflection questions

What is the difference between solving \(|A|=k\) and solving \(A=k\)?
Why do modulus equations sometimes require checking?
How does the graph of \(y=|ax+b|\) reveal the solution structure?
Why is long division the safest written method for polynomial division?
How can synthetic division be used as a checking method?
How is polynomial division connected to numerical substitution?
What is the difference between the factor theorem and the remainder theorem?
What can R show clearly that algebra alone may hide?
What can algebra prove exactly that a graph can only suggest?

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.1 Overview
This chapter develops eight connected ideas:
The main theme is that logarithms and exponentials are inverse processes. Logarithms allow us to solve equations where the unknown appears in an index, and they also allow us to convert some curved relationships into straight-line relationships.

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.2 2.1 Logarithms to base 10

3.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
A logarithm answers the question:

What power is needed?

For example,
\[
10^2=100.
\]
So
\[
\log_{10}100=2.
\]
In general,
\[
\log_{10}x=y
\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad
10^y=x.
\]
The expression \(\log_{10}x\) is defined only when
\[
x>0.
\]


3.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0.05, 100, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- log10(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 10, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Graph of y = log10(x)",
    subtitle = "The graph passes through (1, 0) and (10, 1).",
    x = "x",
    y = "log10(x)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.1: The graph of y = log10(x).





The graph passes through \((1,0)\) because
\[
\log_{10}1=0.
\]
It passes through \((10,1)\) because
\[
\log_{10}10=1.
\]


3.2.3 Worked example
Evaluate
\[
\log_{10}1000.
\]
Since
\[
1000=10^3,
\]
we have
\[
\log_{10}1000=3.
\]


3.2.4 Short investigation
Use R to compare powers of \(10\) with their logarithms.


Code
powers <- -3:5

data.frame(
  power = powers,
  value = 10^powers,
  log10_value = log10(10^powers)
)


  power value log10_value
1    -3 1e-03          -3
2    -2 1e-02          -2
3    -1 1e-01          -1
4     0 1e+00           0
5     1 1e+01           1
6     2 1e+02           2
7     3 1e+03           3
8     4 1e+04           4
9     5 1e+05           5


Observe that
\[
\log_{10}(10^n)=n.
\]


3.2.5 Exercises 2.1

3.2.5.1 Bronze

Evaluate \(\log_{10}100\).
Evaluate \(\log_{10}100000\).
Evaluate \(\log_{10}0.01\).
Write \(10^4=10000\) in logarithmic form.
Solve \(\log_{10}x=3\).



3.2.5.2 Silver

Evaluate \(\log_{10}\sqrt{10}\).
Evaluate \(\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{1000}\right)\).
Solve \(10^{2x}=1000\).
Solve \(\log_{10}(5x)=2\).
Solve \(\log_{10}(x-3)=1\).



3.2.5.3 Gold

Solve \(10^{x+1}=1000\).
Solve \(\log_{10}(3x+1)=2\).
Solve \(10^{2x-1}=100\).
Solve \(\log_{10}(x+5)=\log_{10}(2x-1)\).
Solve \(\log_{10}(x^2)=2\).

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.3 2.2 Logarithms to base \(a\)

3.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
For \(a>0\) and \(a\neq1\),
\[
\log_a x=y
\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad
a^y=x.
\]
For example,
\[
2^3=8
\]
so
\[
\log_2 8=3.
\]
Important facts include:
\[
\log_a1=0,
\]
\[
\log_a a=1,
\]
and
\[
\log_a(a^x)=x.
\]


3.3.2 Visualisation using R


Code
x_values <- seq(0.05, 20, length.out = 800)

df <- data.frame(
  x = rep(x_values, 3),
  base = rep(c("base 2", "base e", "base 10"), each = length(x_values))
)

df$y <- ifelse(
  df$base == "base 2",
  log(df$x, base = 2),
  ifelse(
    df$base == "base e",
    log(df$x),
    log10(df$x)
  )
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = base)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Graphs of y = log_a(x)",
    subtitle = "All logarithm graphs pass through (1, 0).",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Function"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.2: Graphs of logarithms with different bases.





All logarithm graphs pass through \((1,0)\) because
\[
\log_a1=0
\]
for every valid base \(a\).


3.3.3 Worked example
Evaluate
\[
\log_3 81.
\]
Since
\[
81=3^4,
\]
we have
\[
\log_3 81=4.
\]


3.3.4 Short investigation
Change the base \(a\) below.


Code
a <- 3

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0.05, 30, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- log(df$x, base = a)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Graph of y = log base ", a, " of x"),
    subtitle = "Change a and observe how the graph changes.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.3: Exploring y = log_a(x) for different values of a.







3.3.5 Exercises 2.2

3.3.5.1 Bronze

Evaluate \(\log_2 8\).
Evaluate \(\log_3 1\).
Evaluate \(\log_5 5\).
Evaluate \(\log_4\left(\frac{1}{16}\right)\).
If \(\log_7 x=2\), find \(x\).



3.3.5.2 Silver

Solve \(\log_2 x=5\).
Evaluate \(\log_3\left(\frac{1}{81}\right)\).
Solve \(\log_4(x+1)=3\).
Solve \(2^x=16\).
Evaluate \(\log_a(a^5)\), where \(a>0\) and \(a\neq1\).



3.3.5.3 Gold

Solve \(\log_2(x-1)=3\).
Solve \(\log_3(2x+1)=2\).
Solve \(\log_5(x^2)=2\).
Solve \(\log_2(3x-4)=\log_2(x+6)\).
If \(\log_a81=4\), where \(a>0\) and \(a\neq1\), find \(a\).

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.4 2.3 The laws of logarithms

3.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
For \(a>0\), \(a\neq1\), and positive \(x\) and \(y\),
\[
\log_a(xy)=\log_a x+\log_a y.
\]
This is the product rule.
\[
\log_a\left(\frac{x}{y}\right)=\log_a x-\log_a y.
\]
This is the division rule.
\[
\log_a(x^n)=n\log_a x.
\]
This is the power rule.
A useful special case is
\[
\log_a\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=-\log_a x.
\]


3.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
x_values <- seq(1, 20, length.out = 200)
y_fixed <- 3

df <- data.frame(
  x = x_values
)

df$left_side <- log10(df$x * y_fixed)
df$right_side <- log10(df$x) + log10(y_fixed)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = left_side, linetype = "log10(3x)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = right_side, linetype = "log10(x) + log10(3)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Checking log10(3x) = log10(x) + log10(3)",
    subtitle = "The two graphs overlap.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.4: Numerical check of the logarithm product rule.





The two graphs overlap because the two expressions are equivalent.


3.4.3 Worked example
Simplify
\[
\log_a(3x^2)-\log_a(6x).
\]
Using the division rule,
\[
\log_a(3x^2)-\log_a(6x)
=
\log_a\left(\frac{3x^2}{6x}\right).
\]
So
\[
\log_a(3x^2)-\log_a(6x)
=
\log_a\left(\frac{x}{2}\right),
\]
where \(x>0\).


3.4.4 Short investigation
Use R to check the product, division and power rules numerically.


Code
x <- 8
y <- 4
a <- 2

data.frame(
  rule = c("product", "division", "power"),
  left_side = c(
    log(x * y, base = a),
    log(x / y, base = a),
    log(x^3, base = a)
  ),
  right_side = c(
    log(x, base = a) + log(y, base = a),
    log(x, base = a) - log(y, base = a),
    3 * log(x, base = a)
  )
)


      rule left_side right_side
1  product         5          5
2 division         1          1
3    power         9          9




3.4.5 Exercises 2.3

3.4.5.1 Bronze

Simplify \(\log_a x+\log_a y\).
Simplify \(\log_a x-\log_a y\).
Simplify \(3\log_a x\).
Simplify \(\log_a\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\).
Evaluate \(\log_a(a^7)\).



3.4.5.2 Silver

Evaluate \(\log_2 8+\log_2 4\).
Evaluate \(\log_3 27-\log_3 3\).
Evaluate \(\log_5(25^2)\).
If \(\log_{10}2=p\), express \(\log_{10}5\) in terms of \(p\).
Simplify \(\log_a\left(\frac{x^2}{y^3}\right)\).



3.4.5.3 Gold

Solve \(\log_2x+\log_2(x-2)=3\).
Solve \(\log_3(2x+1)-\log_3(x-1)=1\).
Solve \(2\log_{10}x=\log_{10}(3x+4)\).
Solve \(\log_a x+\log_a(x+6)=2\log_a5\), where \(a>0\) and \(a\neq1\).
Simplify \(\log_a\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}}{y^2}\right)\).

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.5 2.4 Solving logarithmic equations

3.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
When solving logarithmic equations, always check the domain.
For example,
\[
\log_a(x-3)
\]
is defined only when
\[
x-3>0.
\]
So
\[
x>3.
\]
If
\[
\log_a A=\log_a B,
\]
then
\[
A=B,
\]
provided \(A>0\) and \(B>0\).


3.5.2 Worked example
Solve
\[
\log_2(x+6)-\log_2x=2.
\]
First, the domain requires
\[
x>0.
\]
Using the division rule,
\[
\log_2\left(\frac{x+6}{x}\right)=2.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\frac{x+6}{x}=2^2.
\]
So
\[
\frac{x+6}{x}=4.
\]
Hence,
\[
x+6=4x.
\]
Therefore,
\[
3x=6,
\]
so
\[
x=2.
\]
Since \(x=2>0\), the solution is valid.


3.5.3 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0.1, 10, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- log(df$x + 6, base = 2) - log(df$x, base = 2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 2, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("point", x = 2, y = 2, size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Graph of y = log2(x + 6) - log2(x)",
    subtitle = "The equation is solved where y = 2.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.5: Solving log2(x + 6) - log2(x) = 2 graphically.







3.5.4 Short investigation
Change the value of \(c\) in
\[
\log_2(x+c)-\log_2x=2.
\]


Code
c <- 6

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0.1, 20, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- log(df$x + c, base = 2) - log(df$x, base = 2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 2, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Graph of y = log2(x + ", c, ") - log2(x)"),
    subtitle = "Change c and observe how the solution changes.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.6: Exploring log2(x + c) - log2(x) = 2.







3.5.5 Exercises 2.4

3.5.5.1 Bronze

Solve \(\log_2x=4\).
Solve \(\log_3(x-2)=2\).
Solve \(\log_{10}(2x)=1\).
Solve \(\log_5(x+1)=0\).
Solve \(\log_4(3x-1)=1\).



3.5.5.2 Silver

Solve \(\log_2x+\log_24=5\).
Solve \(\log_3(x-1)=\log_35\).
Solve \(\log_{10}(x+2)=\log_{10}(3x-4)\).
Solve \(\log_5x+\log_5(x-4)=1\).
Solve \(2\log_2x=\log_2(3x+10)\).



3.5.5.3 Gold

Solve \(\log_2(x+6)-\log_2x=2\).
Solve \(\log_3(2x-1)+\log_3(x+1)=2\).
Solve \(\log_{10}(x^2-5x+6)=0\).
Solve \(\log_2(x+1)=1+\log_2(x-2)\).
Solve \(\log_a x+\log_a(x-1)=\log_a6\), where \(a>0\) and \(a\neq1\).

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.6 2.5 Solving exponential equations

3.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
Exponential equations often have the unknown in an index.
For example,
\[
2^x=7.
\]
Taking natural logarithms gives
\[
\ln(2^x)=\ln7.
\]
Using the power rule,
\[
x\ln2=\ln7.
\]
Therefore,
\[
x=\frac{\ln7}{\ln2}.
\]


3.6.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-2, 5, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- 2^df$x

solution <- log(7) / log(2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 7, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = solution, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("point", x = solution, y = 7, size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Graph of y = 2^x",
    subtitle = "The equation 2^x = 7 has one real solution.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.7: Solving 2^x = 7 graphically.







3.6.3 Worked example
Solve
\[
5^{x-2}=17.
\]
Taking natural logarithms,
\[
\ln(5^{x-2})=\ln17.
\]
Using the power rule,
\[
(x-2)\ln5=\ln17.
\]
Therefore,
\[
x-2=\frac{\ln17}{\ln5}.
\]
So
\[
x=2+\frac{\ln17}{\ln5}.
\]


3.6.4 Short investigation
Change the base \(a\) and value \(b\) in
\[
a^x=b.
\]


Code
a <- 3
b <- 20

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-2, 5, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- a^df$x
solution <- log(b) / log(a)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = b, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = solution, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Graph of y = ", a, "^x"),
    subtitle = paste0("Solution to ", a, "^x = ", b, " is x = ", round(solution, 3)),
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.8: Exploring a^x = b.







3.6.5 Exercises 2.5

3.6.5.1 Bronze

Solve \(2^x=8\).
Solve \(3^x=\frac{1}{9}\).
Solve \(5^{x+1}=125\).
Solve \(10^{2x}=1000\).
Solve \(e^x=1\).



3.6.5.2 Silver

Solve \(2^{x+1}=16\).
Solve \(3^{2x-1}=27\).
Solve \(4^x=8\).
Solve \(5^{x-2}=\frac{1}{25}\).
Solve \(e^{2x}=7\).



3.6.5.3 Gold

Solve \(2^x=5\), giving your answer in exact logarithmic form.
Solve \(3^{x+1}=20\).
Solve \(e^{3x-2}=10\).
Solve \(4^x=2^{x+3}\).
Solve \(2^{2x}-5(2^x)+4=0\).

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.7 2.6 Solving exponential inequalities

3.7.1 Key mathematical ideas
For \(a>1\), the function
\[
y=a^x
\]
is increasing.
Therefore,
\[
a^x>a^k
\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad
x>k.
\]
For \(0<a<1\), the function
\[
y=a^x
\]
is decreasing.
Therefore,
\[
a^x>a^k
\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad
x<k.
\]
The direction of the inequality depends on whether the base is greater than \(1\) or between \(0\) and \(1\).


3.7.2 Visualisation using R


Code
x_values <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 800)

df <- data.frame(
  x = rep(x_values, 2),
  function_name = rep(c("2^x", "(1/2)^x"), each = length(x_values))
)

df$y <- ifelse(df$function_name == "2^x", 2^df$x, (1/2)^df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = function_name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Increasing and decreasing exponentials",
    subtitle = "The base determines the direction of inequality reasoning.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Function"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.9: Comparing increasing and decreasing exponential functions.







3.7.3 Worked example
Solve
\[
3^{2x-1}\geq 27.
\]
Since
\[
27=3^3,
\]
we have
\[
3^{2x-1}\geq3^3.
\]
The base \(3\) is greater than \(1\), so the inequality direction is unchanged:
\[
2x-1\geq3.
\]
Therefore,
\[
2x\geq4,
\]
so
\[
x\geq2.
\]


3.7.4 Short investigation
Change the base \(a\) below. Try both \(a>1\) and \(0<a<1\).


Code
a <- 2
target <- 8

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-5, 5, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- a^df$x

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = target, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Graph of y = ", a, "^x"),
    subtitle = paste0("Compare y with ", target),
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.10: Exploring exponential inequalities with different bases.







3.7.5 Exercises 2.6

3.7.5.1 Bronze

Solve \(2^x>8\).
Solve \(3^x\leq\frac{1}{9}\).
Solve \(10^x<100\).
Solve \(e^x\geq1\).
Solve \(\left(\frac12\right)^x>8\).



3.7.5.2 Silver

Solve \(2^{x+1}<16\).
Solve \(3^{2x-1}\geq27\).
Solve \(5^{x-2}>\frac{1}{25}\).
Solve \(e^{3x}<5\).
Solve \(\left(\frac13\right)^{x+2}\leq9\).



3.7.5.3 Gold

Solve \(2^{2x}-5(2^x)+4\leq0\).
Solve \(3^{2x}-10(3^x)+9>0\).
Solve \(e^{2x}-4e^x+3<0\).
Solve \(4^x>2^{x+3}\).
Solve \(\left(\frac12\right)^{x-1}\geq4\).

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.8 2.7 Natural logarithms

3.8.1 Key mathematical ideas
The number \(e\) is approximately
\[
e\approx2.718.
\]
The logarithm to base \(e\) is called the natural logarithm.
We write
\[
\ln x
\]
instead of
\[
\log_e x.
\]
The functions
\[
y=e^x
\]
and
\[
y=\ln x
\]
are inverse functions.
This means:
\[
e^{\ln x}=x
\]
and
\[
\ln(e^x)=x.
\]


3.8.2 Visualisation using R


Code
x_exp <- seq(-3, 3, length.out = 800)
x_log <- seq(0.05, 20, length.out = 800)

df_exp <- data.frame(
  x = x_exp,
  y = exp(x_exp),
  function_name = "y = e^x"
)

df_log <- data.frame(
  x = x_log,
  y = log(x_log),
  function_name = "y = ln(x)"
)

df_line <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-3, 8, length.out = 300),
  y = seq(-3, 8, length.out = 300),
  function_name = "y = x"
)

df <- rbind(df_exp, df_log, df_line)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = function_name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-3, 8), ylim = c(-3, 8)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "The inverse relationship between e^x and ln(x)",
    subtitle = "The graphs are reflections in the line y = x.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Function"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.11: The graphs of y = e^x and y = ln(x) are reflections in y = x.







3.8.3 Worked example
Solve
\[
\ln(x+2)-\ln(x-1)=\ln2.
\]
First, the domain requires
\[
x+2>0
\]
and
\[
x-1>0.
\]
So
\[
x>1.
\]
Using the division rule,
\[
\ln\left(\frac{x+2}{x-1}\right)=\ln2.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\frac{x+2}{x-1}=2.
\]
So
\[
x+2=2x-2.
\]
Hence,
\[
x=4.
\]
This satisfies \(x>1\), so the solution is valid.


3.8.4 Short investigation
Use R to check that \(e^x\) and \(\ln x\) undo each other.


Code
values <- c(0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10)

data.frame(
  x = values,
  exp_log_x = exp(log(values)),
  log_exp_x = log(exp(values))
)


     x exp_log_x log_exp_x
1  0.5       0.5       0.5
2  1.0       1.0       1.0
3  2.0       2.0       2.0
4  5.0       5.0       5.0
5 10.0      10.0      10.0




3.8.5 Exercises 2.7

3.8.5.1 Bronze

Evaluate \(\ln e\).
Evaluate \(\ln1\).
If \(\ln x=2\), find \(x\).
Solve \(e^x=5\).
Evaluate \(\ln(e^3)\).



3.8.5.2 Silver

Solve \(\ln(e^{2x})=6\).
Solve \(\ln(x-1)=0\).
Solve \(\ln(2x+3)=\ln7\).
Solve \(e^{x+2}=10\).
Simplify \(\ln(x^2e^3)\), where \(x>0\).



3.8.5.3 Gold

Solve \(\ln x+\ln(x-1)=\ln6\).
Solve \(2\ln x=\ln(3x+10)\).
Solve \(\ln(x+2)-\ln(x-1)=\ln2\).
Solve \(e^x+e^{-x}=5\).
Solve \(\ln(x^2-4)=\ln(3x)\).

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.9 2.8 Transforming relationships to linear form

3.9.1 Key mathematical ideas
Logarithms can transform some curved relationships into straight-line relationships.
If
\[
y=kx^n,
\]
then taking natural logarithms gives
\[
\ln y=\ln(kx^n).
\]
So
\[
\ln y=\ln k+n\ln x.
\]
This has the form
\[
Y=mX+c,
\]
where
\[
Y=\ln y,
\quad
X=\ln x,
\quad
m=n,
\quad
c=\ln k.
\]
If
\[
y=ka^x,
\]
then
\[
\ln y=\ln(ka^x).
\]
So
\[
\ln y=\ln k+x\ln a.
\]
This is linear in \(x\).


3.9.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(1, 20, length.out = 200)
)

df$y <- 3 * df$x^2
df$log_x <- log(df$x)
df$log_y <- log(df$y)

ggplot(df, aes(x = log_x, y = log_y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Linear form of y = 3x^2",
    subtitle = "Plotting ln(y) against ln(x) gives a straight line.",
    x = "ln(x)",
    y = "ln(y)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.12: Linearising y = 3x^2 using logarithms.





For
\[
y=3x^2,
\]
we have
\[
\ln y=2\ln x+\ln3.
\]
So the gradient of the line is \(2\), and the intercept is \(\ln3\).


3.9.3 Worked example
The variables \(x\) and \(y\) satisfy
\[
y=kx^n.
\]
The graph of \(\ln y\) against \(\ln x\) is a straight line passing through \((0,1)\) and \((2,5)\).
Find \(k\) and \(n\).
Since
\[
\ln y=n\ln x+\ln k,
\]
the gradient is \(n\).
Therefore,
\[
n=\frac{5-1}{2-0}=2.
\]
The intercept is \(1\), so
\[
\ln k=1.
\]
Hence,
\[
k=e.
\]
Therefore,
\[
y=ex^2.
\]


3.9.4 Short investigation
The code below creates data from a power law, then plots the linearised relationship.


Code
k <- 4
n <- 1.5

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(1, 50, length.out = 100)
)

df$y <- k * df$x^n
df$ln_x <- log(df$x)
df$ln_y <- log(df$y)

ggplot(df, aes(x = ln_x, y = ln_y)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Linearising y = kx^n",
    subtitle = "The gradient estimates n and the intercept estimates ln(k).",
    x = "ln(x)",
    y = "ln(y)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 3.13: A power law becomes linear on a log-log graph.







3.9.5 Exercises 2.8

3.9.5.1 Bronze

Rewrite \(y=4x^3\) in linear form using natural logarithms.
Rewrite \(y=7e^{2x}\) in linear form using natural logarithms.
Rewrite \(y=5(3^x)\) in linear form using natural logarithms.
If \(\ln y=2\ln x+\ln6\), express \(y\) in the form \(kx^n\).
If \(\ln y=-0.4x+\ln10\), express \(y\) in exponential form.



3.9.5.2 Silver

For \(y=kx^n\), a graph of \(\ln y\) against \(\ln x\) passes through \((0,1)\) and \((2,5)\). Find \(k\) and \(n\).
For \(y=ka^x\), a graph of \(\ln y\) against \(x\) passes through \((0,3)\) and \((4,5)\). Find \(k\) and \(a\).
A graph of \(\log_{10}y\) against \(\log_{10}x\) has gradient \(-2\) and intercept \(1\). Express \(y\) in terms of \(x\).
A graph of \(\ln y\) against \(x\) has gradient \(\ln2\) and intercept \(\ln3\). Express \(y\) in terms of \(x\).
If \(\ln y=-3\ln x+\ln4\), express \(y\) in terms of \(x\).



3.9.5.3 Gold

The variables \(x\) and \(y\) satisfy \(y=kx^n\). Given that \((x,y)=(2,12)\) and \((8,96)\), find \(k\) and \(n\).
The variables \(x\) and \(y\) satisfy \(y=ka^x\). Given that \((x,y)=(1,6)\) and \((3,24)\), find \(k\) and \(a\).
The graph of \(\ln y\) against \(x\) is a straight line through \((1,2.2)\) and \((5,3.0)\). Given that \(y=ka^x\), find \(k\) and \(a\).
The graph of \(\ln y\) against \(\ln x\) is a straight line through \((1,5)\) and \((3,-1)\). Given that \(y=kx^n\), find \(k\) and \(n\).
The relationship \(y=Ae^{-bx^2}\) gives a straight line when \(\ln y\) is plotted against \(x^2\). The line passes through \((0,2)\) and \((4,0)\), where the horizontal coordinate is \(x^2\). Find \(A\) and \(b\).

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.10 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require more than routine application.

3.10.1 STEP-style Question 1
Solve
\[
\log_2x+\log_4x=6.
\]


3.10.2 STEP-style Question 2
Solve
\[
2^x+2^{-x}=5.
\]


3.10.3 STEP-style Question 3
Let
\[
y=kx^n
\]
where \(k>0\) and \(x>0\). The graph of \(\ln y\) against \(\ln x\) is a straight line. Prove that its gradient is \(n\).


3.10.4 STEP-style Question 4
Let \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) be positive real numbers not equal to \(1\). Show that
\[
\log_a b \cdot \log_b c \cdot \log_c a=1.
\]


3.10.5 STEP-style Question 5
Solve
\[
\ln x+\ln(4-x)=\ln3.
\]
Be careful about the domain.

title: 3  Logarithmic and Exponential Functions
text:
3.11 Reflection questions

What does a logarithm actually measure?
Why must the argument of a logarithm be positive?
Why does the equation \(\log_aA=\log_aB\) imply \(A=B\)?
Why can logarithms help us solve exponential equations?
When solving an exponential inequality, why does the base matter?
How are \(e^x\) and \(\ln x\) related geometrically?
Why does plotting \(\ln y\) against \(\ln x\) help with relationships of the form \(y=kx^n\)?
What can R show clearly that algebra alone may hide?

title: 4  Trigonometry
text:
4.1 Overview
This chapter develops five connected ideas:
The central theme is that trigonometric expressions can often be transformed into simpler or more useful forms. These transformations are essential for solving equations, proving identities and preparing for later work in differentiation and integration.

title: 4  Trigonometry
4  Trigonometry
section: 4.2 3.1 The cosecant, secant and cotangent ratios
text:
4.2 3.1 The cosecant, secant and cotangent ratios

4.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
The three reciprocal trigonometric functions are defined by
\[
\operatorname{cosec}\theta=\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta},
\]
\[
\sec\theta=\dfrac{1}{\cos\theta},
\]
and
\[
\cot\theta=\dfrac{1}{\tan\theta}.
\]
Since
\[
\tan\theta=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta},
\]
we also have
\[
\cot\theta=\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}.
\]
These definitions immediately show where the reciprocal functions are undefined.
The function \(\operatorname{cosec}\theta\) is undefined when
\[
\sin\theta=0.
\]
The function \(\sec\theta\) is undefined when
\[
\cos\theta=0.
\]
The function \(\cot\theta\) is undefined when
\[
\sin\theta=0.
\]


4.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
theta <- seq(0, 360, length.out = 3000)

df <- data.frame(
  theta = rep(theta, 3),
  function_name = rep(c("cosec(theta)", "sec(theta)", "cot(theta)"), each = length(theta))
)

df$y <- ifelse(
  df$function_name == "cosec(theta)",
  1 / sin_deg(df$theta),
  ifelse(
    df$function_name == "sec(theta)",
    1 / cos_deg(df$theta),
    1 / tan_deg(df$theta)
  )
)

df$y[abs(df$y) > 8] <- NA

ggplot(df, aes(x = theta, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.0, na.rm = TRUE) +
  facet_wrap(~ function_name, ncol = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-8, 8)) +
  labs(
    title = "Reciprocal trigonometric functions",
    subtitle = "Large values near vertical asymptotes have been omitted for readability.",
    x = "theta in degrees",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 4.1: Graphs of y = cosec(theta), y = sec(theta), and y = cot(theta).





The vertical asymptotes occur where the denominator of the reciprocal function is zero.


4.2.3 Worked example
Find the exact value of
\[
\operatorname{cosec}240^\circ.
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{cosec}\theta=\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta},
\]
we have
\[
\operatorname{cosec}240^\circ=\dfrac{1}{\sin240^\circ}.
\]
Now \(240^\circ\) is in the third quadrant, where sine is negative. Its reference angle is \(60^\circ\), so
\[
\sin240^\circ=-\sin60^\circ.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\sin240^\circ=-\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}.
\]
Hence,
\[
\operatorname{cosec}240^\circ=-\dfrac{2}{\sqrt3}
=
-\dfrac{2\sqrt3}{3}.
\]


4.2.4 Short investigation
Use R to compare \(\sin\theta\) and \(\operatorname{cosec}\theta\).


Code
theta <- seq(0, 360, length.out = 3000)

df <- data.frame(
  theta = rep(theta, 2),
  function_name = rep(c("sin(theta)", "cosec(theta)"), each = length(theta))
)

df$y <- ifelse(
  df$function_name == "sin(theta)",
  sin_deg(df$theta),
  1 / sin_deg(df$theta)
)

df$y[abs(df$y) > 6] <- NA

ggplot(df, aes(x = theta, y = y, linetype = function_name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.0, na.rm = TRUE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-6, 6)) +
  labs(
    title = "The reciprocal relationship",
    subtitle = "cosec(theta) is the reciprocal of sin(theta).",
    x = "theta in degrees",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Function"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 4.2: Comparing y = sin(theta) and y = cosec(theta).







4.2.5 Exercises 3.1

4.2.5.1 Bronze

Evaluate \(\sec60^\circ\).
Evaluate \(\operatorname{cosec}30^\circ\).
Evaluate \(\cot45^\circ\).
Evaluate \(\sec180^\circ\).
Evaluate \(\operatorname{cosec}270^\circ\).



4.2.5.2 Silver

Solve \(\sec\theta=2\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).
Solve \(\operatorname{cosec}\theta=2\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).
Solve \(\cot\theta=\sqrt3\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).
Find the exact value of \(\operatorname{cosec}240^\circ\).
Solve \(\sec\theta=-2\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).



4.2.5.3 Gold

Solve \(2\sec^2\theta-5\sec\theta+2=0\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).
Solve \(3\operatorname{cosec}^2\theta-4\operatorname{cosec}\theta+1=0\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).
Solve \(\cot^2\theta=3\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).
Prove that \(\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta\equiv1\).
Solve \(\sec\theta+\tan\theta=1\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).

title: 4  Trigonometry
text:
4.3 3.2 Compound angle formulae

4.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
The compound angle formulae allow us to expand trigonometric functions of sums and differences.
For sine,
\[
\sin(A+B)\equiv\sin A\cos B+\cos A\sin B,
\]
and
\[
\sin(A-B)\equiv\sin A\cos B-\cos A\sin B.
\]
For cosine,
\[
\cos(A+B)\equiv\cos A\cos B-\sin A\sin B,
\]
and
\[
\cos(A-B)\equiv\cos A\cos B+\sin A\sin B.
\]
For tangent,
\[
\tan(A+B)\equiv
\dfrac{\tan A+\tan B}{1-\tan A\tan B},
\]
and
\[
\tan(A-B)\equiv
\dfrac{\tan A-\tan B}{1+\tan A\tan B}.
\]


4.3.2 Visualisation using R


Code
A <- seq(0, 360, length.out = 800)
B <- 40

df <- data.frame(
  A = A
)

df$left_side <- sin_deg(df$A + B)
df$right_side <- sin_deg(df$A) * cos_deg(B) + cos_deg(df$A) * sin_deg(B)

ggplot(df, aes(x = A)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = left_side, linetype = "sin(A + B)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = right_side, linetype = "sin A cos B + cos A sin B"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Compound angle formula for sine",
    subtitle = "The two graphs overlap because the expressions are identical.",
    x = "A in degrees",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 4.3: Checking sin(A + B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B numerically.







4.3.3 Worked example
Find the exact value of
\[
\sin75^\circ.
\]
Write
\[
75^\circ=45^\circ+30^\circ.
\]
Then
\[
\sin75^\circ=\sin(45^\circ+30^\circ).
\]
Using the compound angle formula,
\[
\sin(45^\circ+30^\circ)
=
\sin45^\circ\cos30^\circ+\cos45^\circ\sin30^\circ.
\]
Substituting exact values,
\[
\sin75^\circ
=
\dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}
+
\dfrac{\sqrt2}{2}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\sin75^\circ
=
\dfrac{\sqrt6+\sqrt2}{4}.
\]


4.3.4 Short investigation
Change the value of \(B\) and observe that the two expressions remain equal.


Code
B <- 65

df <- data.frame(
  A = seq(0, 360, length.out = 800)
)

df$left_side <- sin_deg(df$A + B)
df$right_side <- sin_deg(df$A) * cos_deg(B) + cos_deg(df$A) * sin_deg(B)

ggplot(df, aes(x = A)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = left_side, linetype = "left side"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = right_side, linetype = "right side"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Checking sin(A + ", B, ")"),
    subtitle = "Changing B does not change the identity.",
    x = "A in degrees",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 4.4: Exploring the identity for sin(A + B).







4.3.5 Exercises 3.2

4.3.5.1 Bronze

Expand \(\sin(A+B)\).
Expand \(\cos(A-B)\).
Expand \(\tan(A+B)\).
Find the exact value of \(\sin75^\circ\).
Find the exact value of \(\cos75^\circ\).



4.3.5.2 Silver

Find the exact value of \(\tan75^\circ\).
Find the exact value of \(\sin15^\circ\).
Find the exact value of \(\cos15^\circ\).
Expand and simplify \(\sin(x+30^\circ)\).
Expand and simplify \(\cos(x-60^\circ)\).



4.3.5.3 Gold

Solve \(\sin(x+30^\circ)=\cos x\) for \(0^\circ\leq x<360^\circ\).
Solve \(\cos(x+60^\circ)=\sin x\) for \(0^\circ\leq x<360^\circ\).
Solve \(\tan(x+45^\circ)=3\) for \(0^\circ\leq x<180^\circ\).
Prove that \(\cos(A+B)+\cos(A-B)\equiv2\cos A\cos B\).
Given that \(\sin A=\dfrac{3}{5}\), \(\cos B=\dfrac{5}{13}\), and \(A\) and \(B\) are acute, find \(\sin(A+B)\).

title: 4  Trigonometry
text:
4.4 3.3 Double angle formulae

4.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
The double angle formulae are special cases of the compound angle formulae.
For sine,
\[
\sin2A\equiv2\sin A\cos A.
\]
For cosine,
\[
\cos2A\equiv\cos^2A-\sin^2A.
\]
Using \(\sin^2A+\cos^2A\equiv1\), this can also be written as
\[
\cos2A\equiv2\cos^2A-1,
\]
or
\[
\cos2A\equiv1-2\sin^2A.
\]
For tangent,
\[
\tan2A\equiv\dfrac{2\tan A}{1-\tan^2A}.
\]


4.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
A <- seq(0, 360, length.out = 800)

df <- data.frame(
  A = A
)

df$left_side <- sin_deg(2 * df$A)
df$right_side <- 2 * sin_deg(df$A) * cos_deg(df$A)

ggplot(df, aes(x = A)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = left_side, linetype = "sin(2A)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = right_side, linetype = "2 sin(A) cos(A)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Double angle formula for sine",
    subtitle = "The two graphs overlap because the expressions are identical.",
    x = "A in degrees",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 4.5: Checking sin(2A) = 2 sin(A) cos(A).







4.4.3 Worked example
If \(\sin A=\dfrac{3}{5}\) and \(A\) is acute, find \(\sin2A\) and \(\cos2A\).
Since \(A\) is acute,
\[
\cos A>0.
\]
Using \(\sin^2A+\cos^2A=1\),
\[
\cos A=\dfrac{4}{5}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\sin2A=2\sin A\cos A.
\]
So
\[
\sin2A=2\cdot\dfrac{3}{5}\cdot\dfrac{4}{5}=\dfrac{24}{25}.
\]
Also,
\[
\cos2A=\cos^2A-\sin^2A.
\]
Thus,
\[
\cos2A=\dfrac{16}{25}-\dfrac{9}{25}=\dfrac{7}{25}.
\]


4.4.4 Short investigation
Use R to compare the three forms of \(\cos2A\).


Code
A <- seq(0, 360, length.out = 800)

df <- data.frame(
  A = rep(A, 3),
  form = rep(c("cos^2(A) - sin^2(A)", "2cos^2(A) - 1", "1 - 2sin^2(A)"), each = length(A))
)

df$y <- ifelse(
  df$form == "cos^2(A) - sin^2(A)",
  cos_deg(df$A)^2 - sin_deg(df$A)^2,
  ifelse(
    df$form == "2cos^2(A) - 1",
    2 * cos_deg(df$A)^2 - 1,
    1 - 2 * sin_deg(df$A)^2
  )
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = A, y = y, linetype = form)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.0) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Equivalent forms of cos(2A)",
    subtitle = "All three formulae give the same graph.",
    x = "A in degrees",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Form"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 4.6: Three equivalent forms of cos(2A).







4.4.5 Exercises 3.3

4.4.5.1 Bronze

Write down the identity for \(\sin2A\).
Write down three equivalent identities for \(\cos2A\).
Write down the identity for \(\tan2A\).
Use the double angle formula to find \(\sin60^\circ\) from \(\sin30^\circ\) and \(\cos30^\circ\).
Use the double angle formula to find \(\cos120^\circ\) from \(\cos60^\circ\).



4.4.5.2 Silver

If \(\sin A=\dfrac{3}{5}\) and \(A\) is acute, find \(\sin2A\).
If \(\sin A=\dfrac{3}{5}\) and \(A\) is acute, find \(\cos2A\).
If \(\tan A=2\), find \(\tan2A\).
Solve \(\sin2x=1\) for \(0^\circ\leq x\leq180^\circ\).
Solve \(\cos2x=0\) for \(0^\circ\leq x<180^\circ\).



4.4.5.3 Gold

Prove that \(\cos2A\equiv1-2\sin^2A\).
Solve \(2\sin^2x-\sin x=0\) for \(0^\circ\leq x\leq360^\circ\).
Solve \(\cos2x=\cos x\) for \(0^\circ\leq x<360^\circ\).
Solve \(\tan2x=1\) for \(0^\circ\leq x<180^\circ\).
If \(\tan A=\dfrac{3}{4}\) and \(A\) is acute, find \(\sin2A\) and \(\cos2A\).

title: 4  Trigonometry
text:
4.5 3.4 Further trigonometric identities

4.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
The reciprocal functions give two important identities.
Since
\[
\sec\theta=\dfrac{1}{\cos\theta},
\]
and
\[
1+\tan^2\theta\equiv\sec^2\theta,
\]
we have
\[
\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta\equiv1.
\]
Similarly,
\[
1+\cot^2\theta\equiv\operatorname{cosec}^2\theta,
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{cosec}^2\theta-\cot^2\theta\equiv1.
\]
Many trigonometric identities are proved by converting all expressions to sine and cosine.


4.5.2 Visualisation using R


Code
theta <- seq(0, 360, length.out = 3000)

df <- data.frame(
  theta = theta
)

df$y <- (1 / cos_deg(df$theta))^2 - tan_deg(df$theta)^2
df$y[abs(cos_deg(df$theta)) < 0.03] <- NA

ggplot(df, aes(x = theta, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1, na.rm = TRUE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-2, 4)) +
  labs(
    title = "Identity: sec^2(theta) - tan^2(theta) = 1",
    subtitle = "Undefined points near asymptotes are omitted.",
    x = "theta in degrees",
    y = "value"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 4.7: Checking sec^2(theta) - tan^2(theta) = 1.







4.5.3 Worked example
Prove that
\[
(\sec x+\tan x)(\sec x-\tan x)\equiv1.
\]
Using difference of two squares,
\[
(\sec x+\tan x)(\sec x-\tan x)
=
\sec^2x-\tan^2x.
\]
But
\[
\sec^2x-\tan^2x\equiv1.
\]
Therefore,
\[
(\sec x+\tan x)(\sec x-\tan x)\equiv1.
\]


4.5.4 Short investigation
Use R to test an identity numerically.


Code
x <- seq(1, 89, length.out = 10)

data.frame(
  x = round(x, 1),
  left_side = round((1 + tan_deg(x)^2) * cos_deg(x)^2, 6),
  right_side = rep(1, length(x))
)


      x left_side right_side
1   1.0         1          1
2  10.8         1          1
3  20.6         1          1
4  30.3         1          1
5  40.1         1          1
6  49.9         1          1
7  59.7         1          1
8  69.4         1          1
9  79.2         1          1
10 89.0         1          1




4.5.5 Exercises 3.4

4.5.5.1 Bronze

Simplify \(\sec^2x-\tan^2x\).
Simplify \(\operatorname{cosec}^2x-\cot^2x\).
Simplify \(\sin x\sec x\).
Simplify \(\cos x\operatorname{cosec}x\).
Simplify \(\tan x\cot x\).



4.5.5.2 Silver

Simplify \((1-\sin^2x)\sec^2x\).
Simplify \(\dfrac{\sec^2x-1}{\sec^2x}\).
Simplify \(\dfrac{\operatorname{cosec}^2x-1}{\operatorname{cosec}^2x}\).
Prove that \((1+\tan^2x)\cos^2x\equiv1\).
Prove that \((1+\cot^2x)\sin^2x\equiv1\).



4.5.5.3 Gold

Prove that \((\sec x+\tan x)(\sec x-\tan x)\equiv1\).
Prove that \((\operatorname{cosec}x+\cot x)(\operatorname{cosec}x-\cot x)\equiv1\).
Simplify \(\dfrac{1+\sin x}{\cos x}+\dfrac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\).
Show that \(\dfrac{\cos x}{1-\sin x}\equiv\sec x+\tan x\).
Solve \(\sec^2x=4\tan x\) for \(0^\circ\leq x<180^\circ\).

title: 4  Trigonometry
text:
4.6 3.5 Expressing \(a\sin\theta+b\cos\theta\) in the form \(R\sin(\theta\pm\alpha)\) or \(R\cos(\theta\pm\alpha)\)

4.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
Expressions of the form
\[
a\sin\theta+b\cos\theta
\]
can be written as a single sine or cosine function.
For example,
\[
a\sin\theta+b\cos\theta
=
R\sin(\theta+\alpha).
\]
Expanding the right-hand side gives
\[
R\sin(\theta+\alpha)
=
R\sin\theta\cos\alpha+R\cos\theta\sin\alpha.
\]
Comparing coefficients,
\[
R\cos\alpha=a
\]
and
\[
R\sin\alpha=b.
\]
Therefore,
\[
R=\sqrt{a^2+b^2}
\]
and
\[
\tan\alpha=\dfrac{b}{a}.
\]
This form is useful because it makes maximum and minimum values clear.
The maximum value of
\[
R\sin(\theta+\alpha)
\]
is \(R\), and the minimum value is \(-R\).


4.6.2 Visualisation using R


Code
theta <- seq(0, 360, length.out = 800)

alpha <- atan2(4, 3) / deg

df <- data.frame(
  theta = theta
)

df$original <- 3 * sin_deg(df$theta) + 4 * cos_deg(df$theta)
df$single_sine <- 5 * sin_deg(df$theta + alpha)

ggplot(df, aes(x = theta)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = original, linetype = "3sin(theta) + 4cos(theta)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = single_sine, linetype = "5sin(theta + alpha)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Combining sine and cosine",
    subtitle = paste0("Here alpha = ", round(alpha, 1), " degrees."),
    x = "theta in degrees",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 4.8: Writing 3sin(theta) + 4cos(theta) as 5sin(theta + alpha).







4.6.3 Worked example
Express
\[
3\sin\theta+4\cos\theta
\]
in the form
\[
R\sin(\theta+\alpha),
\]
where \(R>0\) and \(0^\circ<\alpha<90^\circ\).
We write
\[
3\sin\theta+4\cos\theta
=
R\sin(\theta+\alpha).
\]
Expanding,
\[
R\sin(\theta+\alpha)
=
R\sin\theta\cos\alpha+R\cos\theta\sin\alpha.
\]
Comparing coefficients gives
\[
R\cos\alpha=3
\]
and
\[
R\sin\alpha=4.
\]
Squaring and adding,
\[
R^2=3^2+4^2=25.
\]
So
\[
R=5.
\]
Also,
\[
\tan\alpha=\dfrac{4}{3}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\alpha=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right).
\]
Hence,
\[
3\sin\theta+4\cos\theta
=
5\sin\left(\theta+\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)\right).
\]


4.6.4 Short investigation
Change the values of \(a\) and \(b\) below.


Code
a <- 5
b <- 12

R <- sqrt(a^2 + b^2)
alpha <- atan2(b, a) / deg

theta <- seq(0, 360, length.out = 800)

df <- data.frame(
  theta = theta
)

df$original <- a * sin_deg(df$theta) + b * cos_deg(df$theta)
df$single_sine <- R * sin_deg(df$theta + alpha)

ggplot(df, aes(x = theta)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = original, linetype = "original"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = single_sine, linetype = "single sine"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = paste0(a, "sin(theta) + ", b, "cos(theta)"),
    subtitle = paste0("R = ", round(R, 3), ", alpha = ", round(alpha, 2), " degrees."),
    x = "theta in degrees",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 4.9: Exploring a sin(theta) + b cos(theta).







4.6.5 Exercises 3.5

4.6.5.1 Bronze

Find \(R\) for \(3\sin\theta+4\cos\theta\).
Express \(3\sin\theta+4\cos\theta\) in the form \(R\sin(\theta+\alpha)\).
Find the maximum and minimum values of \(5\sin(\theta+\alpha)\).
Find \(R\) for \(5\cos\theta+12\sin\theta\).
Express \(\sin\theta+\cos\theta\) in the form \(R\sin(\theta+\alpha)\).



4.6.5.2 Silver

Express \(5\sin\theta+12\cos\theta\) in the form \(R\sin(\theta+\alpha)\).
Express \(4\cos\theta-3\sin\theta\) in the form \(R\cos(\theta+\alpha)\).
Find the maximum and minimum values of \(7\sin\theta-24\cos\theta\).
Solve \(3\sin\theta+4\cos\theta=5\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).
Solve \(\sin\theta+\cos\theta=1\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\).



4.6.5.3 Gold

Solve \(8\sin\theta+15\cos\theta=10\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\), giving answers to \(1\) decimal place.
Find the maximum and minimum values of \(6\sin\theta-8\cos\theta+3\).
Solve \(5\cos\theta+12\sin\theta=0\) for \(0^\circ\leq\theta<360^\circ\), giving answers to \(1\) decimal place.
The maximum value of \(a\sin\theta+b\cos\theta\) is \(10\). If it can be written as \(10\sin(\theta+\alpha)\) where \(\tan\alpha=\dfrac{3}{4}\), find \(a\) and \(b\).
Find the least value of \(12-3\sin\theta+4\cos\theta\).

title: 4  Trigonometry
text:
4.7 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require more than routine application.

4.7.1 STEP-style Question 1
Prove that
\[
\sin3x\equiv3\sin x-4\sin^3x.
\]


4.7.2 STEP-style Question 2
Solve
\[
2\cos2x+3\sin x=0
\]
for \(0^\circ\leq x<360^\circ\), giving answers to \(1\) decimal place.


4.7.3 STEP-style Question 3
Find the exact maximum value of
\[
3\sin x+4\cos x+2.
\]
State the value of \(x\) in the interval \(0^\circ\leq x<360^\circ\) where this maximum occurs.


4.7.4 STEP-style Question 4
Show that
\[
a\sin\theta+b\cos\theta
\]
can always be written in the form
\[
R\sin(\theta+\alpha),
\]
where
\[
R=\sqrt{a^2+b^2}.
\]
Assume \(a>0\) and \(b>0\).


4.7.5 STEP-style Question 5
Solve
\[
\sin x+\sqrt3\cos x=1
\]
for \(0^\circ\leq x<360^\circ\).

title: 4  Trigonometry
text:
4.8 Reflection questions

Why are \(\operatorname{cosec}\theta\), \(\sec\theta\) and \(\cot\theta\) called reciprocal functions?
How do reciprocal functions explain vertical asymptotes?
Why is \(\sin(A+B)\) not equal to \(\sin A+\sin B\)?
How do compound angle formulae lead naturally to double angle formulae?
Why is it often useful to convert an identity into sine and cosine?
How does the form \(R\sin(\theta+\alpha)\) make maximum and minimum values easier to find?
What can R show clearly about trigonometric identities?
What can exact trigonometric algebra prove that a graph can only suggest?

title: 4  Trigonometry
text:
4.9 Answers

4.9.1 Answers to Exercises 3.1

4.9.1.1 Bronze

\(2\).
\(2\).
\(1\).
\(-1\).
\(-1\).



4.9.1.2 Silver

\(\theta=60^\circ\) or \(300^\circ\).
\(\theta=30^\circ\) or \(150^\circ\).
\(\theta=30^\circ\) or \(210^\circ\).
\(-\dfrac{2\sqrt3}{3}\).
\(\theta=120^\circ\) or \(240^\circ\).



4.9.1.3 Gold

\(\theta=60^\circ\) or \(300^\circ\).
\(\theta=90^\circ\).
\(\theta=30^\circ\), \(150^\circ\), \(210^\circ\) or \(330^\circ\).
Since \(\sec^2\theta=\dfrac{1}{\cos^2\theta}\) and \(\tan^2\theta=\dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{\cos^2\theta}\), \[
\sec^2\theta-\tan^2\theta
=
\dfrac{1-\sin^2\theta}{\cos^2\theta}
=
\dfrac{\cos^2\theta}{\cos^2\theta}
=
1.
\]
\(\theta=0^\circ\).




4.9.2 Answers to Exercises 3.2

4.9.2.1 Bronze

\(\sin(A+B)=\sin A\cos B+\cos A\sin B\).
\(\cos(A-B)=\cos A\cos B+\sin A\sin B\).
\(\tan(A+B)=\dfrac{\tan A+\tan B}{1-\tan A\tan B}\).
\(\dfrac{\sqrt6+\sqrt2}{4}\).
\(\dfrac{\sqrt6-\sqrt2}{4}\).



4.9.2.2 Silver

\(2+\sqrt3\).
\(\dfrac{\sqrt6-\sqrt2}{4}\).
\(\dfrac{\sqrt6+\sqrt2}{4}\).
\(\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\sin x+\dfrac{1}{2}\cos x\).
\(\dfrac{1}{2}\cos x+\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\sin x\).



4.9.2.3 Gold

\(x=30^\circ\) or \(210^\circ\).
\(x=15^\circ\) or \(195^\circ\).
\(x=26.6^\circ\).
Expanding both terms, \[
\cos(A+B)+\cos(A-B)
=
(\cos A\cos B-\sin A\sin B)+(\cos A\cos B+\sin A\sin B).
\] Hence, \[
\cos(A+B)+\cos(A-B)=2\cos A\cos B.
\]
\(\dfrac{63}{65}\).




4.9.3 Answers to Exercises 3.3

4.9.3.1 Bronze

\(\sin2A=2\sin A\cos A\).
\(\cos2A=\cos^2A-\sin^2A\), \(\cos2A=2\cos^2A-1\), and \(\cos2A=1-2\sin^2A\).
\(\tan2A=\dfrac{2\tan A}{1-\tan^2A}\).
\(\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\).
\(-\dfrac{1}{2}\).



4.9.3.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{24}{25}\).
\(\dfrac{7}{25}\).
\(-\dfrac{4}{3}\).
\(x=45^\circ\).
\(x=45^\circ\) or \(135^\circ\).



4.9.3.3 Gold

Since \(\cos2A=\cos^2A-\sin^2A\) and \(\cos^2A=1-\sin^2A\), \[
\cos2A=(1-\sin^2A)-\sin^2A=1-2\sin^2A.
\]
\(x=0^\circ\), \(30^\circ\), \(150^\circ\), \(180^\circ\) or \(360^\circ\).
\(x=0^\circ\), \(120^\circ\) or \(240^\circ\).
\(x=22.5^\circ\) or \(112.5^\circ\).
\(\sin2A=\dfrac{24}{25}\) and \(\cos2A=\dfrac{7}{25}\).




4.9.4 Answers to Exercises 3.4

4.9.4.1 Bronze

\(1\).
\(1\).
\(\tan x\).
\(\cot x\).
\(1\).



4.9.4.2 Silver

\(1\).
\(\sin^2x\).
\(\cos^2x\).
Since \(1+\tan^2x=\sec^2x\), \[
(1+\tan^2x)\cos^2x=\sec^2x\cos^2x=1.
\]
Since \(1+\cot^2x=\operatorname{cosec}^2x\), \[
(1+\cot^2x)\sin^2x=\operatorname{cosec}^2x\sin^2x=1.
\]



4.9.4.3 Gold

\(\sec^2x-\tan^2x=1\).
\(\operatorname{cosec}^2x-\cot^2x=1\).
\(2\sec x\).
Multiplying the numerator and denominator by \(1+\sin x\) gives \[
\dfrac{\cos x}{1-\sin x}
=
\dfrac{\cos x(1+\sin x)}{1-\sin^2x}
=
\dfrac{\cos x(1+\sin x)}{\cos^2x}
=
\dfrac{1+\sin x}{\cos x}
=
\sec x+\tan x.
\]
\(x=15^\circ\) or \(75^\circ\).




4.9.5 Answers to Exercises 3.5

4.9.5.1 Bronze

\(R=5\).
\(3\sin\theta+4\cos\theta=5\sin(\theta+\alpha)\), where \(\tan\alpha=\dfrac{4}{3}\).
Maximum \(5\), minimum \(-5\).
\(R=13\).
\(\sin\theta+\cos\theta=\sqrt2\sin(\theta+45^\circ)\).



4.9.5.2 Silver

\(5\sin\theta+12\cos\theta=13\sin(\theta+\alpha)\), where \(\tan\alpha=\dfrac{12}{5}\).
\(4\cos\theta-3\sin\theta=5\cos(\theta+\alpha)\), where \(\tan\alpha=\dfrac{3}{4}\).
Maximum \(25\), minimum \(-25\).
\(\theta=36.9^\circ\).
\(\theta=0^\circ\) or \(90^\circ\).



4.9.5.3 Gold

\(\theta=82.0^\circ\) or \(334.1^\circ\).
Maximum \(13\), minimum \(-7\).
\(\theta=157.4^\circ\) or \(337.4^\circ\).
\(a=8\) and \(b=6\).
\(7\).




4.9.6 Answers to STEP-style extension questions

Using \(\sin(2x+x)\), \[
\sin3x=\sin2x\cos x+\cos2x\sin x.
\] Now \(\sin2x=2\sin x\cos x\) and \(\cos2x=1-2\sin^2x\). Therefore, \[
\sin3x=2\sin x\cos^2x+\sin x(1-2\sin^2x).
\] Since \(\cos^2x=1-\sin^2x\), \[
\sin3x=2\sin x(1-\sin^2x)+\sin x(1-2\sin^2x).
\] Hence, \[
\sin3x=3\sin x-4\sin^3x.
\]
\(x=205.2^\circ\) or \(334.8^\circ\).
The maximum value is \(7\). It occurs when \(3\sin x+4\cos x=5\), so \(x=36.9^\circ\).
Expand \(R\sin(\theta+\alpha)\): \[
R\sin(\theta+\alpha)=R\sin\theta\cos\alpha+R\cos\theta\sin\alpha.
\] Choose \(R\cos\alpha=a\) and \(R\sin\alpha=b\). Squaring and adding gives \[
R^2=a^2+b^2.
\] Hence, \[
R=\sqrt{a^2+b^2}.
\]
Since \[
\sin x+\sqrt3\cos x=2\sin(x+60^\circ),
\] the equation becomes \[
2\sin(x+60^\circ)=1.
\] Hence \(\sin(x+60^\circ)=\dfrac{1}{2}\). Therefore \(x+60^\circ=30^\circ\) or \(150^\circ\) modulo \(360^\circ\), giving \[
x=90^\circ
\] or \[
x=330^\circ.
\]

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.1 Overview
This chapter develops seven connected differentiation ideas:
The central aim is to extend differentiation beyond simple powers of \(x\). We now differentiate products, quotients, exponential functions, logarithmic functions, trigonometric functions, implicit curves and parametric curves.

title: 5  Differentiation
5  Differentiation
text:
5.2 4.1 The product rule

5.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
Suppose
\[
y=uv,
\]
where \(u\) and \(v\) are both functions of \(x\).
Then the product rule is
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
u\dfrac{dv}{dx}
+
v\dfrac{du}{dx}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(uv)=u'v+uv'.
\]
The product rule is needed when a function is naturally written as the product of two differentiable functions.


5.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
f <- function(x) x^2 * exp(x)

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-3, 2, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- f(df$x)
df$exact_derivative <- exp(df$x) * (df$x^2 + 2 * df$x)
df$numeric_derivative <- numeric_derivative(f, df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = exact_derivative, linetype = "exact derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = numeric_derivative, linetype = "numerical derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Derivative of y = x^2 e^x",
    subtitle = "The product rule agrees with numerical gradient estimates.",
    x = "x",
    y = "gradient",
    linetype = "Method"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.1: Comparing the numerical and exact derivative of y = x^2 e^x.







5.2.3 Worked example
Differentiate
\[
y=x^2e^x.
\]
Let
\[
u=x^2
\]
and
\[
v=e^x.
\]
Then
\[
\dfrac{du}{dx}=2x
\]
and
\[
\dfrac{dv}{dx}=e^x.
\]
Using the product rule,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
x^2e^x+e^x(2x).
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
e^x(x^2+2x).
\]


5.2.4 Short investigation
Change the power of \(x\) in the code below.


Code
n <- 3

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-3, 3, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- df$x^n * exp(df$x)
df$dy <- exp(df$x) * (n * df$x^(n - 1) + df$x^n)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y, linetype = "function"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = dy, linetype = "derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Function and derivative for y = x^", n, " e^x"),
    subtitle = "The product rule creates two terms.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.2: Exploring derivatives of y = x^n e^x.







5.2.5 Exercises 4.1

5.2.5.1 Bronze

Differentiate \(y=x(x+3)\).
Differentiate \(y=x^2(x-1)\).
Differentiate \(y=x^3(2x+1)\).
Differentiate \(y=(x+1)(x^2+2)\).
Differentiate \(y=(2x-1)(3x+4)\).



5.2.5.2 Silver

Differentiate \(y=x^2e^x\).
Differentiate \(y=(x+1)e^{2x}\).
Differentiate \(y=x^3\ln x\), where \(x>0\).
Differentiate \(y=(2x-1)\sin x\).
Differentiate \(y=x^2\cos3x\).



5.2.5.3 Gold

Find the stationary point of \(y=xe^{-x}\).
Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=x^2\ln x\) at \(x=1\).
Show that \(y=(x^2+1)e^{-2x}\) has no stationary points.
Show that if \(y=x^ne^x\), where \(n\) is a positive integer, then \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=x^{n-1}e^x(x+n).
\]
Find the \(x\)-coordinates of the stationary points of \(y=(x^2-4)e^x\).

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.3 4.2 The quotient rule

5.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
Suppose
\[
y=\dfrac{u}{v},
\]
where \(u\) and \(v\) are differentiable functions of \(x\) and \(v\neq0\).
Then the quotient rule is
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
\dfrac{v\dfrac{du}{dx}-u\dfrac{dv}{dx}}{v^2}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right)
=
\dfrac{u'v-uv'}{v^2}.
\]
A common error is to differentiate the numerator and denominator separately. In general,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right)
\neq
\dfrac{u'}{v'}.
\]


5.3.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-5, 5, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- df$x / (df$x^2 + 1)
df$dy <- (1 - df$x^2) / (df$x^2 + 1)^2

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y, linetype = "function"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = dy, linetype = "derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Function and derivative",
    subtitle = "For y = x / (x^2 + 1), the derivative is zero at x = -1 and x = 1.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.3: The function y = x / (x^2 + 1) and its derivative.







5.3.3 Worked example
Differentiate
\[
y=\dfrac{x^2+1}{x-1}.
\]
Let
\[
u=x^2+1
\]
and
\[
v=x-1.
\]
Then
\[
\dfrac{du}{dx}=2x
\]
and
\[
\dfrac{dv}{dx}=1.
\]
Using the quotient rule,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
\dfrac{(x-1)(2x)-(x^2+1)(1)}{(x-1)^2}.
\]
Simplifying the numerator,
\[
(x-1)(2x)-(x^2+1)
=
2x^2-2x-x^2-1
=
x^2-2x-1.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
\dfrac{x^2-2x-1}{(x-1)^2}.
\]


5.3.4 Short investigation
Change the numerator and denominator and observe how the derivative changes.


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-4, 6, length.out = 1200)
)

df <- subset(df, abs(x - 1) > 0.05)

df$y <- (df$x^2 + 1) / (df$x - 1)
df$dy <- (df$x^2 - 2 * df$x - 1) / (df$x - 1)^2

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y, linetype = "function"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = dy, linetype = "derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-10, 10)) +
  labs(
    title = "Quotient rule and stationary points",
    subtitle = "Stationary points occur where the derivative is zero.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.4: Stationary points of y = (x^2 + 1)/(x - 1).







5.3.5 Exercises 4.2

5.3.5.1 Bronze

Differentiate \(y=\dfrac{x+1}{x-2}\).
Differentiate \(y=\dfrac{2x-3}{x+4}\).
Differentiate \(y=\dfrac{x^2}{x+1}\).
Differentiate \(y=\dfrac{x^2+1}{x}\).
Differentiate \(y=\dfrac{1}{x^2+1}\).



5.3.5.2 Silver

Differentiate \(y=\dfrac{x^2+3x}{x-1}\).
Differentiate \(y=\dfrac{e^x}{x}\).
Differentiate \(y=\dfrac{\ln x}{x}\), where \(x>0\).
Find the gradient of \(y=\dfrac{x+2}{x^2+1}\) at \(x=1\).
Find the \(x\)-coordinates of the stationary points of \(y=\dfrac{x^2+1}{x-1}\).



5.3.5.3 Gold

Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=\dfrac{x^2-4}{x+1}\) at \(x=0\).
Find the equation of the normal to \(y=\dfrac{2x+1}{x-3}\) at \(x=1\).
Find and classify the stationary points of \(y=\dfrac{x}{x^2+1}\).
Show that \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{ax+b}{cx+d}\right)
=
\dfrac{ad-bc}{(cx+d)^2}.
\]
Given that \(y=\dfrac{x^2+ax+1}{x+1}\) has a stationary point when \(x=2\), find \(a\).

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.4 4.3 Derivatives of exponential functions

5.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
The natural exponential function has the special property
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(e^x\right)=e^x.
\]
Using the chain rule,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(e^{f(x)}\right)=f'(x)e^{f(x)}.
\]
In particular,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(e^{ax+b}\right)=ae^{ax+b}.
\]


5.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-3, 3, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- exp(df$x)
df$dy <- exp(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y, linetype = "function"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = dy, linetype = "derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "The derivative of e^x",
    subtitle = "The function and its derivative are identical.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.5: The function y = e^x is its own derivative.







5.4.3 Worked example
Differentiate
\[
y=e^{3x-2}.
\]
Let
\[
u=3x-2.
\]
Then
\[
\dfrac{du}{dx}=3.
\]
Using the chain rule,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3e^{3x-2}.
\]


5.4.4 Short investigation
Change the value of \(a\) in \(y=e^{ax}\).


Code
a <- 2

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-2, 2, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- exp(a * df$x)
df$dy <- a * exp(a * df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y, linetype = "function"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = dy, linetype = "derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Function and derivative for y = e^(", a, "x)"),
    subtitle = "The chain rule multiplies by the coefficient of x.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.6: Exploring y = e^(ax) and its derivative.







5.4.5 Exercises 4.3

5.4.5.1 Bronze

Differentiate \(y=e^x\).
Differentiate \(y=e^{3x}\).
Differentiate \(y=5e^{2x}\).
Differentiate \(y=e^{-x}\).
Differentiate \(y=e^{1-4x}\).



5.4.5.2 Silver

Differentiate \(y=xe^x\).
Differentiate \(y=x^2e^{3x}\).
Solve \(\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(e^{2x-1}\right)=10\).
Find the stationary point of \(y=(x-2)e^x\).
Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=e^{2x}\) at \(x=0\).



5.4.5.3 Gold

Find the \(x\)-coordinates of the stationary points of \(y=(x^2-3x)e^x\).
Find the stationary point of \(y=\dfrac{e^x}{x+1}\).
Show that the tangent to \(y=e^{-x}\) at \(x=0\) meets the \(x\)-axis at \(x=1\).
Solve \(e^{2x}-3e^x+2=0\).
Find the minimum value of \(y=e^x+e^{-x}\).

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.5 4.4 Derivatives of natural logarithmic functions

5.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
The derivative of the natural logarithm is
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(\ln x)=\dfrac{1}{x},
\quad x>0.
\]
Using the chain rule,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\ln f(x)\right)
=
\dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)}.
\]
In particular,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\ln(ax+b)\right)
=
\dfrac{a}{ax+b}.
\]
The argument of a logarithm must be positive.


5.5.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0.1, 6, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- log(df$x)
df$dy <- 1 / df$x

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y, linetype = "ln(x)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = dy, linetype = "1/x"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Natural logarithm and its derivative",
    subtitle = "The derivative decreases as x increases.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.7: The derivative of y = ln(x) is y = 1/x.







5.5.3 Worked example
Differentiate
\[
y=\ln(2x+5).
\]
Let
\[
f(x)=2x+5.
\]
Then
\[
f'(x)=2.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
\dfrac{2}{2x+5}.
\]


5.5.4 Short investigation
Change the linear expression inside the logarithm.


Code
a <- 2
b <- 3

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0.1, 6, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- log(a * df$x + b)
df$dy <- a / (a * df$x + b)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y, linetype = "function"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = dy, linetype = "derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Function and derivative for y = ln(", a, "x + ", b, ")"),
    subtitle = "The derivative has the form a / (ax + b).",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.8: Exploring derivatives of y = ln(ax + b).







5.5.5 Exercises 4.4

5.5.5.1 Bronze

Differentiate \(y=\ln x\).
Differentiate \(y=\ln(3x)\).
Differentiate \(y=\ln(x+2)\).
Differentiate \(y=5\ln x\).
Differentiate \(y=\ln(x^2)\).



5.5.5.2 Silver

Differentiate \(y=\ln(2x-1)\).
Differentiate \(y=x\ln x\), where \(x>0\).
Differentiate \(y=\ln(x^2+1)\).
Find the stationary point of \(y=\dfrac{\ln x}{x}\), where \(x>0\).
Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=\ln(x+1)\) at \(x=0\).



5.5.5.3 Gold

Find the stationary point of \(y=x^2\ln x\), where \(x>0\).
Find the stationary point of \(y=\dfrac{\ln x}{x^2}\), where \(x>0\).
Show that \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\ln\sqrt{x^2+1}\right)
=
\dfrac{x}{x^2+1}.
\]
Solve \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\ln(x^2-4x+5)\right)=0.
\]
Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=\ln(2x+1)\) that is parallel to \(y=2x+3\).

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.6 4.5 Derivatives of trigonometric functions

5.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
For trigonometric differentiation, angles must be measured in radians.
The key derivatives are
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x)=\cos x,
\]
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(\cos x)=-\sin x,
\]
and
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(\tan x)=\sec^2x.
\]
Using the chain rule,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\sin(ax+b)\right)=a\cos(ax+b),
\]
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\cos(ax+b)\right)=-a\sin(ax+b),
\]
and
\[
