\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\tan(ax+b)\right)=a\sec^2(ax+b).
\]


5.6.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-2 * pi, 2 * pi, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- sin(df$x)
df$dy <- cos(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y, linetype = "sin(x)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = dy, linetype = "cos(x)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "The derivative of sin(x)",
    subtitle = "Angles are measured in radians.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.9: The derivative of y = sin(x) is y = cos(x).







5.6.3 Worked example
Differentiate
\[
y=\sin(3x+1).
\]
Using the chain rule,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3\cos(3x+1).
\]


5.6.4 Short investigation
Compare a trigonometric function with its derivative.


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-2 * pi, 2 * pi, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- cos(2 * df$x)
df$dy <- -2 * sin(2 * df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y, linetype = "cos(2x)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = dy, linetype = "derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Derivative of y = cos(2x)",
    subtitle = "The chain rule changes the amplitude of the derivative.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.10: Function and derivative for y = cos(2x).







5.6.5 Exercises 4.5

5.6.5.1 Bronze

Differentiate \(y=\sin x\).
Differentiate \(y=\cos x\).
Differentiate \(y=\tan x\).
Differentiate \(y=\sin2x\).
Differentiate \(y=\cos(3x+1)\).



5.6.5.2 Silver

Differentiate \(y=x\sin x\).
Differentiate \(y=e^x\sin x\).
Differentiate \(y=\sin^2x\).
Differentiate \(y=\tan2x\).
Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=\sin x\) at \(x=\dfrac{\pi}{6}\).



5.6.5.3 Gold

Find and classify the stationary points of \(y=\sin x+\cos x\) for \(0\leq x<2\pi\).
Find the stationary points of \(y=\sin x\cos x\) for \(0\leq x\leq\pi\).
Solve \(\dfrac{d}{dx}(x+2\cos x)=0\) for \(0\leq x\leq2\pi\).
Prove that \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sec x)=\sec x\tan x.
\]
Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=\tan x\) at \(x=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\).

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.7 4.6 Implicit differentiation

5.7.1 Key mathematical ideas
Implicit differentiation is used when a curve is not written directly in the form
\[
y=f(x).
\]
For example,
\[
x^2+y^2=25
\]
defines a circle. We differentiate both sides with respect to \(x\), remembering that \(y\) is a function of \(x\).
Since
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(y^2)=2y\dfrac{dy}{dx},
\]
we get
\[
2x+2y\dfrac{dy}{dx}=0.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{x}{y}.
\]


5.7.2 Visualisation using R


Code
theta <- seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 800)

circle <- data.frame(
  x = 5 * cos(theta),
  y = 5 * sin(theta)
)

tangent <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-1, 6, length.out = 200)
)

tangent$y <- 4 - (3 / 4) * (tangent$x - 3)

ggplot() +
  geom_path(data = circle, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(data = tangent, aes(x = x, y = y), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1.1) +
  annotate("point", x = 3, y = 4, size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title = "Implicit differentiation on a circle",
    subtitle = "At (3, 4), the gradient is -3/4.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.11: The circle x^2 + y^2 = 25 and the tangent at (3, 4).







5.7.3 Worked example
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) for
\[
x^2+xy+y^2=7.
\]
Differentiate each term with respect to \(x\).
The derivative of \(x^2\) is \(2x\).
For \(xy\), use the product rule:
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(xy)=x\dfrac{dy}{dx}+y.
\]
The derivative of \(y^2\) is
\[
2y\dfrac{dy}{dx}.
\]
So
\[
2x+x\dfrac{dy}{dx}+y+2y\dfrac{dy}{dx}=0.
\]
Collecting the \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) terms gives
\[
(x+2y)\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-(2x+y).
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
-\dfrac{2x+y}{x+2y}.
\]


5.7.4 Short investigation
Change the point on the circle and observe the tangent.


Code
x0 <- 4
y0 <- 3

theta <- seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 800)

circle <- data.frame(
  x = 5 * cos(theta),
  y = 5 * sin(theta)
)

m <- -x0 / y0

tangent <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-1, 6, length.out = 200)
)

tangent$y <- y0 + m * (tangent$x - x0)

ggplot() +
  geom_path(data = circle, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(data = tangent, aes(x = x, y = y), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1.1) +
  annotate("point", x = x0, y = y0, size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title = "Tangent to x^2 + y^2 = 25",
    subtitle = paste0("At (", x0, ", ", y0, "), the gradient is ", round(m, 3), "."),
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.12: Changing the point of tangency on x^2 + y^2 = 25.







5.7.5 Exercises 4.6

5.7.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x^2+y^2=25\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(xy=6\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(y^2=4x\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x^2+xy=10\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x+y^3=7\).



5.7.5.2 Silver

Find the gradient of \(x^2+y^2=25\) at \((3,4)\).
Find the equation of the tangent to \(xy=12\) at \((3,4)\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x^2+xy+y^2=7\).
Find the gradient of \(y^2+xy=6\) at \((2,2)\).
Find the equation of the normal to \(x^2+y^2=10\) at \((1,3)\).



5.7.5.3 Gold

For the circle \(x^2+y^2=r^2\), prove that the tangent is perpendicular to the radius at any point on the circle.
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) for \(x^3+y^3=6xy\).
Find the equation of the tangent to \(x^2+xy+y^2=3\) at \((1,1)\).
Find the points on \(x^2+y^2=25\) where the tangent has gradient \(\dfrac{3}{4}\).
If \(\ln(xy)=x-y\), find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) in terms of \(x\) and \(y\).

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.8 4.7 Parametric differentiation

5.8.1 Key mathematical ideas
A curve may be defined parametrically by
\[
x=f(t),
\qquad
y=g(t).
\]
If both \(x\) and \(y\) depend on \(t\), then
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
\dfrac{\dfrac{dy}{dt}}{\dfrac{dx}{dt}},
\]
provided
\[
\dfrac{dx}{dt}\neq0.
\]
This is the chain rule in the form
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dt}
=
\dfrac{dy}{dx}\dfrac{dx}{dt}.
\]


5.8.2 Visualisation using R


Code
t <- seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 800)

df <- data.frame(
  t = t,
  x = cos(t),
  y = sin(t)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_path(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title = "Parametric circle",
    subtitle = "x = cos(t), y = sin(t).",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.13: The parametric curve x = cos(t), y = sin(t).







5.8.3 Worked example
A curve is defined by
\[
x=t^2,
\qquad
y=t^3.
\]
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\).
First,
\[
\dfrac{dx}{dt}=2t
\]
and
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dt}=3t^2.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
\dfrac{\dfrac{dy}{dt}}{\dfrac{dx}{dt}}
=
\dfrac{3t^2}{2t}.
\]
For \(t\neq0\),
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{3t}{2}.
\]


5.8.4 Short investigation
Change the parametric equations and observe the gradient.


Code
t <- seq(-3, 3, length.out = 800)

df <- data.frame(
  t = t,
  x = t^2,
  y = t^3 - 3 * t
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_path(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Parametric curve",
    subtitle = "x = t^2, y = t^3 - 3t.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 5.14: The parametric curve x = t^2, y = t^3 - 3t.







5.8.5 Exercises 4.7

5.8.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x=t^2\) and \(y=t^3\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x=2t+1\) and \(y=t^2\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x=\sin t\) and \(y=\cos t\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x=e^t\) and \(y=e^{2t}\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x=t+\dfrac{1}{t}\) and \(y=t-\dfrac{1}{t}\).



5.8.5.2 Silver

For \(x=t^2+1\) and \(y=t^3-t\), find the values of \(t\) where the tangent is parallel to the \(x\)-axis.
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x=2\cos t\) and \(y=3\sin t\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x=t^2\) and \(y=\ln t\), where \(t>0\).
Find the equation of the tangent to \(x=t+1\), \(y=t^2+2t\) at \(t=1\).



5.8.5.3 Gold

For \(x=t^2\) and \(y=t^3-3t\), find the values of \(t\) where the tangent is horizontal or vertical.
For \(x=e^t\) and \(y=te^t\), find the equation of the tangent at \(t=0\).
Find \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) if \(x=\cos t\) and \(y=\sin2t\).
For \(x=t^2+1\) and \(y=t^3-3t\), find the values of \(t\) where the tangent is parallel to \(y=3x\).
For \(x=t^2\) and \(y=t^3\), where \(t>0\), show that \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{3}{2}\sqrt{x}.
\]

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.9 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require more than routine application.

5.9.1 STEP-style Question 1
Let \(n\) be a positive integer and define
\[
f(x)=x^ne^{-x},
\qquad x>0.
\]
Show that \(f\) has exactly one stationary point, and find its maximum value.


5.9.2 STEP-style Question 2
Let
\[
f(x)=x+\dfrac{1}{x},
\qquad x>0.
\]
Use differentiation to prove that
\[
x+\dfrac{1}{x}\geq2.
\]


5.9.3 STEP-style Question 3
The curve
\[
x^2+xy+y^2=3
\]
has tangents parallel to the coordinate axes at four points.
Find these four points.


5.9.4 STEP-style Question 4
The parametric curve
\[
x=t^2+1,
\qquad
y=t^3-3t
\]
has tangents parallel to the line \(y=3x\).
Find the corresponding values of \(t\) and the coordinates of the points.


5.9.5 STEP-style Question 5
Find constants \(a\) and \(b\) such that
\[
f(x)=e^x(a\sin x+b\cos x)
\]
satisfies
\[
f'(x)=e^x\sin x.
\]

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.10 Reflection questions

Why is the product rule not simply the product of the derivatives?
Why is the quotient rule more delicate than differentiating numerator and denominator separately?
What makes \(e^x\) special among exponential functions?
Why must logarithmic differentiation respect the domain of the logarithm?
Why must trigonometric derivatives use radians?
What is the difference between explicit and implicit differentiation?
How does parametric differentiation depend on the chain rule?
What can R show clearly about gradients that algebra alone may hide?
What can exact differentiation prove that numerical graphing can only suggest?

title: 5  Differentiation
text:
5.11 Answers

5.11.1 Answers to Exercises 4.1

5.11.1.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2x+3\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3x^2-2x\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=8x^3+3x^2\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3x^2+2x+2\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=12x+5\).



5.11.1.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^x(x^2+2x)\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^{2x}(2x+3)\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=x^2(3\ln x+1)\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2\sin x+(2x-1)\cos x\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2x\cos3x-3x^2\sin3x\).



5.11.1.3 Gold

The stationary point is \(\left(1,\dfrac{1}{e}\right)\).
The tangent is \(y=x-1\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-2e^{-2x}(x^2-x+1)\). Since \(x^2-x+1>0\) for all real \(x\), there are no stationary points.
Using the product rule, \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=nx^{n-1}e^x+x^ne^x=x^{n-1}e^x(x+n).
\]
\(x=-1+\sqrt5\) or \(x=-1-\sqrt5\).




5.11.2 Answers to Exercises 4.2

5.11.2.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{3}{(x-2)^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{11}{(x+4)^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{x^2+2x}{(x+1)^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{x^2-1}{x^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{2x}{(x^2+1)^2}\).



5.11.2.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{x^2-2x-3}{(x-1)^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{e^x(x-1)}{x^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{1-\ln x}{x^2}\).
The gradient is \(-1\).
\(x=1+\sqrt2\) or \(x=1-\sqrt2\).



5.11.2.3 Gold

The tangent is \(y=4x-4\).
The normal is \[
y+\dfrac{3}{2}=\dfrac{4}{7}(x-1).
\]
The stationary points are \(\left(-1,-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\) and \(\left(1,\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\). The first is a minimum and the second is a maximum.
Using the quotient rule, \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{ax+b}{cx+d}\right)
=
\dfrac{a(cx+d)-c(ax+b)}{(cx+d)^2}
=
\dfrac{ad-bc}{(cx+d)^2}.
\]
\(a=-7\).




5.11.3 Answers to Exercises 4.3

5.11.3.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^x\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3e^{3x}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=10e^{2x}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-e^{-x}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-4e^{1-4x}\).



5.11.3.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^x(x+1)\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^{3x}(3x^2+2x)\).
\(x=\dfrac{1+\ln5}{2}\).
The stationary point is \((1,-e)\).
The tangent is \(y=2x+1\).



5.11.3.3 Gold

\(x=\dfrac{3+\sqrt{21}}{2}\) or \(x=\dfrac{3-\sqrt{21}}{2}\).
The stationary point is \((0,1)\).
At \(x=0\), the tangent is \(y=1-x\), which meets the \(x\)-axis at \(x=1\).
\(x=0\) or \(x=\ln2\).
The minimum value is \(2\).




5.11.4 Answers to Exercises 4.4

5.11.4.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{1}{x}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{1}{x}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{1}{x+2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{5}{x}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2}{x}\).



5.11.4.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2}{2x-1}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\ln x+1\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2x}{x^2+1}\).
The stationary point occurs when \(x=e\).
The tangent is \(y=x\).



5.11.4.3 Gold

The stationary point occurs when \(x=e^{-1/2}\).
The stationary point occurs when \(x=\sqrt e\).
Since \(\ln\sqrt{x^2+1}=\dfrac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+1)\), \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\ln\sqrt{x^2+1}\right)
=
\dfrac{1}{2}\cdot\dfrac{2x}{x^2+1}
=
\dfrac{x}{x^2+1}.
\]
\(x=2\).
The tangent is \(y=2x\).




5.11.5 Answers to Exercises 4.5

5.11.5.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\cos x\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\sin x\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\sec^2x\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2\cos2x\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-3\sin(3x+1)\).



5.11.5.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\sin x+x\cos x\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^x(\sin x+\cos x)\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2\sin x\cos x\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2\sec^2(2x)\).
The tangent is \[
y-\dfrac{1}{2}
=
\dfrac{\sqrt3}{2}\left(x-\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right).
\]



5.11.5.3 Gold

The stationary points are \(x=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\) and \(x=\dfrac{5\pi}{4}\). The first is a maximum with value \(\sqrt2\), and the second is a minimum with value \(-\sqrt2\).
\(x=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\) and \(x=\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\).
\(x=\dfrac{\pi}{6}\) or \(x=\dfrac{5\pi}{6}\).
Since \(\sec x=\dfrac{1}{\cos x}\), \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sec x)
=
\dfrac{\sin x}{\cos^2x}
=
\sec x\tan x.
\]
The tangent is \[
y-1=2\left(x-\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right).
\]




5.11.6 Answers to Exercises 4.6

5.11.6.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{x}{y}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{y}{x}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2}{y}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{2x+y}{x}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{1}{3y^2}\).



5.11.6.2 Silver

The gradient is \(-\dfrac{3}{4}\).
The tangent is \[
y-4=-\dfrac{4}{3}(x-3).
\]
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{2x+y}{x+2y}\).
The gradient is \(-\dfrac{1}{3}\).
The normal is \[
y-3=3(x-1).
\]



5.11.6.3 Gold

For \(x^2+y^2=r^2\), \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{x}{y}.
\] The radius from the origin to \((x,y)\) has gradient \(\dfrac{y}{x}\). The product of the two gradients is \(-1\), so the tangent is perpendicular to the radius.
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2y-x^2}{y^2-2x}\).
The tangent is \(y-1=-(x-1)\).
The points are \((-3,4)\) and \((3,-4)\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{y(x-1)}{x(y+1)}\).




5.11.7 Answers to Exercises 4.7

5.11.7.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{3t}{2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=t\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\tan t\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2e^t\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{t^2+1}{t^2-1}\).



5.11.7.2 Silver

The tangent is \(y-8=3(x-4)\).
\(t=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt3}\) or \(t=-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt3}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{3\cos t}{2\sin t}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{1}{2t^2}\).
The tangent is \(y-3=4(x-2)\).



5.11.7.3 Gold

Horizontal tangents occur at \(t=1\) and \(t=-1\). A vertical tangent occurs at \(t=0\).
The tangent is \(y=x-1\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{2\cos2t}{\sin t}\).
\(t=1+\sqrt2\) or \(t=1-\sqrt2\).
Since \(t>0\), \(t=\sqrt x\). Therefore, \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{3t}{2}=\dfrac{3}{2}\sqrt x.
\]




5.11.8 Answers to STEP-style extension questions

We have \[
f'(x)=nx^{n-1}e^{-x}-x^ne^{-x}
=
x^{n-1}e^{-x}(n-x).
\] Since \(x>0\), the factors \(x^{n-1}\) and \(e^{-x}\) are positive. Therefore \(f'(x)=0\) only when \(x=n\). The derivative changes from positive to negative at \(x=n\), so this is a maximum. The maximum value is \[
f(n)=n^ne^{-n}.
\]
For \(x>0\), \[
f'(x)=1-\dfrac{1}{x^2}.
\] Thus \(f'(x)=0\) when \(x=1\). The derivative is negative for \(0<x<1\) and positive for \(x>1\), so \(x=1\) gives a minimum. Therefore, \[
f(x)\geq f(1)=2.
\] Hence \[
x+\dfrac{1}{x}\geq2.
\]
For \[
x^2+xy+y^2=3,
\] implicit differentiation gives \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{2x+y}{x+2y}.
\] Horizontal tangents occur when \(2x+y=0\), so \(y=-2x\). Substitution gives \(x=\pm1\), producing \((1,-2)\) and \((-1,2)\). Vertical tangents occur when \(x+2y=0\), so \(x=-2y\). Substitution gives \(y=\pm1\), producing \((-2,1)\) and \((2,-1)\).
For \[
x=t^2+1,
\qquad
y=t^3-3t,
\] we have \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{3t^2-3}{2t}.
\] Setting this equal to \(3\) gives \[
\dfrac{3t^2-3}{2t}=3.
\] Hence \[
t^2-2t-1=0.
\] Therefore, \[
t=1+\sqrt2
\quad \text{or} \quad
t=1-\sqrt2.
\] The corresponding points are \[
(4+2\sqrt2,\ 4+2\sqrt2)
\] and \[
(4-2\sqrt2,\ 4-2\sqrt2).
\]
Let \[
f(x)=e^x(a\sin x+b\cos x).
\] Then \[
f'(x)
=
e^x(a\sin x+b\cos x)
+
e^x(a\cos x-b\sin x).
\] So \[
f'(x)
=
e^x\left((a-b)\sin x+(a+b)\cos x\right).
\] We require \[
(a-b)\sin x+(a+b)\cos x=\sin x.
\] Therefore, \[
a-b=1
\] and \[
a+b=0.
\] Solving gives \[
a=\dfrac{1}{2},
\qquad
b=-\dfrac{1}{2}.
\]

title: 6  Integration
text:
6.1 Overview
This chapter develops five connected integration ideas:
The central aim is to treat integration as reverse differentiation, while also developing numerical and graphical ways to estimate areas.

title: 6  Integration
6  Integration
text:
6.2 5.1 Integration of exponential functions

6.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
Since
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^x)=e^x,
\]
we have
\[
\int e^x\,dx=e^x+C.
\]
More generally,
\[
\int e^{ax+b}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{a}e^{ax+b}+C,
\quad a\neq0.
\]
This is because differentiating \(\dfrac{1}{a}e^{ax+b}\) gives
\[
\dfrac{1}{a}\cdot ae^{ax+b}=e^{ax+b}.
\]


6.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 1, length.out = 500)
)

df$y <- exp(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Area under y = e^x",
    subtitle = "The exact area from 0 to 1 is e - 1.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.1: The area under y = e^x from x = 0 to x = 1.





The exact area is
\[
\int_0^1 e^x\,dx
=
\left[e^x\right]_0^1
=
e-1.
\]


6.2.3 Worked example
Find
\[
\int e^{3x-2}\,dx.
\]
Since the derivative of \(3x-2\) is \(3\), we divide by \(3\):
\[
\int e^{3x-2}\,dx
=
\dfrac{1}{3}e^{3x-2}+C.
\]
Check by differentiating:
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{1}{3}e^{3x-2}\right)
=
\dfrac{1}{3}\cdot3e^{3x-2}
=
e^{3x-2}.
\]


6.2.4 Short investigation
Change the value of \(a\) in the code below and compare \(e^{ax}\) with its antiderivative.


Code
a <- 2

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-2, 2, length.out = 600)
)

df$y_function <- exp(a * df$x)
df$antiderivative <- (1 / a) * exp(a * df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y_function, linetype = "function"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = antiderivative, linetype = "antiderivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Function and antiderivative for y = e^(", a, "x)"),
    subtitle = "The antiderivative is divided by the coefficient of x.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.2: The function y = e^(ax) and an antiderivative.







6.2.5 Exercises 5.1

6.2.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\int e^x\,dx\).
Find \(\int e^{3x}\,dx\).
Find \(\int 5e^{2x}\,dx\).
Find \(\int e^{-x}\,dx\).
Find \(\int 4e^{1-2x}\,dx\).



6.2.5.2 Silver

Find \(\int \left(e^x+2e^{3x}\right)\,dx\).
Find \(\int 3e^{2x-1}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^1 e^{2x}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\ln2} e^x\,dx\).
Find \(a\) if \(\int e^{ax}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{5}e^{5x}+C\).



6.2.5.3 Gold

Evaluate \(\int_0^1 4e^{4x}\,dx\).
Find \(\int e^{3x+2}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\ln3} 2e^{2x}\,dx\).
Find \(k\) if \(\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(ke^{-2x}\right)=e^{-2x}\).
The area under \(y=3e^x\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=a\) is \(6\). Find \(a\).

title: 6  Integration
text:
6.3 5.2 Integration of \(\dfrac{1}{ax+b}\)

6.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
Since
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\ln|x|\right)=\dfrac{1}{x},
\]
we have
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx=\ln|x|+C.
\]
More generally,
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{ax+b}\,dx
=
\dfrac{1}{a}\ln|ax+b|+C,
\quad a\neq0.
\]
This follows because differentiating \(\ln|ax+b|\) gives
\[
\dfrac{a}{ax+b}.
\]
Therefore, if the numerator is exactly the derivative of the denominator, the integral becomes a logarithm.


6.3.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 2, length.out = 500)
)

df$y <- 1 / (df$x + 1)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Area under y = 1/(x + 1)",
    subtitle = "The exact area from 0 to 2 is ln 3.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.3: The area under y = 1/(x + 1) from x = 0 to x = 2.





The exact area is
\[
\int_0^2 \dfrac{1}{x+1}\,dx
=
\left[\ln|x+1|\right]_0^2
=
\ln3.
\]


6.3.3 Worked example
Find
\[
\int \dfrac{5}{2x-3}\,dx.
\]
Since
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{2x-3}\,dx
=
\dfrac{1}{2}\ln|2x-3|+C,
\]
we have
\[
\int \dfrac{5}{2x-3}\,dx
=
\dfrac{5}{2}\ln|2x-3|+C.
\]


6.3.4 Short investigation
Change the values of \(a\) and \(b\) below.


Code
a <- 2
b <- 1

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0.1, 5, length.out = 600)
)

df$y_function <- 1 / (a * df$x + b)
df$antiderivative <- (1 / a) * log(abs(a * df$x + b))

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y_function, linetype = "function"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = antiderivative, linetype = "antiderivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Function and antiderivative for y = 1/(", a, "x + ", b, ")"),
    subtitle = "The antiderivative is logarithmic.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.4: The function y = 1/(ax + b) and an antiderivative.







6.3.5 Exercises 5.2

6.3.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x+2}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{2x+1}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{3}{3x-4}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{5}{x-1}\,dx\).



6.3.5.2 Silver

Find \(\int \dfrac{4}{2x+3}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{7}{5x-2}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^1 \dfrac{1}{x+1}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_1^3 \dfrac{2}{2x+1}\,dx\).
Find \(a\) and \(b\) if \[
\int \dfrac{1}{ax+b}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{4}\ln|4x-3|+C.
\]



6.3.5.3 Gold

Evaluate \(\int_0^2 \dfrac{3}{3x+1}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^2 \dfrac{5}{5x+2}\,dx\).
Solve \[
\int_0^a \dfrac{1}{x+1}\,dx=\ln5.
\]
Find \[
\int \left(\dfrac{1}{x+1}+\dfrac{1}{x+2}\right)\,dx.
\]
Find \(k\) if \[
\int_0^1 \dfrac{k}{2x+1}\,dx=\ln3.
\]

title: 6  Integration
text:
6.4 5.3 Integration of \(\sin(ax+b)\), \(\cos(ax+b)\) and \(\sec^2(ax+b)\)

6.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
Angles are measured in radians.
The standard trigonometric integrals are
\[
\int \sin x\,dx=-\cos x+C,
\]
\[
\int \cos x\,dx=\sin x+C,
\]
and
\[
\int \sec^2x\,dx=\tan x+C.
\]
Using the chain rule in reverse,
\[
\int \sin(ax+b)\,dx
=
-\dfrac{1}{a}\cos(ax+b)+C,
\]
\[
\int \cos(ax+b)\,dx
=
\dfrac{1}{a}\sin(ax+b)+C,
\]
and
\[
\int \sec^2(ax+b)\,dx
=
\dfrac{1}{a}\tan(ax+b)+C.
\]


6.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-pi, pi, length.out = 800)
)

df$y_function <- cos(2 * df$x)
df$antiderivative <- 0.5 * sin(2 * df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y_function, linetype = "function"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = antiderivative, linetype = "antiderivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Function and antiderivative for y = cos(2x)",
    subtitle = "The antiderivative is 0.5 sin(2x).",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.5: The function y = cos(2x) and an antiderivative.







6.4.3 Worked example
Find
\[
\int 6\sin(3x-1)\,dx.
\]
Since
\[
\int \sin(3x-1)\,dx
=
-\dfrac{1}{3}\cos(3x-1)+C,
\]
we have
\[
\int 6\sin(3x-1)\,dx
=
-2\cos(3x-1)+C.
\]
Check by differentiating:
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(-2\cos(3x-1)\right)
=
6\sin(3x-1).
\]


6.4.4 Short investigation
Compare \(\sec^2x\) and its antiderivative \(\tan x\).


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-1.2, 1.2, length.out = 600)
)

df$y_function <- 1 / cos(df$x)^2
df$antiderivative <- tan(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y_function, linetype = "sec^2(x)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = antiderivative, linetype = "tan(x)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Function and antiderivative",
    subtitle = "The derivative of tan(x) is sec^2(x).",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.6: The function y = sec^2(x) and its antiderivative y = tan(x).







6.4.5 Exercises 5.3

6.4.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\int \cos x\,dx\).
Find \(\int \sin x\,dx\).
Find \(\int \sec^2x\,dx\).
Find \(\int \cos2x\,dx\).
Find \(\int \sin3x\,dx\).



6.4.5.2 Silver

Find \(\int 4\cos(2x+1)\,dx\).
Find \(\int 6\sin(3x-2)\,dx\).
Find \(\int 5\sec^2(5x)\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\cos x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\sec^2x\,dx\).



6.4.5.3 Gold

Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}3\cos3x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}2\sin x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{8}}4\sec^2(2x)\,dx\).
Find \(a\) if \[
\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}a\cos2x\,dx=3.
\]
Solve \[
\int_0^x \cos2t\,dt=\dfrac{1}{2}
\] for \(0\leq x\leq\dfrac{\pi}{2}\).

title: 6  Integration
text:
6.5 5.4 Further integration of trigonometric functions

6.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
Some trigonometric integrals require identities before they can be integrated.
The most useful identities in this section are
\[
\sin^2x=\dfrac{1-\cos2x}{2},
\]
\[
\cos^2x=\dfrac{1+\cos2x}{2},
\]
and
\[
2\sin x\cos x=\sin2x.
\]
These identities turn powers or products of trigonometric functions into expressions that can be integrated directly.


6.5.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 800)
)

df$left_side <- sin(df$x)^2
df$right_side <- (1 - cos(2 * df$x)) / 2

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = left_side, linetype = "sin^2(x)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = right_side, linetype = "(1 - cos(2x))/2"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Identity for sin^2(x)",
    subtitle = "The two graphs overlap.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.7: Checking sin^2(x) = (1 - cos(2x))/2.







6.5.3 Worked example
Find
\[
\int \sin^2x\,dx.
\]
Using the identity
\[
\sin^2x=\dfrac{1-\cos2x}{2},
\]
we get
\[
\int \sin^2x\,dx
=
\int \dfrac{1-\cos2x}{2}\,dx.
\]
So
\[
\int \sin^2x\,dx
=
\dfrac{x}{2}-\dfrac{1}{4}\sin2x+C.
\]


6.5.4 Short investigation
Compare exact area under \(\sin^2x\) with numerical area.


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, pi, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- sin(df$x)^2

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Area under y = sin^2(x)",
    subtitle = "The exact area from 0 to pi is pi/2.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.8: The area under y = sin^2(x) from 0 to pi.







6.5.5 Exercises 5.4

6.5.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\int \sin^2x\,dx\).
Find \(\int \cos^2x\,dx\).
Find \(\int \sin x\cos x\,dx\).
Find \(\int (1+\cos2x)\,dx\).
Find \(\int (1-\cos2x)\,dx\).



6.5.5.2 Silver

Evaluate \(\int_0^\pi \sin^2x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^\pi \cos^2x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\sin x\cos x\,dx\).
Find \(\int 4\cos^2x\,dx\).
Find \(\int 6\sin^2(2x)\,dx\).



6.5.5.3 Gold

Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\sin^2x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}\cos^2x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}(\sin x+\cos x)^2\,dx\).
Find \(k\) if \[
\int_0^\pi k\sin^2x\,dx=5\pi.
\]
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\sin^2x\cos^2x\,dx\).

title: 6  Integration
text:
6.6 5.5 The trapezium rule

6.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
The trapezium rule estimates the area under a curve by splitting the interval into equal strips and replacing each curved strip with a trapezium.
Suppose we want to estimate
\[
\int_a^b f(x)\,dx
\]
using \(n\) equal strips.
The strip width is
\[
h=\dfrac{b-a}{n}.
\]
The \(x\)-values are
\[
x_0=a,\quad x_1=a+h,\quad x_2=a+2h,\quad \ldots,\quad x_n=b.
\]
Let
\[
y_i=f(x_i).
\]
Then the trapezium rule gives
\[
\int_a^b f(x)\,dx
\approx
\dfrac{h}{2}
\left[
y_0+2y_1+2y_2+\cdots+2y_{n-1}+y_n
\right].
\]


6.6.2 Visualisation using R


Code
f <- function(x) x^2

curve_df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 2, length.out = 800)
)

curve_df$y <- f(curve_df$x)

x_points <- seq(0, 2, length.out = 5)
y_points <- f(x_points)

trapezia <- do.call(
  rbind,
  lapply(1:(length(x_points) - 1), function(i) {
    data.frame(
      strip = i,
      x = c(x_points[i], x_points[i], x_points[i + 1], x_points[i + 1]),
      y = c(0, y_points[i], y_points[i + 1], 0)
    )
  })
)

ggplot() +
  geom_polygon(
    data = trapezia,
    aes(x = x, y = y, group = strip),
    alpha = 0.3
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = curve_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y),
    linewidth = 1.1
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = data.frame(x = x_points, y = y_points),
    aes(x = x, y = y),
    size = 2
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Trapezium rule",
    subtitle = "The curve y = x^2 is approximated by straight-line segments.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.9: Trapezium rule estimate for y = x^2 from x = 0 to x = 2.







6.6.3 Worked example
Use the trapezium rule with \(4\) strips to estimate
\[
\int_0^2 x^2\,dx.
\]
Here,
\[
a=0,\quad b=2,\quad n=4.
\]
Therefore,
\[
h=\dfrac{2-0}{4}=\dfrac{1}{2}.
\]
The \(x\)-values are
\[
0,\quad \dfrac{1}{2},\quad 1,\quad \dfrac{3}{2},\quad 2.
\]
The corresponding \(y\)-values for \(y=x^2\) are
\[
0,\quad \dfrac{1}{4},\quad 1,\quad \dfrac{9}{4},\quad 4.
\]
Using the trapezium rule,
\[
T
=
\dfrac{h}{2}
\left[
y_0+2y_1+2y_2+2y_3+y_4
\right].
\]
So
\[
T
=
\dfrac{1}{4}
\left[
0+2\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)+2(1)+2\left(\dfrac{9}{4}\right)+4
\right].
\]
Therefore,
\[
T=\dfrac{11}{4}.
\]


6.6.4 Short investigation
Change the number of strips and observe the estimate.


Code
trapezium_estimate <- function(f, a, b, n) {
  x <- seq(a, b, length.out = n + 1)
  y <- f(x)
  h <- (b - a) / n
  (h / 2) * (y[1] + 2 * sum(y[2:n]) + y[n + 1])
}

n_values <- 1:20

df <- data.frame(
  n = n_values,
  estimate = sapply(
    n_values,
    function(n) trapezium_estimate(function(x) x^2, 0, 2, n)
  )
)

df$exact <- 8 / 3

ggplot(df, aes(x = n)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = estimate, linetype = "trapezium estimate"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = exact, linetype = "exact value"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Trapezium rule estimates",
    subtitle = "As the number of strips increases, the estimate approaches the exact value.",
    x = "number of strips",
    y = "area estimate",
    linetype = "Quantity"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 6.10: Trapezium rule estimates for y = x^2 on [0, 2].







6.6.5 Exercises 5.5

6.6.5.1 Bronze

Use the trapezium rule with \(2\) strips to estimate \(\int_0^2 x^2\,dx\).
Use the trapezium rule with \(4\) strips to estimate \(\int_0^4 (x+1)\,dx\).
Use the trapezium rule with \(2\) strips to estimate \(\int_1^3 \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx\).
Use the trapezium rule with \(1\) strip to estimate \(\int_0^1 e^x\,dx\).
Use the trapezium rule with \(2\) strips to estimate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\cos x\,dx\).



6.6.5.2 Silver

Use the trapezium rule with \(4\) strips to estimate \(\int_0^2 x^2\,dx\).
Use the trapezium rule with \(2\) strips to estimate \(\int_0^2 \dfrac{1}{x+1}\,dx\).
Use the trapezium rule with \(2\) strips to estimate \(\int_0^1 e^x\,dx\).
Use the trapezium rule with \(4\) strips to estimate \(\int_0^\pi \sin x\,dx\).
Write the trapezium rule estimate using \(3\) strips for \(\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\).



6.6.5.3 Gold

Use the trapezium rule with \(4\) strips to estimate \(\int_0^2 x^2\,dx\), and compare it with the exact value.
For \(f(x)=x^2+1\), use \(2\) strips on \([0,a]\). Find the positive integer \(a\) such that the trapezium rule estimate is \(5\).
Prove that the trapezium rule is exact for any linear function \(f(x)=mx+c\).
Use the trapezium rule with \(4\) strips to estimate \(\int_1^2 \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx\).
Use the trapezium rule with \(3\) strips to estimate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}\sin x\,dx\).

title: 6  Integration
text:
6.7 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require proof, structure or exact reasoning.

6.7.1 STEP-style Question 1
Prove that
\[
\int_0^\pi \sin^2x\,dx=\dfrac{\pi}{2}.
\]


6.7.2 STEP-style Question 2
Let \(k>0\) and \(a>0\). Suppose
\[
e^{ka}=4
\]
and
\[
\int_0^a e^{kx}\,dx=3.
\]
Find \(k\) and \(a\).


6.7.3 STEP-style Question 3
Use calculus to prove that
\[
\ln(1+a)<a
\]
for all \(a>0\).
Then interpret the inequality in terms of the area under
\[
y=\dfrac{1}{1+x}.
\]


6.7.4 STEP-style Question 4
Find constants \(A\) and \(B\) such that
\[
F(x)=Ae^{2x}+B\sin3x
\]
is an antiderivative of
\[
6e^{2x}+12\cos3x.
\]


6.7.5 STEP-style Question 5
For \(f(x)=x^2\) on the interval \([0,a]\), use \(n\) equal strips.
Prove that the trapezium rule estimate is
\[
\dfrac{a^3}{3}+\dfrac{a^3}{6n^2}.
\]
Hence explain why the trapezium rule overestimates
\[
\int_0^a x^2\,dx.
\]

title: 6  Integration
text:
6.8 Reflection questions

Why is integration called reverse differentiation?
Why does \(\int e^{ax+b}\,dx\) require division by \(a\)?
Why does \(\int \dfrac{1}{ax+b}\,dx\) produce a logarithm?
Why must trigonometric integrals use radians?
Why are identities useful before integrating powers of sine and cosine?
What does the trapezium rule estimate geometrically?
Why does increasing the number of strips usually improve the trapezium rule estimate?
What can R show clearly about area that algebra alone may hide?
What can exact integration prove that numerical approximation can only suggest?

title: 6  Integration
text:
6.9 Answers

6.9.1 Answers to Exercises 5.1

6.9.1.1 Bronze

\(e^x+C\).
\(\dfrac{1}{3}e^{3x}+C\).
\(\dfrac{5}{2}e^{2x}+C\).
\(-e^{-x}+C\).
\(-2e^{1-2x}+C\).



6.9.1.2 Silver

\(e^x+\dfrac{2}{3}e^{3x}+C\).
\(\dfrac{3}{2}e^{2x-1}+C\).
\(\dfrac{e^2-1}{2}\).
\(1\).
\(a=5\).



6.9.1.3 Gold

\(e^4-1\).
\(\dfrac{1}{3}e^{3x+2}+C\).
\(8\).
\(k=-\dfrac{1}{2}\).
\(a=\ln3\).




6.9.2 Answers to Exercises 5.2

6.9.2.1 Bronze

\(\ln|x|+C\).
\(\ln|x+2|+C\).
\(\dfrac{1}{2}\ln|2x+1|+C\).
\(\ln|3x-4|+C\).
\(5\ln|x-1|+C\).



6.9.2.2 Silver

\(2\ln|2x+3|+C\).
\(\dfrac{7}{5}\ln|5x-2|+C\).
\(\ln2\).
\(\ln\left(\dfrac{7}{3}\right)\).
\(a=4\) and \(b=-3\).



6.9.2.3 Gold

\(\ln7\).
\(\ln6\).
\(a=4\).
\(\ln|x+1|+\ln|x+2|+C\).
\(k=2\).




6.9.3 Answers to Exercises 5.3

6.9.3.1 Bronze

\(\sin x+C\).
\(-\cos x+C\).
\(\tan x+C\).
\(\dfrac{1}{2}\sin2x+C\).
\(-\dfrac{1}{3}\cos3x+C\).



6.9.3.2 Silver

\(2\sin(2x+1)+C\).
\(-2\cos(3x-2)+C\).
\(\tan5x+C\).
\(1\).
\(1\).



6.9.3.3 Gold

\(1\).
\(2\).
\(2\).
\(a=4\sqrt3\).

6.9.4 Answers to Exercises 5.4

6.9.4.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{x}{2}-\dfrac{1}{4}\sin2x+C\).
\(\dfrac{x}{2}+\dfrac{1}{4}\sin2x+C\).
\(\dfrac{1}{2}\sin^2x+C\).
\(x+\dfrac{1}{2}\sin2x+C\).

6.9.4.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\).
\(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\).
\(\dfrac{1}{2}\).
\(2x+\sin2x+C\).
\(3x-\dfrac{3}{4}\sin4x+C\).



6.9.4.3 Gold

\(\dfrac{\pi}{8}+\dfrac{1}{4}\).
\(\dfrac{\pi}{2}+1\).
\(k=10\).
