6.9.5 Answers to Exercises 5.5

6.9.5.1 Bronze

\(3\).
\(12\).
\(\dfrac{7}{6}\).
\(\dfrac{1+e}{2}\).
\(\dfrac{\pi}{8}(1+\sqrt2)\).



6.9.5.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{11}{4}\).
\(\dfrac{7}{6}\).
\(\dfrac{1+2\sqrt e+e}{4}\).
\(\dfrac{\pi}{4}(1+\sqrt2)\).
If \(h=\dfrac{b-a}{3}\), then \[
T=\dfrac{h}{2}\left[y_0+2y_1+2y_2+y_3\right].
\]



6.9.5.3 Gold

The trapezium estimate is \(\dfrac{11}{4}\). The exact value is \(\dfrac{8}{3}\). The estimate is too large by \(\dfrac{1}{12}\).
\(a=2\).
On each strip, a linear function is already a straight line, so the trapezium exactly matches the area under the graph. Hence the trapezium rule is exact for linear functions.
\(\dfrac{1171}{1680}\).
\(\dfrac{\pi}{12}(2+\sqrt3)\).




6.9.6 Answers to STEP-style extension questions

Since \[
\sin^2x=\dfrac{1-\cos2x}{2},
\] we have \[
\int_0^\pi \sin^2x\,dx
=
\int_0^\pi \dfrac{1-\cos2x}{2}\,dx.
\] Therefore, \[
\int_0^\pi \sin^2x\,dx
=
\left[\dfrac{x}{2}-\dfrac{1}{4}\sin2x\right]_0^\pi
=
\dfrac{\pi}{2}.
\]
Since \(e^{ka}=4\), \[
\int_0^a e^{kx}\,dx
=
\left[\dfrac{1}{k}e^{kx}\right]_0^a
=
\dfrac{4-1}{k}.
\] This is equal to \(3\), so \(k=1\). Hence \(e^a=4\), giving \(a=\ln4\).
Define \[
g(a)=a-\ln(1+a).
\] Then \[
g'(a)=1-\dfrac{1}{1+a}=\dfrac{a}{1+a}.
\] For \(a>0\), \(g'(a)>0\), and \(g(0)=0\). Therefore \(g(a)>0\) for \(a>0\), so \[
\ln(1+a)<a.
\] Since \[
\ln(1+a)=\int_0^a \dfrac{1}{1+x}\,dx,
\] this says that the area under \(y=\dfrac{1}{1+x}\) from \(0\) to \(a\) is less than the area of the rectangle of width \(a\) and height \(1\).
We require \[
F'(x)=6e^{2x}+12\cos3x.
\] Since \[
F'(x)=2Ae^{2x}+3B\cos3x,
\] we get \[
2A=6
\] and \[
3B=12.
\] Therefore, \[
A=3
\quad \text{and} \quad
B=4.
\]
The strip width is \[
h=\dfrac{a}{n}.
\] The ordinates are \[
y_i=\left(\dfrac{ia}{n}\right)^2.
\] The trapezium estimate is \[
T=
\dfrac{h}{2}
\left[
y_0+2y_1+\cdots+2y_{n-1}+y_n
\right].
\] Since \(y_0=0\) and \(y_n=a^2\), \[
T
=
\dfrac{a}{2n}
\left[
2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\left(\dfrac{ia}{n}\right)^2+a^2
\right].
\] Hence \[
T=
\dfrac{a^3}{2n}
\left[
\dfrac{2}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}i^2+1
\right].
\] Using \[
\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}i^2=\dfrac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6},
\] we obtain \[
T=\dfrac{a^3}{3}+\dfrac{a^3}{6n^2}.
\] But \[
\int_0^a x^2\,dx=\dfrac{a^3}{3}.
\] Therefore the trapezium rule overestimates the exact value by \[
\dfrac{a^3}{6n^2}.
\]

title: 7  Numerical solutions of equations
text:
7.1 Overview
This chapter develops three connected numerical methods ideas:
The central aim is to understand how equations can be solved approximately when exact algebraic methods are difficult or unavailable.

title: 7  Numerical solutions of equations
text:
finding a starting point for a root;
improving a solution using iteration;
using iterative processes to solve problems involving other areas of mathematics.








Note



The exercises are organised into tiers.
Bronze develops fluency.
Silver develops simple application.
Gold develops exam-style reasoning.
The STEP-style section extends the chapter beyond routine A Level fluency.

title: 7  Numerical solutions of equations
text:
7.2 6.1 Finding a starting point

7.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
A root of the equation
\[
f(x)=0
\]
is a value of \(x\) where the graph of \(y=f(x)\) crosses or touches the \(x\)-axis.
A common way to locate a starting interval is the change of sign test.
If \(f\) is continuous on \([a,b]\) and
\[
f(a)f(b)<0,
\]
then there is at least one root of
\[
f(x)=0
\]
between \(a\) and \(b\).
This works because a continuous graph cannot move from one side of the \(x\)-axis to the other without crossing it.
However, the test must be used carefully:

a change of sign guarantees at least one root only when the function is continuous;
no change of sign does not always mean there is no root;
a root may touch the axis without crossing it.



7.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
f <- function(x) x^3 - x - 4

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 3, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- f(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  annotate(
    "rect",
    xmin = 1,
    xmax = 2,
    ymin = -Inf,
    ymax = Inf,
    alpha = 0.15
  ) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  annotate("point", x = 1, y = f(1), size = 3) +
  annotate("point", x = 2, y = f(2), size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = 1, y = f(1) - 1, label = "f(1) < 0") +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = f(2) + 1, label = "f(2) > 0") +
  labs(
    title = "Change of sign",
    subtitle = "The shaded interval contains a root because the function changes sign.",
    x = "x",
    y = "f(x)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 7.1: The root of f(x) = x^3 - x - 4 lies between 1 and 2.







7.2.3 Worked example
Show that the equation
\[
x^3-x-4=0
\]
has a root between \(1\) and \(2\).
Let
\[
f(x)=x^3-x-4.
\]
Then
\[
f(1)=1^3-1-4=-4.
\]
Also,
\[
f(2)=2^3-2-4=2.
\]
Since
\[
f(1)<0
\]
and
\[
f(2)>0,
\]
there is a change of sign between \(1\) and \(2\).
Therefore, since \(f\) is continuous, the equation
\[
x^3-x-4=0
\]
has at least one root between \(1\) and \(2\).


7.2.4 Short investigation
Use R to test smaller intervals for the root of
\[
x^3-x-4=0.
\]

Code
f <- function(x) x^3 - x - 4

test_points <- seq(1.6, 1.9, by = 0.05)

data.frame(
  x = test_points,
  f_x = round(f(test_points), 6)
)




Table 7.1



     x       f_x
1 1.60 -1.504000
2 1.65 -1.157875
3 1.70 -0.787000
4 1.75 -0.390625
5 1.80  0.032000
6 1.85  0.481625
7 1.90  0.959000




This table helps us identify a smaller interval containing the root.


7.2.5 Exercises 6.1

7.2.5.1 Bronze

Show that \(x^2-3=0\) has a root between \(1\) and \(2\).
Show that \(x^3-2x-5=0\) has a root between \(2\) and \(3\).
Show that \(e^x-3=0\) has a root between \(1\) and \(2\).
Show that \(\ln x+x-2=0\) has a root between \(1\) and \(2\).
Show that \(\cos x-x=0\) has a root between \(0\) and \(1\).



7.2.5.2 Silver

Show that \(x^3-x-4=0\) has a root between \(1.7\) and \(1.8\).
Show that \(x^3-2x-2=0\) has a root between \(1.7\) and \(1.8\).
Show that \(e^{-x}-x=0\) has a root between \(0.5\) and \(0.6\).
Show that \(\ln x+x-3=0\) has a root between \(2\) and \(3\).
Show that \(x^3-6x+2=0\) has roots in each of the intervals \((-3,-2)\), \((0,1)\) and \((2,3)\).



7.2.5.3 Gold

Show that \(\ln x+x-2=0\) has exactly one root for \(x>0\).
Show that \(x^3-3x+1=0\) has three roots, one in each of the intervals \((-2,-1)\), \((0,1)\) and \((1,2)\).
Explain why a change of sign does not guarantee a root if the function is not continuous.
Explain why no change of sign does not prove that there is no root.
Show that the root of \(x^3-x-4=0\) is between \(1.7955\) and \(1.7965\).

title: 7  Numerical solutions of equations
text:
7.3 6.2 Improving your solution

7.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
Once we have a starting point or a starting interval, we can improve the approximation using an iterative formula.
An iterative formula has the form
\[
x_{n+1}=g(x_n).
\]
This means:

start with an initial value \(x_1\);
substitute it into the formula to get \(x_2\);
substitute \(x_2\) into the formula to get \(x_3\);
continue until the values appear stable to the required accuracy.

If the sequence converges to a value \(\alpha\), then
\[
\alpha=g(\alpha).
\]
So \(\alpha\) is a fixed point of \(g\).
An iteration may:

converge quickly;
converge slowly;
oscillate while converging;
enter a periodic loop;
diverge.

A starting point and a rearrangement must therefore be chosen carefully.


7.3.2 Visualisation using R: convergence


Code
g <- function(x) (x + 4)^(1 / 3)

curve_df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(1.2, 2.2, length.out = 800)
)

curve_df$g_x <- g(curve_df$x)

line_df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(1.2, 2.2, length.out = 800)
)

line_df$y <- line_df$x

segments_df <- make_cobweb(g, 1.5, 6)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = x, y = g_x), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(data = line_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_segment(
    data = segments_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Convergent fixed-point iteration",
    subtitle = "The cobweb moves towards the fixed point.",
    x = "x_n",
    y = "x_{n+1}"
  ) +
  coord_equal() +
  book_theme()








Figure 7.2: Cobweb diagram for x_{n+1} = (x_n + 4)^(1/3), starting from x_1 = 1.5.







7.3.3 Worked example
The equation
\[
x^3-x-4=0
\]
can be rearranged as
\[
x=\sqrt[3]{x+4}.
\]
This gives the iterative formula
\[
x_{n+1}=\sqrt[3]{x_n+4}.
\]
Starting with
\[
x_1=1.5,
\]
we obtain
\[
x_2=\sqrt[3]{1.5+4}=1.765174\ldots
\]
Then
\[
x_3=\sqrt[3]{1.765174\ldots+4}=1.793098\ldots
\]
and
\[
x_4=1.795988\ldots
\]
Continuing,
\[
x_5=1.796287\ldots
\]
and
\[
x_6=1.796318\ldots
\]
The values are settling down to
\[
x\approx1.796.
\]
Therefore, the root of
\[
x^3-x-4=0
\]
is approximately
\[
1.796
\]
to three decimal places.


7.3.4 Why starting point and rearrangement matter
The same equation
\[
x^3-x-4=0
\]
can also be rearranged as
\[
x=x^3-4.
\]
This gives
\[
x_{n+1}=x_n^3-4.
\]
Starting from \(x_1=1.5\) gives
\[
x_2=-0.625,
\]
\[
x_3=-4.244140625,
\]
and then the values move rapidly away from the root.
So the rearrangement
\[
x_{n+1}=x_n^3-4
\]
is a poor iteration for this root.


7.3.5 Visualisation using R: divergence


Code
g_diverge <- function(x) x^3 - 4

iteration_df <- fixed_point_steps(g_diverge, 1.5, 4)
iteration_df$x_clipped <- pmax(pmin(iteration_df$x_value, 10), -10)

ggplot(iteration_df, aes(x = iteration, y = x_clipped)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "A divergent iteration",
    subtitle = "Values have been clipped to the interval [-10, 10] for readability.",
    x = "iteration",
    y = "x_n"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 7.3: Divergence for x_{n+1} = x_n^3 - 4, starting from x_1 = 1.5.







7.3.6 Visualisation using R: periodic loop
The formula
\[
x_{n+1}=1-x_n
\]
has fixed point
\[
x=\dfrac{1}{2}.
\]
But if we start from
\[
x_1=0.2,
\]
then
\[
x_2=0.8,
\]
\[
x_3=0.2,
\]
and the sequence loops forever.


Code
g_period <- function(x) 1 - x

curve_df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 1, length.out = 800)
)

curve_df$g_x <- g_period(curve_df$x)

line_df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 1, length.out = 800)
)

line_df$y <- line_df$x

segments_df <- make_cobweb(g_period, 0.2, 6)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = x, y = g_x), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(data = line_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_segment(
    data = segments_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Periodic loop",
    subtitle = "The sequence alternates between 0.2 and 0.8.",
    x = "x_n",
    y = "x_{n+1}"
  ) +
  coord_equal() +
  book_theme()








Figure 7.4: A period-two loop for x_{n+1} = 1 - x_n.







7.3.7 A useful convergence test
Suppose the iteration is
\[
x_{n+1}=g(x_n)
\]
and \(\alpha\) is a fixed point.
If
\[
|g'(\alpha)|<1,
\]
then the iteration is usually locally convergent near \(\alpha\).
If
\[
|g'(\alpha)|>1,
\]
then the iteration is usually locally divergent near \(\alpha\).
For
\[
g(x)=\sqrt[3]{x+4},
\]
we have
\[
g'(x)=\dfrac{1}{3(x+4)^{2/3}}.
\]
Near the root \(\alpha\approx1.796\),
\[
|g'(\alpha)|\approx0.103<1,
\]
so the iteration converges.
For
\[
g(x)=x^3-4,
\]
we have
\[
g'(x)=3x^2.
\]
Near \(\alpha\approx1.796\),
\[
|g'(\alpha)|\approx9.68>1,
\]
so the iteration diverges.


7.3.8 Exercises 6.2

7.3.8.1 Bronze

Use \(x_{n+1}=\sqrt{2+x_n}\) with \(x_1=1\) to find \(x_2\), \(x_3\) and \(x_4\).
Use \(x_{n+1}=\sqrt[3]{x_n+4}\) with \(x_1=1.5\) to find \(x_2\), \(x_3\) and \(x_4\).
Use \(x_{n+1}=\cos x_n\) with \(x_1=0.5\) to find \(x_2\), \(x_3\) and \(x_4\).
Use \(x_{n+1}=e^{-x_n}\) with \(x_1=0.5\) to find \(x_2\), \(x_3\) and \(x_4\).
Use \(x_{n+1}=1-x_n\) with \(x_1=0.2\) to find \(x_2\), \(x_3\), \(x_4\) and \(x_5\).



7.3.8.2 Silver

Use \(x_{n+1}=\sqrt{2+x_n}\) with \(x_1=1\) to estimate the fixed point to three decimal places.
Use \(x_{n+1}=\sqrt[3]{x_n+4}\) with \(x_1=1.5\) to estimate the root of \(x^3-x-4=0\) to three decimal places.
Use \(x_{n+1}=e^{-x_n}\) with \(x_1=0.5\) to estimate the solution of \(e^{-x}=x\) to three decimal places.
Show that \(x_{n+1}=x_n^3-4\) diverges when \(x_1=1.5\).
Show that \(x_{n+1}=\dfrac{2}{x_n}\) enters a periodic loop when \(x_1=1\).



7.3.8.3 Gold

For the equation \(x^3-x-4=0\), compare the two iterations \[
x_{n+1}=\sqrt[3]{x_n+4}
\] and \[
x_{n+1}=x_n^3-4.
\] Explain why one is suitable and the other is not.
Prove that the iteration \(x_{n+1}=1-x_n\) enters a period-two loop unless \(x_1=\dfrac{1}{2}\).
Explain why \(x_{n+1}=e^{-x_n}\) converges with oscillation near its fixed point.
Suppose an iteration \(x_{n+1}=g(x_n)\) converges to a value \(L\). Prove that \(L=g(L)\), assuming \(g\) is continuous.
Use the iteration \[
x_{n+1}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(x_n+\dfrac{3}{x_n}\right)
\] with \(x_1=2\) to estimate \(\sqrt3\) to five decimal places.

title: 7  Numerical solutions of equations
section: 7.4 6.3 Using iterative processes to solve problems involving other areas of mathematics
text:
7.4 6.3 Using iterative processes to solve problems involving other areas of mathematics

7.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
Iteration is not restricted to polynomial equations. It can be used in many areas of mathematics, including:

exponential equations;
logarithmic equations;
trigonometric equations;
geometry problems leading to transcendental equations;
equations where exact algebraic methods are unavailable.

For example, the equation
\[
\cos x=x
\]
cannot be solved exactly using elementary algebra.
But it can be written as
\[
x=\cos x,
\]
which gives the iteration
\[
x_{n+1}=\cos x_n.
\]
The solution is the fixed point where the graphs of
\[
y=\cos x
\]
and
\[
y=x
\]
intersect.


7.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
g_cos <- function(x) cos(x)

curve_df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 1.2, length.out = 800)
)

curve_df$g_x <- g_cos(curve_df$x)

line_df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 1.2, length.out = 800)
)

line_df$y <- line_df$x

segments_df <- make_cobweb(g_cos, 0.5, 8)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = x, y = g_x), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(data = line_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_segment(
    data = segments_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Fixed point of cos(x)",
    subtitle = "The iteration x_{n+1} = cos(x_n) converges to approximately 0.739.",
    x = "x_n",
    y = "x_{n+1}"
  ) +
  coord_equal() +
  book_theme()








Figure 7.5: Solving cos(x) = x using fixed-point iteration.







7.4.3 Worked example
Use iteration to solve
\[
\cos x=x
\]
to three decimal places.
Use
\[
x_{n+1}=\cos x_n
\]
with
\[
x_1=0.5.
\]
Then
\[
x_2=\cos(0.5)=0.877582\ldots
\]
\[
x_3=\cos(0.877582\ldots)=0.639012\ldots
\]
\[
x_4=0.802685\ldots
\]
The values oscillate but slowly converge.
Continuing gives
\[
x\approx0.739.
\]
Therefore, the solution of
\[
\cos x=x
\]
is
\[
x=0.739
\]
to three decimal places.


7.4.4 A trigonometric example
The equation
\[
\theta=2\sin\theta
\]
has the fixed-point iteration
\[
\theta_{n+1}=2\sin\theta_n.
\]
This type of equation can occur when a geometric or circular problem produces both a linear angle term and a trigonometric term.


Code
g_theta <- function(theta) 2 * sin(theta)

curve_df <- data.frame(
  theta = seq(0, 2.4, length.out = 800)
)

curve_df$g_theta <- g_theta(curve_df$theta)

line_df <- data.frame(
  theta = seq(0, 2.4, length.out = 800)
)

line_df$y <- line_df$theta

segments_df <- make_cobweb(g_theta, 1.85, 10)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = theta, y = g_theta), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(data = line_df, aes(x = theta, y = y), linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_segment(
    data = segments_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Iteration for theta = 2 sin(theta)",
    subtitle = "The non-zero solution is approximately 1.895.",
    x = "theta_n",
    y = "theta_{n+1}"
  ) +
  coord_equal() +
  book_theme()








Figure 7.6: Solving theta = 2 sin(theta) using iteration.







7.4.5 Worked example
Use the iteration
\[
\theta_{n+1}=2\sin\theta_n
\]
with
\[
\theta_1=1.85
\]
to solve
\[
\theta=2\sin\theta
\]
to three decimal places.
The first few iterations are
\[
\theta_2=1.922550\ldots,
\]
\[
\theta_3=1.877539\ldots,
\]
\[
\theta_4=1.906644\ldots,
\]
\[
\theta_5=1.888262\ldots.
\]
The sequence oscillates while converging.
Continuing gives
\[
\theta\approx1.895.
\]
To confirm the rounding, define
\[
f(\theta)=\theta-2\sin\theta.
\]
Then
\[
f(1.8945)<0
\]
and
\[
f(1.8955)>0.
\]
Therefore, the non-zero solution is
\[
\theta=1.895
\]
to three decimal places.


7.4.6 Short investigation
Compare four different iterative behaviours.

Code
iterations <- data.frame(
  behaviour = c(
    "converges quickly",
    "oscillates but converges",
    "periodic loop",
    "diverges"
  ),
  iteration = c(
    "x_{n+1} = (x_n + 4)^(1/3)",
    "x_{n+1} = cos(x_n)",
    "x_{n+1} = 1 - x_n",
    "x_{n+1} = x_n^3 - 4"
  ),
  starting_value = c(1.5, 0.5, 0.2, 1.5)
)

iterations




Table 7.2



                 behaviour                 iteration starting_value
1        converges quickly x_{n+1} = (x_n + 4)^(1/3)            1.5
2 oscillates but converges        x_{n+1} = cos(x_n)            0.5
3            periodic loop         x_{n+1} = 1 - x_n            0.2
4                 diverges       x_{n+1} = x_n^3 - 4            1.5






7.4.7 Exercises 6.3

7.4.7.1 Bronze

Use \(x_{n+1}=\cos x_n\) with \(x_1=0.5\) to solve \(\cos x=x\) to three decimal places.
Use \(x_{n+1}=e^{-x_n}\) with \(x_1=0.5\) to solve \(e^{-x}=x\) to three decimal places.
Use \(\theta_{n+1}=2\sin\theta_n\) with \(\theta_1=1.85\) to estimate the non-zero solution of \(\theta=2\sin\theta\).
Use \(x_{n+1}=\ln(x_n+2)\) with \(x_1=1\) to solve \(\ln(x+2)=x\) to three decimal places.
The iteration \(x_{n+1}=\sqrt{x_n+6}\) has a positive fixed point. Find the equation satisfied by this fixed point and hence find its value.



7.4.7.2 Silver

Use a suitable iteration to solve \(x^3-x-4=0\) to three decimal places.
Use \(\theta_{n+1}=2\sin\theta_n\) to solve \(\theta=2\sin\theta\) to three decimal places.
Explain why the iteration \(x_{n+1}=\dfrac{2}{x_n}\) is not suitable for finding \(\sqrt2\) when \(x_1=1\).
Explain why the iteration \(x_{n+1}=2x_n-1\) is not suitable for finding its fixed point unless the starting value is already correct.
Use \(x_{n+1}=\ln(x_n+2)\) with \(x_1=1\) to solve \(\ln(x+2)=x\) to three decimal places.



7.4.7.3 Gold

Prove that \(\cos x=x\) has exactly one solution in the interval \((0,1)\).
Show that the iteration \(x_{n+1}=e^{-x_n}\) converges with oscillation near its fixed point.
Prove that \(x_{n+1}=1-x_n\) enters a period-two loop unless \(x_1=\dfrac{1}{2}\).
Use a change of sign test to confirm that the root of \(x^3-x-4=0\) is \(1.796\) to three decimal places.
Use a change of sign test to confirm that the non-zero solution of \(\theta=2\sin\theta\) is \(1.895\) to three decimal places.

title: 7  Numerical solutions of equations
text:
7.5 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require proof, structure or exact reasoning.

7.5.1 STEP-style Question 1
Let \(f\) be continuous on \([a,b]\).
Suppose
\[
f(a)<0
\]
and
\[
f(b)>0.
\]
Explain why there must be at least one value \(\alpha\in(a,b)\) such that
\[
f(\alpha)=0.
\]
Then give an example showing why continuity is needed.


7.5.2 STEP-style Question 2
Suppose \(g\) maps the interval \([a,b]\) into itself and there is a constant \(k\) such that
\[
0<k<1
\]
and
\[
|g'(x)|\leq k
\]
for every \(x\in[a,b]\).
Let \(\alpha\) be a fixed point of \(g\) in \([a,b]\).
Show that the iteration
\[
x_{n+1}=g(x_n)
\]
satisfies
\[
|x_{n+1}-\alpha|\leq k|x_n-\alpha|.
\]
Hence explain why the iteration converges to \(\alpha\).


7.5.3 STEP-style Question 3
The equation
\[
x^2=2
\]
can be written as
\[
x=\dfrac{2}{x}.
\]
This gives the iteration
\[
x_{n+1}=\dfrac{2}{x_n}.
\]
Show that if \(x_1\neq\sqrt2\) and \(x_1\neq-\sqrt2\), then the sequence does not converge but enters a period-two loop.


7.5.4 STEP-style Question 4
The equation
\[
x^3-x-4=0
\]
can be rearranged in two ways:
\[
x=\sqrt[3]{x+4}
\]
and
\[
x=x^3-4.
\]
Let \(\alpha\) be the root between \(1\) and \(2\).
Use derivatives of the two iteration functions to explain why
\[
x_{n+1}=\sqrt[3]{x_n+4}
\]
converges near \(\alpha\), while
\[
x_{n+1}=x_n^3-4
\]
diverges near \(\alpha\).


7.5.5 STEP-style Question 5
Let
\[
f(x)=\cos x-x.
\]
Use calculus and a change of sign argument to prove that the equation
\[
\cos x=x
\]
has exactly one solution in \([0,1]\).
Then explain why the iteration
\[
x_{n+1}=\cos x_n
\]
converges with oscillation when started near the solution.

title: 7  Numerical solutions of equations
text:
7.6 Reflection questions

Why is a starting point needed before using an iterative method?
What does a change of sign tell us about a continuous function?
Why does no change of sign not always rule out a root?
What is a fixed point of an iteration?
Why can two rearrangements of the same equation behave differently?
What does a cobweb diagram show about convergence?
How can a starting point cause a periodic loop?
Why might an iteration diverge even though it has the correct fixed point?
What can R show clearly about iteration that numerical tables may hide?
What can exact reasoning prove that numerical iteration can only suggest?

title: 7  Numerical solutions of equations
text:
7.7 Answers

7.7.1 Answers to Exercises 6.1

7.7.1.1 Bronze

Let \(f(x)=x^2-3\). Then \(f(1)=-2\) and \(f(2)=1\), so there is a root between \(1\) and \(2\).
Let \(f(x)=x^3-2x-5\). Then \(f(2)=-1\) and \(f(3)=16\), so there is a root between \(2\) and \(3\).
Let \(f(x)=e^x-3\). Then \(f(1)=e-3<0\) and \(f(2)=e^2-3>0\), so there is a root between \(1\) and \(2\).
Let \(f(x)=\ln x+x-2\). Then \(f(1)=-1\) and \(f(2)=\ln2>0\), so there is a root between \(1\) and \(2\).
Let \(f(x)=\cos x-x\). Then \(f(0)=1\) and \(f(1)=\cos1-1<0\), so there is a root between \(0\) and \(1\).



7.7.1.2 Silver

For \(f(x)=x^3-x-4\), \(f(1.7)=-0.787\) and \(f(1.8)=0.032\), so there is a root between \(1.7\) and \(1.8\).
For \(f(x)=x^3-2x-2\), \(f(1.7)=-0.487\) and \(f(1.8)=0.232\), so there is a root between \(1.7\) and \(1.8\).
For \(f(x)=e^{-x}-x\), \(f(0.5)=0.1065\ldots\) and \(f(0.6)=-0.0512\ldots\), so there is a root between \(0.5\) and \(0.6\).
For \(f(x)=\ln x+x-3\), \(f(2)=\ln2-1<0\) and \(f(3)=\ln3>0\), so there is a root between \(2\) and \(3\).
For \(f(x)=x^3-6x+2\), there are sign changes on \((-3,-2)\), \((0,1)\) and \((2,3)\), so each interval contains a root.



7.7.1.3 Gold

Let \(f(x)=\ln x+x-2\). Since \[
f'(x)=\dfrac{1}{x}+1>0
\] for \(x>0\), \(f\) is strictly increasing. Since \(f(1)<0\) and \(f(2)>0\), there is exactly one root for \(x>0\).
Let \(f(x)=x^3-3x+1\). We have sign changes on \((-2,-1)\), \((0,1)\) and \((1,2)\), so the equation has roots in all three intervals.
For example, \[
f(x)=\dfrac{1}{x-1}
\] has \(f(0)<0\) and \(f(2)>0\), but it has no root between \(0\) and \(2\). The sign change occurs because of a discontinuity.
For example, \[
f(x)=(x-1)^2
\] has no sign change between \(0\) and \(2\), but it has a root at \(x=1\).
For \(f(x)=x^3-x-4\), \(f(1.7955)<0\) and \(f(1.7965)>0\), so the root lies between \(1.7955\) and \(1.7965\). Therefore the root is \(1.796\) to three decimal places.




7.7.2 Answers to Exercises 6.2

7.7.2.1 Bronze

\(x_2=1.7321\), \(x_3=1.9319\), \(x_4=1.9829\).
\(x_2=1.7652\), \(x_3=1.7931\), \(x_4=1.7960\).
\(x_2=0.8776\), \(x_3=0.6390\), \(x_4=0.8027\).
\(x_2=0.6065\), \(x_3=0.5452\), \(x_4=0.5797\).
\(x_2=0.8\), \(x_3=0.2\), \(x_4=0.8\), \(x_5=0.2\).



7.7.2.2 Silver

The iteration converges to \(x=2.000\).
The root is \(x=1.796\) to three decimal places.
The solution is \(x=0.567\) to three decimal places.
Starting with \(x_1=1.5\), the iteration gives \(x_2=-0.625\), \(x_3=-4.2441\ldots\) and \(x_4=-80.4486\ldots\), so it diverges.
Starting with \(x_1=1\), the iteration gives \(x_2=2\), \(x_3=1\), \(x_4=2\), and so on. It enters a period-two loop.



7.7.2.3 Gold

For \(g_1(x)=\sqrt[3]{x+4}\), \[
|g_1'(\alpha)|\approx0.103<1,
\] so the iteration is locally convergent. For \(g_2(x)=x^3-4\), \[
|g_2'(\alpha)|\approx9.68>1,
\] so the iteration is locally divergent.
If \(x_{n+1}=1-x_n\), then \[
x_{n+2}=1-x_{n+1}=1-(1-x_n)=x_n.
\] Therefore the sequence repeats every two terms unless \(x_n=\dfrac{1}{2}\).
At the fixed point \(\alpha\), \(e^{-\alpha}=\alpha\). For \(g(x)=e^{-x}\), \[
g'(\alpha)=-e^{-\alpha}=-\alpha\approx-0.567.
\] Since \(|g'(\alpha)|<1\), the iteration converges. Since \(g'(\alpha)<0\), it oscillates while converging.
If \(x_n\to L\) and \(x_{n+1}=g(x_n)\), then taking limits gives \[
L=g(L),
\] provided \(g\) is continuous.
The iteration gives \[
x_2=1.75,
\] \[
x_3=1.732142857\ldots,
\] \[
x_4=1.73205081\ldots.
\] Hence \[
\sqrt3\approx1.73205.
\]




7.7.3 Answers to Exercises 6.3

7.7.3.1 Bronze

\(x=0.739\) to three decimal places.
\(x=0.567\) to three decimal places.
The non-zero solution is \(\theta=1.895\) to three decimal places.
\(x=1.146\) to three decimal places.
A fixed point satisfies \[
x=\sqrt{x+6}.
\] Squaring gives \[
x^2=x+6,
\] so \[
x^2-x-6=0.
\] The positive fixed point is \(x=3\).



7.7.3.2 Silver

A suitable iteration is \[
x_{n+1}=\sqrt[3]{x_n+4}.
\] Starting near the root gives \(x=1.796\) to three decimal places.
The non-zero solution is \(\theta=1.895\) to three decimal places.
Starting with \(x_1=1\), the iteration gives \(1,2,1,2,\ldots\), so it does not converge to \(\sqrt2\).
The fixed point satisfies \(x=2x-1\), so \(x=1\). But if \(x_1\neq1\), then the distance from \(1\) doubles each time, so the iteration diverges.
The solution is \(x=1.146\) to three decimal places.



7.7.3.3 Gold

Let \(f(x)=\cos x-x\). Then \[
f'(x)=-\sin x-1<0
\] on \((0,1)\). So \(f\) is strictly decreasing. Since \(f(0)>0\) and \(f(1)<0\), there is exactly one root in \((0,1)\).
For \(g(x)=e^{-x}\), at the fixed point \(\alpha\), \[
g'(\alpha)=-e^{-\alpha}=-\alpha.
\] Since \(\alpha\approx0.567\), we have \(|g'(\alpha)|<1\), so the iteration converges. The negative derivative explains the oscillation.
Since \[
x_{n+2}=1-x_{n+1}=1-(1-x_n)=x_n,
\] the sequence repeats every two terms. It is constant only if \(x_n=1-x_n\), giving \(x_n=\dfrac{1}{2}\).
For \(f(x)=x^3-x-4\), \(f(1.7955)<0\) and \(f(1.7965)>0\), so the root lies between \(1.7955\) and \(1.7965\). Therefore the root is \(1.796\) to three decimal places.
For \(f(\theta)=\theta-2\sin\theta\), \(f(1.8945)<0\) and \(f(1.8955)>0\), so the non-zero solution lies between \(1.8945\) and \(1.8955\). Therefore \[
\theta=1.895
\] to three decimal places.




7.7.4 Answers to STEP-style extension questions

Since \(f\) is continuous, its graph cannot move from a negative value at \(x=a\) to a positive value at \(x=b\) without crossing the \(x\)-axis. Therefore there must be some \(\alpha\in(a,b)\) such that \[
f(\alpha)=0.
\] Continuity is needed. For example, \[
f(x)=\dfrac{1}{x-1}
\] changes sign between \(0\) and \(2\), but has no root in that interval because it is discontinuous at \(x=1\).
By the mean value theorem, for some point \(c\) between \(x_n\) and \(\alpha\), \[
g(x_n)-g(\alpha)=g'(c)(x_n-\alpha).
\] Since \(g(\alpha)=\alpha\), \[
x_{n+1}-\alpha=g'(c)(x_n-\alpha).
\] Taking absolute values gives \[
|x_{n+1}-\alpha|
=
|g'(c)||x_n-\alpha|
\leq
k|x_n-\alpha|.
\] Since \(0<k<1\), the error shrinks at each step, so the iteration converges to \(\alpha\).
The iteration is \[
x_{n+1}=\dfrac{2}{x_n}.
\] Then \[
x_{n+2}=\dfrac{2}{x_{n+1}}
=
\dfrac{2}{\dfrac{2}{x_n}}
=
x_n.
\] Hence the sequence repeats every two terms. It is constant only when \[
x_n=\dfrac{2}{x_n},
\] giving \[
x_n^2=2.
\] Therefore the sequence converges only if the starting value is already \(\sqrt2\) or \(-\sqrt2\).
Let \[
g_1(x)=\sqrt[3]{x+4}.
\] Then \[
g_1'(x)=\dfrac{1}{3(x+4)^{2/3}}.
\] At the root \(\alpha\approx1.796\), \[
|g_1'(\alpha)|\approx0.103<1.
\] So the iteration \[
x_{n+1}=\sqrt[3]{x_n+4}
\] converges near \(\alpha\).
Now let \[
g_2(x)=x^3-4.
\] Then \[
g_2'(x)=3x^2.
\] At \(\alpha\approx1.796\), \[
|g_2'(\alpha)|\approx9.68>1.
\] Therefore the iteration \[
x_{n+1}=x_n^3-4
\] diverges near \(\alpha\).
Let \[
f(x)=\cos x-x.
\] Then \[
f(0)=1>0
\] and \[
f(1)=\cos1-1<0.
\] So there is at least one root in \((0,1)\).
Also, \[
f'(x)=-\sin x-1<0
\] for \(0\leq x\leq1\). Therefore \(f\) is strictly decreasing and can cross the \(x\)-axis only once. Hence the solution is unique.
The iteration \[
x_{n+1}=\cos x_n
\] has iteration function \(g(x)=\cos x\). At the fixed point \(\alpha\), \[
g'(\alpha)=-\sin\alpha.
\] Since \(0<\sin\alpha<1\), we have \[
|g'(\alpha)|<1.
\] Therefore the iteration converges near \(\alpha\). Since \(g'(\alpha)<0\), the convergence is oscillatory.

title: 8  Further algebra
text:
8.1 Overview
This chapter develops five connected algebraic ideas:
The central aim is to rewrite algebraic expressions into forms that are easier to expand, approximate, integrate or sum.

title: 8  Further algebra
8  Further algebra
text:
8.2 7.1 Improper algebraic fractions

8.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
An algebraic fraction is proper when
\[
\deg(\text{numerator})<\deg(\text{denominator}).
\]
It is improper when
\[
\deg(\text{numerator})\geq\deg(\text{denominator}).
\]
If an algebraic fraction is improper, do long division first.
The division algorithm says that
\[
P(x)=D(x)Q(x)+R(x),
\]
where \(Q(x)\) is the quotient and \(R(x)\) is the remainder.
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{P(x)}{D(x)}
=
Q(x)+\dfrac{R(x)}{D(x)}.
\]
The fraction
\[
\dfrac{R(x)}{D(x)}
\]
is then proper.






Warning



Before using partial fractions, always check the degrees. If the power of the top is greater than or equal to the power of the bottom, do long division first.




8.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-4, 4, length.out = 1000)
)

df <- subset(df, abs(x^2 + 1) > 0.05)

df$original <- (2 * df$x^3 + 3 * df$x^2 - df$x + 5) / (df$x^2 + 1)
df$decomposed <- 2 * df$x + 3 + (-3 * df$x + 2) / (df$x^2 + 1)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = original, linetype = "original fraction"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = decomposed, linetype = "polynomial plus proper fraction"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Checking an improper algebraic fraction",
    subtitle = "The two graphs overlap because the two expressions are identical.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 8.1: An improper algebraic fraction written as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.







8.2.3 Worked example
Express
\[
\dfrac{2x^3+3x^2-x+5}{x^2+1}
\]
as the sum of a polynomial and a proper fraction.
Since the degree of the numerator is greater than the degree of the denominator, we divide first.
First,
\[
\dfrac{2x^3}{x^2}=2x.
\]
Multiply:
\[
2x(x^2+1)=2x^3+2x.
\]
Subtract:
\[
2x^3+3x^2-x+5-(2x^3+2x)=3x^2-3x+5.
\]
Next,
\[
\dfrac{3x^2}{x^2}=3.
\]
Multiply:
\[
3(x^2+1)=3x^2+3.
\]
Subtract:
\[
3x^2-3x+5-(3x^2+3)=-3x+2.
\]
Therefore,
\[
2x^3+3x^2-x+5
=
(x^2+1)(2x+3)+(-3x+2).
\]
Hence,
\[
\dfrac{2x^3+3x^2-x+5}{x^2+1}
=
2x+3+\dfrac{-3x+2}{x^2+1}.
\]


8.2.4 Short investigation
Change the numerator below and compare the original fraction with its decomposed form.


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-5, 5, length.out = 1000)
)

df <- subset(df, abs(x - 2) > 0.05)

df$original <- (df$x^3 - 4 * df$x + 1) / (df$x - 2)
df$decomposed <- df$x^2 + 2 * df$x + 1 / (df$x - 2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = original, linetype = "original"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = decomposed, linetype = "decomposed"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-20, 20)) +
  labs(
    title = "Long division check",
    subtitle = "The vertical line marks the value excluded from the domain.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 8.2: Checking a long-division decomposition graphically.







8.2.5 Exercises 7.1

8.2.5.1 Bronze

Express \(\dfrac{x^2+5x+6}{x+2}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Express \(\dfrac{x^2-1}{x-1}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Express \(\dfrac{x^3+2x^2+3x+4}{x+1}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Express \(\dfrac{2x^3+x^2-5}{x+2}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Express \(\dfrac{x^4-1}{x^2+1}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.



8.2.5.2 Silver

Express \(\dfrac{x^3-4x+1}{x-2}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Express \(\dfrac{3x^3-2x^2+x-7}{x+1}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Express \(\dfrac{x^4+2x^2+1}{x^2+2x+1}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Express \(\dfrac{2x^4-x^3+3x^2-x+5}{x^2-1}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Express \(\dfrac{x^5+x+1}{x^2+x+1}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.



8.2.5.3 Gold

Express \(\dfrac{x^3+1}{x^2+x+1}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Express \(\dfrac{3x^4+2x^3-x+7}{x^2+x-1}\) as a polynomial plus a proper fraction.
Explain why \(\dfrac{x^3+2x+1}{x^2+1}\) must be divided before using partial fractions.
Given that \[
\dfrac{x^3+ax^2+bx+5}{x+1}
=
x^2+2x-1+\dfrac{6}{x+1},
\] find \(a\) and \(b\).
Show that \[
\dfrac{x^4-1}{x^2+1}=x^2-1.
\]

title: 8  Further algebra
text:
8.3 7.2 Partial fractions

8.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
Partial fractions reverse the process of adding algebraic fractions.
This chapter uses three main proper-fraction types.


8.3.2 Type 1: distinct linear factors
If the denominator has distinct linear factors, use one constant numerator over each factor.
For example,
\[
\dfrac{5x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)}
=
\dfrac{A}{x-1}+\dfrac{B}{x+2}.
\]


8.3.3 Type 2: repeated linear factors
If the denominator has a repeated linear factor, include each power of that factor.
For example,
\[
\dfrac{3x+5}{(x-1)^2}
=
\dfrac{A}{x-1}+\dfrac{B}{(x-1)^2}.
\]
For a denominator such as \((x-1)^3\), we would use
\[
\dfrac{A}{x-1}+\dfrac{B}{(x-1)^2}+\dfrac{C}{(x-1)^3}.
\]


8.3.4 Type 3: an irreducible quadratic factor
If the denominator contains a quadratic factor that cannot be factorised over the real numbers, use a linear numerator over that quadratic.
For example,
\[
\dfrac{7x-1}{(x-1)(x^2+2)}
=
\dfrac{A}{x-1}+\dfrac{Bx+C}{x^2+2}.
\]


8.3.5 Worked example
Express
\[
\dfrac{7x-1}{(x-1)(x^2+2)}
\]
in partial fractions.
Write
\[
\dfrac{7x-1}{(x-1)(x^2+2)}
=
\dfrac{A}{x-1}+\dfrac{Bx+C}{x^2+2}.
\]
Multiplying throughout by \((x-1)(x^2+2)\) gives
\[
7x-1=A(x^2+2)+(Bx+C)(x-1).
\]
Expanding,
\[
7x-1
=
A x^2+2A+Bx^2-Bx+Cx-C.
\]
Collecting terms,
\[
7x-1
=
(A+B)x^2+(C-B)x+(2A-C).
\]
Equating coefficients gives
\[
A+B=0,
\]
\[
C-B=7,
\]
and
\[
2A-C=-1.
\]
Solving gives
\[
A=2,\quad B=-2,\quad C=5.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{7x-1}{(x-1)(x^2+2)}
=
\dfrac{2}{x-1}+\dfrac{-2x+5}{x^2+2}.
\]


8.3.6 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-4, 4, length.out = 1200)
)

df <- subset(df, abs(x - 1) > 0.05)

df$original <- (7 * df$x - 1) / ((df$x - 1) * (df$x^2 + 2))
df$decomposed <- 2 / (df$x - 1) + (-2 * df$x + 5) / (df$x^2 + 2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = original, linetype = "original"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = decomposed, linetype = "partial fractions"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-12, 12)) +
  labs(
    title = "Partial fractions",
    subtitle = "The two graphs overlap away from the excluded value x = 1.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 8.3: Checking a partial-fraction decomposition.







8.3.7 Short investigation
Compare the three partial-fraction templates.

Code
data.frame(
  type = c(
    "distinct linear factors",
    "repeated linear factor",
    "irreducible quadratic factor"
  ),
  example_denominator = c(
    "(x - 1)(x + 2)",
    "(x - 1)^2",
    "(x - 1)(x^2 + 2)"
  ),
  template = c(
    "A/(x - 1) + B/(x + 2)",
    "A/(x - 1) + B/(x - 1)^2",
    "A/(x - 1) + (Bx + C)/(x^2 + 2)"
  )
)




Table 8.1



                          type example_denominator
1      distinct linear factors      (x - 1)(x + 2)
2       repeated linear factor           (x - 1)^2
3 irreducible quadratic factor    (x - 1)(x^2 + 2)
                        template
1          A/(x - 1) + B/(x + 2)
2        A/(x - 1) + B/(x - 1)^2
3 A/(x - 1) + (Bx + C)/(x^2 + 2)






8.3.8 Exercises 7.2

8.3.8.1 Bronze

Express \(\dfrac{5x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)}\) in partial fractions.
Express \(\dfrac{7x+1}{(x+1)(x+3)}\) in partial fractions.
Express \(\dfrac{3x+5}{(x-1)^2}\) in partial fractions.
Express \(\dfrac{4x-1}{(x+2)^2}\) in partial fractions.
Express \(\dfrac{7x-1}{(x-1)(x^2+2)}\) in partial fractions.



8.3.8.2 Silver

Express \(\dfrac{2x+5}{(x-2)(x+1)}\) in partial fractions.
Express \(\dfrac{2x+1}{(x+2)^2}\) in partial fractions.
Express \(\dfrac{3x^2+x+3}{(x+1)(x^2+4)}\) in partial fractions.
Express \(\dfrac{1}{x(x+1)}\) in partial fractions.
Express \(\dfrac{x^2+5x+7}{(x+1)(x+2)}\) in partial fractions.



8.3.8.3 Gold

Express \(\dfrac{4x+8}{(x+1)^2(x+3)}\) in partial fractions.
Express \(\dfrac{2x^2+4x-1}{(x-2)(x^2+1)}\) in partial fractions.
Explain why \(\dfrac{x^2+5x+7}{(x+1)(x+2)}\) needs long division first.
Show that \[
\dfrac{1}{r(r+1)}=\dfrac{1}{r}-\dfrac{1}{r+1}.
\] Hence find \[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{r(r+1)}.
\]
Suppose \[
\dfrac{kx+5}{(x-1)(x+2)}
=
\dfrac{A}{x-1}+\dfrac{B}{x+2}
\] and \(A=2B\). Find \(k\).

title: 8  Further algebra
section: 8.4 7.3 Binomial expansion of \((1+x)^n\) for values of \(n\) that are not positive integers
text:
8.4 7.3 Binomial expansion of \((1+x)^n\) for values of \(n\) that are not positive integers

8.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
For any rational number \(n\),
\[
(1+x)^n
=
1+nx+\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2!}x^2
+
\dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{3!}x^3
+\cdots
\]
provided
\[
|x|<1.
\]
When \(n\) is not a non-negative integer, the expansion does not usually terminate. The more terms we include, the better the approximation tends to be inside the interval of validity.


8.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
power <- -0.5

x_values <- seq(-0.9, 0.9, length.out = 800)

exact_df <- data.frame(
  x = x_values,
  y_exact = (1 + x_values)^power
)

approx_df <- expand.grid(
  x = x_values,
  highest_power = c(1, 2, 3, 4)
)

approx_df$terms_label <- paste0("up to x^", approx_df$highest_power)
approx_df$y_value <- mapply(
  function(x_input, highest_power_input) {
    binomial_approx(x_input, power, highest_power_input)
  },
  approx_df$x,
  approx_df$highest_power
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = exact_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y_exact, linetype = "exact"),
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = approx_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y_value, linetype = terms_label),
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 4)) +
  labs(
    title = "Binomial approximation for (1 + x)^n",
    subtitle = "Here n = -0.5. Extra terms improve the approximation inside -1 < x < 1.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 8.4: Approximating (1 + x)^n using more binomial terms.







8.4.3 Worked example
Expand
\[
(1+x)^{-2}
\]
up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Here,
\[
n=-2.
\]
So
\[
(1+x)^{-2}
=
1+(-2)x
+
\dfrac{(-2)(-3)}{2!}x^2
+
\dfrac{(-2)(-3)(-4)}{3!}x^3
+\cdots
\]
Therefore,
\[
(1+x)^{-2}
=
1-2x+3x^2-4x^3+\cdots.
\]
The expansion is valid for
\[
|x|<1.
\]


8.4.4 Short investigation
Use R to compare approximations for several powers.


Code
x_values <- seq(-0.8, 0.8, length.out = 600)

df <- expand.grid(
  x = x_values,
  power_label = c("n = 1/2", "n = -1", "n = -2")
)

df$power <- ifelse(df$power_label == "n = 1/2", 0.5, ifelse(df$power_label == "n = -1", -1, -2))

df$exact <- (1 + df$x)^df$power
df$approximation <- mapply(
  function(x_input, power_input) {
    binomial_approx(x_input, power_input, 3)
  },
  df$x,
  df$power
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = exact, linetype = "exact"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = approximation, linetype = "up to x^3"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ power_label) +
  labs(
    title = "Binomial approximations up to x^3",
    subtitle = "The approximation is most reliable near x = 0.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 8.5: Different rational powers give different binomial approximation behaviour.







8.4.5 Exercises 7.3

8.4.5.1 Bronze

Expand \((1+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\).
Expand \((1+x)^{-1}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Expand \((1+x)^{-2}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Expand \((1+x)^{\frac{3}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\).
State the range of validity for the expansion of \((1+x)^n\) when \(n\) is not a non-negative integer.



8.4.5.2 Silver

Expand \((1-2x)^{-1}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\). State the range of validity.
Expand \((1+3x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Expand \((1-4x)^{-\frac{1}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\).
Use the binomial expansion of \((1+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\) to estimate \(\sqrt{1.04}\).
Expand \((1+x)^{-\frac{1}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).



8.4.5.3 Gold

Show that the coefficient of \(x^3\) in \((1+x)^n\) is \[
\dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6}.
\]
Find the coefficient of \(x^3\) in \((1-2x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\).
Expand \((1+x)^{\frac{1}{3}}(1-x)^{-1}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Find \(k\) if the coefficient of \(x^2\) in \((1+kx)^{-\frac{1}{2}}\) is \(6\).
Find the range of validity for the expansion of \((1-2x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\) and explain why substituting \(x=0.6\) is invalid.

title: 8  Further algebra
section: 8.5 7.4 Binomial expansion of \((a+x)^n\) for values of \(n\) that are not positive integers
text:
8.5 7.4 Binomial expansion of \((a+x)^n\) for values of \(n\) that are not positive integers

8.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
To expand
\[
(a+x)^n,
\]
factor out \(a\) first:
\[
(a+x)^n
=
a^n\left(1+\dfrac{x}{a}\right)^n.
\]
Then apply the binomial expansion to
\[
\left(1+\dfrac{x}{a}\right)^n.
\]
The expansion is valid when
\[
\left|\dfrac{x}{a}\right|<1.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
|x|<|a|.
\]


8.5.2 Visualisation using R


Code
x_values <- seq(-3.5, 3.5, length.out = 800)

exact_df <- data.frame(
  x = x_values,
  y_exact = sqrt(4 + x_values)
)

approx_df <- expand.grid(
  x = x_values,
  highest_power = c(1, 2, 3)
)

approx_df$terms_label <- paste0("up to x^", approx_df$highest_power)
approx_df$y_value <- 2 * mapply(
  function(x_input, highest_power_input) {
    binomial_approx(x_input / 4, 0.5, highest_power_input)
  },
  approx_df$x,
  approx_df$highest_power
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = exact_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y_exact, linetype = "exact"),
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = approx_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y_value, linetype = terms_label),
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Binomial approximation for sqrt(4 + x)",
    subtitle = "The expansion is valid for |x| < 4.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 8.6: Approximating sqrt(4 + x) using binomial expansions.







8.5.3 Worked example
Expand
\[
(4+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}
\]
up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
First write
\[
(4+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}
=
4^{\frac{1}{2}}\left(1+\dfrac{x}{4}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}.
\]
So
\[
(4+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}
=
2\left(1+\dfrac{x}{4}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}.
\]
Now
\[
(1+u)^{\frac{1}{2}}
=
1+\dfrac{1}{2}u-\dfrac{1}{8}u^2+\dfrac{1}{16}u^3+\cdots.
\]
Using
\[
u=\dfrac{x}{4},
\]
we get
\[
(4+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}
=
2\left[
1+\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{x}{4}\right)
-\dfrac{1}{8}\left(\dfrac{x}{4}\right)^2
+\dfrac{1}{16}\left(\dfrac{x}{4}\right)^3
+\cdots
\right].
\]
Therefore,
\[
(4+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}
=
2+\dfrac{x}{4}-\dfrac{x^2}{64}+\dfrac{x^3}{512}+\cdots.
\]
The expansion is valid when
\[
|x|<4.
\]


8.5.4 Short investigation
Use the approximation to estimate square roots near \(4\).

Code
x_values <- c(-0.5, -0.2, 0.1, 0.5)

data.frame(
  x = x_values,
  exact = round(sqrt(4 + x_values), 8),
  approximation_up_to_x_squared = round(2 + x_values / 4 - x_values^2 / 64, 8)
)




Table 8.2



     x    exact approximation_up_to_x_squared
1 -0.5 1.870829                      1.871094
2 -0.2 1.949359                      1.949375
3  0.1 2.024846                      2.024844
4  0.5 2.121320                      2.121094






8.5.5 Exercises 7.4

8.5.5.1 Bronze

Expand \((2+x)^{-1}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\).
Expand \((4+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\).
Expand \((3+x)^{-1}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\).
State the range of validity for the expansion of \((5+x)^{-\frac{1}{2}}\).
Use the expansion of \((4+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\) to estimate \(\sqrt{4.1}\).



8.5.5.2 Silver

Expand \((2-3x)^{-1}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Expand \((9+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\).
Expand \((4-2x)^{-\frac{1}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\).
Find the range of validity for the expansion of \((3-2x)^{-1}\).
Find the coefficient of \(x^3\) in the expansion of \((2+x)^{-3}\).



8.5.5.3 Gold

Show that \[
(a+x)^n
=
a^n\left(1+\dfrac{x}{a}\right)^n.
\] Hence explain why the range of validity is \(|x|<|a|\).
Find \(k\) if the coefficient of \(x^2\) in \((4+kx)^{\frac{1}{2}}\) is \(-\dfrac{1}{64}\).
Expand \[
\dfrac{\sqrt{4+x}}{2-x}
\] up to and including the term in \(x^2\).
Find the range of validity for the expansion of \[
\dfrac{\sqrt{4+x}}{2-x}.
\]
Use the expansion of \((9+x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\) to estimate \(\sqrt{9.2}\).

title: 8  Further algebra
text:
8.6 7.5 Partial fractions and binomial expansions

8.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
Partial fractions are often used before binomial expansion.
For example,
\[
\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}
\]
can be split into simpler fractions:
\[
\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}
=
\dfrac{2}{1-x}-\dfrac{1}{1+2x}.
\]
Each term can then be expanded separately.
The expansion
\[
\dfrac{1}{1-x}
=
1+x+x^2+x^3+\cdots
\]
is valid for
\[
|x|<1.
\]
The expansion
\[
\dfrac{1}{1+2x}
=
1-2x+4x^2-8x^3+\cdots
\]
is valid for
\[
|2x|<1.
\]
So the combined expansion is valid for
\[
|x|<\dfrac{1}{2}.
\]


8.6.2 Visualisation using R


Code
x_values <- seq(-0.45, 0.45, length.out = 800)

df <- data.frame(
  x = x_values
)

df$exact <- (1 + 5 * df$x) / ((1 - df$x) * (1 + 2 * df$x))
df$approximation <- 1 + 4 * df$x - 2 * df$x^2 + 10 * df$x^3

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = exact, linetype = "exact"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = approximation, linetype = "up to x^3"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Partial fractions before binomial expansion",
    subtitle = "The approximation is accurate near x = 0 and valid for |x| < 1/2.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 8.7: Exact function and binomial approximation after partial fractions.







8.6.3 Worked example
Obtain the expansion of
\[
\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}
\]
in ascending powers of \(x\), up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
First write
\[
\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}
=
\dfrac{A}{1-x}+\dfrac{B}{1+2x}.
\]
Multiplying through by \((1-x)(1+2x)\) gives
\[
1+5x=A(1+2x)+B(1-x).
\]
Equating coefficients gives
\[
A+B=1
\]
and
\[
2A-B=5.
\]
Solving gives
\[
A=2,\quad B=-1.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}
=
\dfrac{2}{1-x}-\dfrac{1}{1+2x}.
\]
Now,
\[
\dfrac{2}{1-x}
=
2(1+x+x^2+x^3+\cdots)
\]
and
\[
-\dfrac{1}{1+2x}
=
-(1-2x+4x^2-8x^3+\cdots).
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}
=
1+4x-2x^2+10x^3+\cdots.
\]
The expansion is valid when
\[
|x|<\dfrac{1}{2}.
\]


8.6.4 Short investigation
Compare exact and approximate values.

Code
x_values <- c(-0.2, -0.1, 0.1, 0.2)

data.frame(
  x = x_values,
  exact = round((1 + 5 * x_values) / ((1 - x_values) * (1 + 2 * x_values)), 6),
  approximation = round(1 + 4 * x_values - 2 * x_values^2 + 10 * x_values^3, 6)
)




Table 8.3



     x    exact approximation
1 -0.2 0.000000          0.04
2 -0.1 0.568182          0.57
3  0.1 1.388889          1.39
4  0.2 1.785714          1.80






8.6.5 Exercises 7.5

8.6.5.1 Bronze

Obtain the expansion of \(\dfrac{3+x}{(1-x)(1+x)}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Obtain the expansion of \(\dfrac{1}{(1-x)(1+2x)}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Obtain the expansion of \(\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
State the range of validity for the expansion of \(\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}\).
Obtain the expansion of \(\dfrac{2+7x}{(1-x)(1+3x)}\) up to and including the term in \(x^2\).



8.6.5.2 Silver

Obtain the expansion of \(\dfrac{1+4x+x^2}{(1-x)(1+2x)}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Obtain the expansion of \(\dfrac{5+2x}{(1-x)(1+x)^2}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\).
Obtain the expansion of \(\dfrac{2-5x}{(1+x)(1-3x)}\) up to and including the term in \(x^3\). State the range of validity.
Use the expansion of \(\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}\) to estimate its value when \(x=0.1\).
Find the coefficient of \(x^3\) in the expansion of \(\dfrac{1+5x}{(1-x)(1+2x)}\).



8.6.5.3 Gold

Show that \[
\dfrac{5+2x}{(1-x)(1+x)^2}
=
\dfrac{7}{4(1-x)}
+\dfrac{7}{4(1+x)}
+\dfrac{3}{2(1+x)^2}.
\]
Explain why long division is needed before expanding \[
\dfrac{1+4x+x^2}{(1-x)(1+2x)}.
\]
Find the range of validity for the expansion of \[
\dfrac{2-5x}{(1+x)(1-3x)}.
\]
Find \(k\) if the coefficient of \(x\) in the expansion of \[
\dfrac{1+kx}{(1-x)(1+2x)}
\] is \(5\).
Find the coefficient of \(x^3\) in the expansion of \[
\dfrac{1+ax}{(1-x)(1+2x)}
\] in terms of \(a\).

title: 8  Further algebra
text:
8.7 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require proof, structure or exact reasoning.

8.7.1 STEP-style Question 1
Let \(n\) be rational. Prove that the coefficient of \(x^r\) in the binomial expansion of \((1+x)^n\) is
\[
\dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-r+1)}{r!}.
\]
Use your result to find the coefficient of \(x^4\) in
\[
(1-2x)^{-\frac{1}{2}}.
\]


8.7.2 STEP-style Question 2
Use partial fractions to prove that
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{r(r+1)}
=
\dfrac{n}{n+1}.
\]


8.7.3 STEP-style Question 3
Show that
\[
\dfrac{1+x}{(1-2x)(1+x)}
=
\dfrac{1}{1-2x},
\]
for \(x\neq -1\). Then explain why this simplification affects the apparent range of validity of a binomial expansion.


8.7.4 STEP-style Question 4
Express
\[
\dfrac{x^3+3x^2+5x+1}{(x+1)(x^2+1)}
\]
in the form
\[
A+\dfrac{B}{x+1}+\dfrac{Cx+D}{x^2+1}.
\]


8.7.5 STEP-style Question 5
Let
\[
F(x)=\dfrac{1+ax}{(1-x)(1+2x)}.
\]
Find the value of \(a\) such that the coefficient of \(x^3\) in the expansion of \(F(x)\) is zero.

title: 8  Further algebra
text:
8.8 Reflection questions

Why must an improper algebraic fraction be divided before partial fractions?
What are the three main partial-fraction types in this chapter?
Why does an irreducible quadratic factor require a linear numerator?
Why does the binomial expansion of \((1+x)^n\) have a validity condition when \(n\) is not a non-negative integer?
Why do extra terms improve approximation near \(x=0\)?
Why should \((a+x)^n\) be rewritten as \(a^n(1+\frac{x}{a})^n\) before expanding?
Why does the final range of validity in a combined expansion come from the most restrictive condition?
What can R show clearly about approximation error that algebra alone may hide?
What can exact algebra prove that a graph can only suggest?

title: 8  Further algebra
text:
8.9 Answers

8.9.1 Answers to Exercises 7.1

8.9.1.1 Bronze

\(x+3\).
\(x+1\).
\(x^2+x+2+\dfrac{2}{x+1}\).
\(2x^2-3x+6-\dfrac{17}{x+2}\).
\(x^2-1\).



8.9.1.2 Silver

\(x^2+2x+\dfrac{1}{x-2}\).
\(3x^2-5x+6-\dfrac{13}{x+1}\).
\(x^2-2x+5+\dfrac{-8x-4}{x^2+2x+1}\).
\(2x^2-x+5+\dfrac{-2x+10}{x^2-1}\).
\(x^3-x^2+1\).



8.9.1.3 Gold

\(x-1+\dfrac{2}{x^2+x+1}\).
\(3x^2-x+4+\dfrac{-6x+11}{x^2+x-1}\).
The numerator has degree \(3\) and the denominator has degree \(2\), so the fraction is improper.
\(a=3\) and \(b=1\).
Since \(x^4-1=(x^2+1)(x^2-1)\), the quotient is \(x^2-1\) and the remainder is \(0\).




8.9.2 Answers to Exercises 7.2

8.9.2.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{2}{x-1}+\dfrac{3}{x+2}\).
\(-\dfrac{3}{x+1}+\dfrac{10}{x+3}\).
\(\dfrac{3}{x-1}+\dfrac{8}{(x-1)^2}\).
\(\dfrac{4}{x+2}-\dfrac{9}{(x+2)^2}\).
\(\dfrac{2}{x-1}+\dfrac{-2x+5}{x^2+2}\).



8.9.2.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{3}{x-2}-\dfrac{1}{x+1}\).
\(\dfrac{2}{x+2}-\dfrac{3}{(x+2)^2}\).
\(\dfrac{1}{x+1}+\dfrac{2x-1}{x^2+4}\).
\(\dfrac{1}{x}-\dfrac{1}{x+1}\).
\(1+\dfrac{3}{x+1}-\dfrac{1}{x+2}\).



8.9.2.3 Gold

\(\dfrac{1}{x+1}+\dfrac{2}{(x+1)^2}-\dfrac{1}{x+3}\).
\(\dfrac{3}{x-2}+\dfrac{-x+2}{x^2+1}\).
The numerator and denominator both have degree \(2\), so the fraction is improper and long division must be done first.
The identity is \[
\dfrac{1}{r(r+1)}=\dfrac{1}{r}-\dfrac{1}{r+1}.
\] Therefore, \[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{r(r+1)}
=
1-\dfrac{1}{n+1}
=
\dfrac{n}{n+1}.
\]
\(k=5\).




8.9.3 Answers to Exercises 7.3

8.9.3.1 Bronze

\(1+\dfrac{1}{2}x-\dfrac{1}{8}x^2+\cdots\).
\(1-x+x^2-x^3+\cdots\).
\(1-2x+3x^2-4x^3+\cdots\).
\(1+\dfrac{3}{2}x+\dfrac{3}{8}x^2+\cdots\).
\(|x|<1\).



8.9.3.2 Silver

\(1+2x+4x^2+8x^3+\cdots\), valid for \(|x|<\dfrac{1}{2}\).
\(1+\dfrac{3}{2}x-\dfrac{9}{8}x^2+\dfrac{27}{16}x^3+\cdots\).
\(1+2x+6x^2+\cdots\).
\(\sqrt{1.04}\approx1.0198\).
\(1-\dfrac{1}{2}x+\dfrac{3}{8}x^2-\dfrac{5}{16}x^3+\cdots\).



8.9.3.3 Gold

The coefficient of \(x^3\) is the third binomial coefficient: \[
\dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{3!}
=
\dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6}.
\]
\(-\dfrac{1}{2}\).
\(1+\dfrac{4}{3}x+\dfrac{11}{9}x^2+\dfrac{104}{81}x^3+\cdots\).
\(k=\pm4\).
The range is \(|x|<\dfrac{1}{2}\). Substituting \(x=0.6\) is invalid because \(|2x|>1\).




8.9.4 Answers to Exercises 7.4

8.9.4.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{x}{4}+\dfrac{x^2}{8}+\cdots\).
\(2+\dfrac{x}{4}-\dfrac{x^2}{64}+\cdots\).
\(\dfrac{1}{3}-\dfrac{x}{9}+\dfrac{x^2}{27}+\cdots\).
\(|x|<5\).
\(\sqrt{4.1}\approx2.02484\).



8.9.4.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{3}{4}x+\dfrac{9}{8}x^2+\dfrac{27}{16}x^3+\cdots\).
\(3+\dfrac{x}{6}-\dfrac{x^2}{216}+\cdots\).
\(\dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{x}{8}+\dfrac{3x^2}{64}+\cdots\).
\(|x|<\dfrac{3}{2}\).
\(-\dfrac{5}{32}\).



8.9.4.3 Gold

Factorising gives \[
(a+x)^n=a^n\left(1+\dfrac{x}{a}\right)^n.
\] The binomial expansion requires \[
\left|\dfrac{x}{a}\right|<1,
\] so \(|x|<|a|\).
\(k=\pm1\).
\(1+\dfrac{5}{8}x+\dfrac{39}{128}x^2+\cdots\).
\(|x|<2\).
\(\sqrt{9.2}\approx3.03315\).




8.9.5 Answers to Exercises 7.5

8.9.5.1 Bronze

\(3+x+3x^2+x^3+\cdots\).
\(1-x+3x^2-5x^3+\cdots\).
\(1+4x-2x^2+10x^3+\cdots\).
\(|x|<\dfrac{1}{2}\).
\(2+3x+\cdots\).



8.9.5.2 Silver

\(1+3x+6x^3+\cdots\).
\(5-3x+8x^2-6x^3+\cdots\).
\(2-x+4x^2+5x^3+\cdots\), valid for \(|x|<\dfrac{1}{3}\).
The estimate is \(1.390\).
\(10\).



8.9.5.3 Gold

Multiplying the right-hand side by \((1-x)(1+x)^2\) gives \(5+2x\), so the identity holds.
The numerator and denominator both have degree \(2\), so the fraction is improper.
The conditions are \(|x|<1\) and \(|3x|<1\), so the range is \(|x|<\dfrac{1}{3}\).
\(k=6\).
\(3a-5\).




8.9.6 Answers to STEP-style extension questions

The coefficient of \(x^r\) is obtained by choosing the first \(r\) descending factors from \(n\) and dividing by \(r!\): \[
\dfrac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-r+1)}{r!}.
\] For \((1-2x)^{-\frac{1}{2}}\), the coefficient of \(x^4\) is \[
\dfrac{35}{128}\cdot16=\dfrac{35}{8}.
\]
Since \[
\dfrac{1}{r(r+1)}
=
\dfrac{1}{r}-\dfrac{1}{r+1},
\] the sum telescopes: \[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{r(r+1)}
=
\left(1-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)+\left(\dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{1}{3}\right)+\cdots+\left(\dfrac{1}{n}-\dfrac{1}{n+1}\right).
\] Therefore, \[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{r(r+1)}
=
1-\dfrac{1}{n+1}
=
\dfrac{n}{n+1}.
\]
For \(x\neq-1\), \[
\dfrac{1+x}{(1-2x)(1+x)}
=
\dfrac{1}{1-2x}.
\] If the expression is simplified first, the only binomial expansion needed is \[
\dfrac{1}{1-2x}=1+2x+4x^2+\cdots,
\] which is valid for \(|x|<\dfrac{1}{2}\). Without simplification, the factor \(1+x\) may misleadingly suggest another condition, but it cancels from the function.
Long division gives quotient \(1\) and remainder \(2x^2+4x\). Therefore, \[
\dfrac{x^3+3x^2+5x+1}{(x+1)(x^2+1)}
=
1+\dfrac{2x^2+4x}{(x+1)(x^2+1)}.
\] Now \[
\dfrac{2x^2+4x}{(x+1)(x^2+1)}
=
-\dfrac{1}{x+1}+\dfrac{3x+1}{x^2+1}.
\] Hence, \[
\dfrac{x^3+3x^2+5x+1}{(x+1)(x^2+1)}
=
1-\dfrac{1}{x+1}+\dfrac{3x+1}{x^2+1}.
\]
Expanding \[
\dfrac{1+ax}{(1-x)(1+2x)}
\] gives coefficient of \(x^3\) equal to \[
3a-5.
\] Setting this equal to zero gives \[
a=\dfrac{5}{3}.
\]

title: 9  Further calculus
text:
9.1 Overview
This chapter develops seven connected calculus ideas:
The central aim is to recognise structure before choosing a method. In this chapter, the difficulty is often not the calculation itself, but deciding which method is appropriate.

title: 9  Further calculus
9  Further calculus
text:
9.2 8.1 Derivative of \(\tan^{-1}x\)

9.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
The derivative of the inverse tangent function is
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\tan^{-1}x\right)
=
\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}.
\]
Using the chain rule,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\tan^{-1}(f(x))\right)
=
\dfrac{f'(x)}{1+[f(x)]^2}.
\]
In particular,
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\tan^{-1}(ax+b)\right)
=
\dfrac{a}{1+(ax+b)^2}.
\]


9.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-6, 6, length.out = 1000)
)

df$inverse_tangent <- atan(df$x)
df$derivative <- 1 / (1 + df$x^2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = inverse_tangent, linetype = "tan^{-1}(x)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = derivative, linetype = "1/(1 + x^2)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Inverse tangent and its derivative",
    subtitle = "The derivative is always positive and becomes small as |x| becomes large.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.1: The graph of y = tan^{-1}(x) and its derivative y = 1/(1 + x^2).





This graph is useful because it compares the function with its gradient function.
The curve
\[
y=\tan^{-1}x
\]
is increasing, but it gradually flattens out. The derivative
\[
y=\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}
\]
explains this: the gradient is positive for all \(x\), but tends to \(0\) as \(|x|\) becomes large.


9.2.3 Worked example
Differentiate
\[
y=\tan^{-1}(3x-2).
\]
Let
\[
u=3x-2.
\]
Then
\[
\dfrac{du}{dx}=3.
\]
Using the chain rule,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}
=
\dfrac{3}{1+(3x-2)^2}.
\]


9.2.4 Short investigation
Change the value of \(a\) below and observe how the derivative changes.


Code
a <- 3

df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-3, 3, length.out = 800)
)

df$inverse_tangent <- atan(a * df$x)
df$derivative <- a / (1 + (a * df$x)^2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = inverse_tangent, linetype = "tan^{-1}(ax)"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = derivative, linetype = "derivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = paste0("Function and derivative for y = tan^{-1}(", a, "x)"),
    subtitle = "The derivative is largest at x = 0.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.2: Comparing y = tan^{-1}(ax) with its derivative.







9.2.5 Exercises 8.1

9.2.5.1 Bronze

Differentiate \(y=\tan^{-1}x\).
Differentiate \(y=\tan^{-1}(3x)\).
Differentiate \(y=\tan^{-1}(2x-1)\).
Differentiate \(y=\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\).
Differentiate \(y=\tan^{-1}(1-x)\).



9.2.5.2 Silver

Differentiate \(y=x\tan^{-1}x\).
Differentiate \(y=\dfrac{\tan^{-1}x}{x}\), where \(x\neq0\).
Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=\tan^{-1}(2x)\) at \(x=0\).
Find the gradient of \(y=\tan^{-1}(x^2)\) at \(x=1\).
Show that, for \(x>0\), \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)\right)
=
-\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}.
\]



9.2.5.3 Gold

Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=\tan^{-1}x\) at \(x=1\).
Show that \(\tan^{-1}x+\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)\) is constant for \(x>0\), and find the constant.
Show that \(y=x-\tan^{-1}x\) has a stationary point at \(x=0\), but that this point is not a maximum or a minimum.
Differentiate \(y=\tan^{-1}(3x)-\tan^{-1}x\) and simplify your answer.
Find the maximum value of \[
y=\tan^{-1}x-\dfrac{x}{2}.
\]

title: 9  Further calculus
text:
9.3 8.2 Integration of \(\dfrac{1}{x^2+a^2}\)

9.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
Since
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\tan^{-1}x\right)=\dfrac{1}{1+x^2},
\]
we have
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx=\tan^{-1}x+C.
\]
More generally,
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+a^2}\,dx
=
\dfrac{1}{a}\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)+C,
\quad a>0.
\]
This result is particularly useful when a quadratic denominator cannot be factorised over the real numbers.


9.3.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 1, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- 1 / (1 + df$x^2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Area under y = 1/(1 + x^2)",
    subtitle = "The exact area from 0 to 1 is pi/4.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.3: The area under y = 1/(1 + x^2) from x = 0 to x = 1.





The exact area is
\[
\int_0^1 \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx
=
\left[\tan^{-1}x\right]_0^1
=
\dfrac{\pi}{4}.
\]


9.3.3 Worked example
Find
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+9}\,dx.
\]
Since
\[
x^2+9=x^2+3^2,
\]
we use \(a=3\).
Therefore,
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+9}\,dx
=
\dfrac{1}{3}\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{3}\right)+C.
\]


9.3.4 Short investigation
Change the value of \(a\) and observe how the curve changes.


Code
df <- expand.grid(
  x = seq(-6, 6, length.out = 800),
  a_value = c(1, 2, 3)
)

df$curve_label <- paste0("a = ", df$a_value)
df$y <- 1 / (df$x^2 + df$a_value^2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = curve_label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "The family y = 1/(x^2 + a^2)",
    subtitle = "The parameter a changes both height and spread.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Parameter"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.4: Graphs of y = 1/(x^2 + a^2).







9.3.5 Exercises 8.2

9.3.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+4}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+9}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{3}{x^2+9}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{2}{4x^2+1}\,dx\).



9.3.5.2 Silver

Evaluate \(\int_0^1 \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^2 \dfrac{1}{x^2+4}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{5}{x^2+25}\,dx\).
Find \(k\) if \[
\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+k^2}\,dx
=
\dfrac{1}{3}\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{3}\right)+C.
\]
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\sqrt3}\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx\).



9.3.5.3 Gold

Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{4x^2+9}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^1 \dfrac{6}{9+x^2}\,dx\).
Solve \[
\int_0^a \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx=\dfrac{\pi}{6},
\quad a>0.
\]
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+2x+2}\,dx\).
Show that \[
\int_0^1 \dfrac{1}{4x^2+1}\,dx
=
\dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}2.
\]

title: 9  Further calculus
text:
9.4 8.3 Integration of \(\dfrac{kf'(x)}{f(x)}\)

9.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
If \(f(x)\) is differentiable, then
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\ln|f(x)|\right)
=
\dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\int \dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)}\,dx
=
\ln|f(x)|+C.
\]
More generally,
\[
\int \dfrac{kf'(x)}{f(x)}\,dx
=
k\ln|f(x)|+C.
\]
The key is to recognise that the numerator is a constant multiple of the derivative of the denominator.


9.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(-3, 3, length.out = 800)
)

df$integrand <- 2 * df$x / (df$x^2 + 1)
df$antiderivative <- log(df$x^2 + 1)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = integrand, linetype = "integrand"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = antiderivative, linetype = "antiderivative"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Recognising f'(x)/f(x)",
    subtitle = "The antiderivative is logarithmic.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Curve"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.5: The structure f’(x)/f(x) for f(x) = x^2 + 1.







9.4.3 Worked example
Find
\[
\int \dfrac{6x+4}{3x^2+4x+1}\,dx.
\]
Let
\[
f(x)=3x^2+4x+1.
\]
Then
\[
f'(x)=6x+4.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\int \dfrac{6x+4}{3x^2+4x+1}\,dx
=
\ln|3x^2+4x+1|+C.
\]


9.4.4 Short investigation
Use R to check by differentiating numerically.

Code
x_values <- c(-2, -1, 0, 1, 2)

f <- function(x) x^2 + 1
f_prime <- function(x) 2 * x
antiderivative <- function(x) log(x^2 + 1)

numerical_derivative <- function(g, x, h = 1e-5) {
  (g(x + h) - g(x - h)) / (2 * h)
}

data.frame(
  x = x_values,
  integrand = round(f_prime(x_values) / f(x_values), 6),
  derivative_of_log = round(numerical_derivative(antiderivative, x_values), 6)
)




Table 9.1



   x integrand derivative_of_log
1 -2      -0.8              -0.8
2 -1      -1.0              -1.0
3  0       0.0               0.0
4  1       1.0               1.0
5  2       0.8               0.8






9.4.5 Exercises 8.3

9.4.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\int \dfrac{2x}{x^2+1}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x+1}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{3x^2}{x^3+4}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{2x+3}{x^2+3x+5}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{5e^x}{e^x+2}\,dx\).



9.4.5.2 Silver

Evaluate \(\int_0^1 \dfrac{2x}{x^2+1}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_1^3 \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{4x-1}{2x^2-x+5}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{6x+6}{3x^2+6x+1}\,dx\).
Find \(k\) if \[
\int \dfrac{kx}{x^2+1}\,dx=3\ln(x^2+1)+C.
\]



9.4.5.3 Gold

Evaluate \(\int_0^1 \dfrac{2x+2}{x^2+2x+2}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{x+1}{x^2+2x+5}\,dx\).
Prove that \[
\int \dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)}\,dx=\ln|f(x)|+C.
\]
Solve \[
\int_0^a \dfrac{2x}{1+x^2}\,dx=\ln5,
\quad a>0.
\]
Find \(\int \dfrac{2x+3}{x^2+3x+3}\,dx\).

title: 9  Further calculus
text:
9.5 8.4 Integration by substitution

9.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
Integration by substitution is the reverse of the chain rule.
If
\[
u=f(x),
\]
then
\[
\dfrac{du}{dx}=f'(x),
\]
so
\[
du=f'(x)\,dx.
\]
The purpose of substitution is to rewrite the whole integral in terms of a new variable.
A typical pattern is
\[
\int f'(x)[f(x)]^n\,dx.
\]
Using
\[
u=f(x),
\]
this becomes
\[
\int u^n\,du.
\]


9.5.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 1, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- 2 * df$x * (1 + df$x^2)^2

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "A substitution structure",
    subtitle = "For 2x(1 + x^2)^2, use u = 1 + x^2.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.6: The shaded area represents an integral suited to substitution.







9.5.3 Worked example
Find
\[
\int 2x(1+x^2)^4\,dx.
\]
Let
\[
u=1+x^2.
\]
Then
\[
\dfrac{du}{dx}=2x,
\]
so
\[
du=2x\,dx.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\int 2x(1+x^2)^4\,dx
=
\int u^4\,du.
\]
Hence,
\[
\int u^4\,du=\dfrac{u^5}{5}+C.
\]
Substituting back gives
\[
\int 2x(1+x^2)^4\,dx
=
\dfrac{(1+x^2)^5}{5}+C.
\]


9.5.4 Short investigation
Compare the exact area with a numerical approximation.

Code
exact_area <- ((1 + 1^2)^3 - (1 + 0^2)^3) / 3
numerical_area <- integrate(function(x) 2 * x * (1 + x^2)^2, lower = 0, upper = 1)$value

data.frame(
  method = c("exact", "R numerical integration"),
  area = c(exact_area, numerical_area)
)




Table 9.2



                   method     area
1                   exact 2.333333
2 R numerical integration 2.333333






9.5.5 Exercises 8.4

9.5.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\int 2x(x^2+1)^3\,dx\).
Find \(\int 3(3x+1)^4\,dx\).
Find \(\int x\sqrt{x^2+4}\,dx\).
Find \(\int e^{2x+1}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \cos5x\,dx\).



9.5.5.2 Silver

Evaluate \(\int_0^1 2x(x^2+1)^2\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^\pi \sin x\cos^2x\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{x}{x^2+1}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{e^x}{1+e^x}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^1 \dfrac{x}{(1+x^2)^2}\,dx\).



9.5.5.3 Gold

Evaluate \(\int_0^1 6x(1+x^2)^2\,dx\).
