Find integers \(a\) and \(b\) such that \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left((1+x^2)^5\right)=ax(1+x^2)^b.
\]
Find \(\int \dfrac{x^3}{1+x^2}\,dx\).
Show that, for \(a>0\), \[
\int_0^a \dfrac{2x}{(1+x^2)^2}\,dx
=
\dfrac{a^2}{1+a^2}.
\]

title: 9  Further calculus
section: 9.6 8.5 The use of partial fractions in integration
text:
9.6 8.5 The use of partial fractions in integration

9.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
Partial fractions are useful in integration because they split a complicated rational function into simpler terms.
For example,
\[
\dfrac{1}{x(x+1)}
=
\dfrac{1}{x}-\dfrac{1}{x+1}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{x(x+1)}\,dx
=
\ln|x|-\ln|x+1|+C.
\]






Warning



Before using partial fractions, check whether the algebraic fraction is proper. If the degree of the numerator is greater than or equal to the degree of the denominator, do long division first.




9.6.2 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0.2, 5, length.out = 800)
)

df$original <- 1 / (df$x * (df$x + 1))
df$partial_fraction <- 1 / df$x - 1 / (df$x + 1)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = original, linetype = "original"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = partial_fraction, linetype = "partial fractions"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Partial fractions before integration",
    subtitle = "The two expressions are identical on their common domain.",
    x = "x",
    y = "value",
    linetype = "Expression"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.7: A rational function split into partial fractions.







9.6.3 Worked example
Find
\[
\int \dfrac{3x+5}{(x+1)(x+2)}\,dx.
\]
First write
\[
\dfrac{3x+5}{(x+1)(x+2)}
=
\dfrac{A}{x+1}+\dfrac{B}{x+2}.
\]
Multiplying throughout by \((x+1)(x+2)\) gives
\[
3x+5=A(x+2)+B(x+1).
\]
So
\[
3x+5=(A+B)x+(2A+B).
\]
Equating coefficients gives
\[
A+B=3
\]
and
\[
2A+B=5.
\]
Solving gives
\[
A=2,
\quad
B=1.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\int \dfrac{3x+5}{(x+1)(x+2)}\,dx
=
\int \left(\dfrac{2}{x+1}+\dfrac{1}{x+2}\right)\,dx.
\]
Hence,
\[
\int \dfrac{3x+5}{(x+1)(x+2)}\,dx
=
2\ln|x+1|+\ln|x+2|+C.
\]


9.6.4 Short investigation
Use R to check a definite integral after partial fractions.

Code
exact_value <- log(4 / 3)
numerical_value <- integrate(function(x) 1 / ((x + 1) * (x + 2)), lower = 0, upper = 1)$value

data.frame(
  method = c("exact", "R numerical integration"),
  value = c(exact_value, numerical_value)
)




Table 9.3



                   method     value
1                   exact 0.2876821
2 R numerical integration 0.2876821






9.6.5 Exercises 8.5

9.6.5.1 Bronze

Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x(x+1)}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{(x-1)(x+2)}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{2x+3}{(x+1)(x+2)}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{(x+1)^2}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{5x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)}\,dx\).



9.6.5.2 Silver

Find \(\int \dfrac{3x+5}{(x+1)(x+2)}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x^2-1}\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^1 \dfrac{1}{(x+1)(x+2)}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{x+3}{x^2+3x+2}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x(x+1)^2}\,dx\).



9.6.5.3 Gold

Find \(\int \dfrac{x^2+1}{x(x+1)}\,dx\).
Find \(\int \dfrac{1}{x(x+1)(x+2)}\,dx\).
Use partial fractions to prove that \[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{r(r+1)}=\dfrac{n}{n+1}.
\]
Evaluate \(\int_1^2 \dfrac{1}{x(x+1)}\,dx\).
Find \(k\) if \[
\int \dfrac{kx+8}{(x+1)(x+2)}\,dx
=
3\ln|x+1|+2\ln|x+2|+C.
\]

title: 9  Further calculus
text:
9.7 8.6 Integration by parts

9.7.1 Key mathematical ideas
Integration by parts is the reverse of the product rule.
The product rule says
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}(uv)=u\dfrac{dv}{dx}+v\dfrac{du}{dx}.
\]
Rearranging and integrating gives
\[
\int u\dfrac{dv}{dx}\,dx
=
uv-\int v\dfrac{du}{dx}\,dx.
\]
This is usually written as
\[
\int u\,dv=uv-\int v\,du.
\]


9.7.2 Why display integration by parts?
A graph does not show the algebraic mechanism of integration by parts as directly as it shows area or volume. Its value is different: it shows the integral as an area under a product curve, while the method rewrites that area in terms of a boundary term minus a simpler area.
So integration by parts is worth displaying when we want students to see that an apparently difficult product integral such as
\[
\int xe^x\,dx
\]
still represents an area, even though the exact calculation needs an algebraic transformation.


9.7.3 Visualisation using R


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 1, length.out = 600)
)

df$y <- df$x * exp(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Area under y = x e^x",
    subtitle = "Integration by parts gives the exact area from 0 to 1 as 1.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.8: The shaded area represents the integral of x e^x from 0 to 1.







9.7.4 Worked example
Find
\[
\int xe^x\,dx.
\]
Choose
\[
u=x,
\quad
\dfrac{dv}{dx}=e^x.
\]
Then
\[
\dfrac{du}{dx}=1,
\quad
v=e^x.
\]
Using integration by parts,
\[
\int xe^x\,dx
=
xe^x-\int e^x\,dx.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\int xe^x\,dx
=
xe^x-e^x+C.
\]
So
\[
\int xe^x\,dx
=
(x-1)e^x+C.
\]


9.7.5 Short investigation
Compare exact and numerical values for an integration-by-parts problem.

Code
exact_area <- 1
numerical_area <- integrate(function(x) x * exp(x), lower = 0, upper = 1)$value

data.frame(
  method = c("exact", "R numerical integration"),
  area = c(exact_area, numerical_area)
)




Table 9.4



                   method area
1                   exact    1
2 R numerical integration    1






9.7.6 Exercises 8.6

9.7.6.1 Bronze

Find \(\int xe^x\,dx\).
Find \(\int x\cos x\,dx\).
Find \(\int x\sin x\,dx\).
Find \(\int \ln x\,dx\), where \(x>0\).
Find \(\int x\ln x\,dx\), where \(x>0\).



9.7.6.2 Silver

Evaluate \(\int_0^1 xe^x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}x\cos x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^\pi x\sin x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_1^e \ln x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^1 xe^{2x}\,dx\).



9.7.6.3 Gold

Find \(\int x^2e^x\,dx\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^1 x^2e^x\,dx\).
Find \(\int x^2\ln x\,dx\), where \(x>0\).
Evaluate \(\int_0^\pi x\sin2x\,dx\).
Prove the integration by parts formula from the product rule.

title: 9  Further calculus
text:
9.8 8.7 Further integration

9.8.1 Key mathematical ideas
Further integration is about choosing from several techniques:

direct reverse differentiation;
substitution;
partial fractions;
integration by parts;
trigonometric identities;
area formulae;
volume of revolution formulae.

For area under a curve above the \(x\)-axis,
\[
A=\int_a^b y\,dx.
\]
For volume generated by rotating the region under \(y=f(x)\) about the \(x\)-axis,
\[
V=\pi\int_a^b y^2\,dx.
\]


9.8.2 Visualisation using R: shaded area


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, pi, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- df$x * sin(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Area under y = x sin(x)",
    subtitle = "The exact area from 0 to pi is pi.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.9: The shaded area under y = x sin(x) from 0 to pi.







9.8.3 Worked example: area
Find the exact area under
\[
y=x\sin x
\]
from \(x=0\) to \(x=\pi\).
The area is
\[
A=\int_0^\pi x\sin x\,dx.
\]
Using integration by parts with
\[
u=x,
\quad
\dfrac{dv}{dx}=\sin x,
\]
we get
\[
\dfrac{du}{dx}=1,
\quad
v=-\cos x.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\int x\sin x\,dx
=
-x\cos x+\int \cos x\,dx.
\]
So
\[
\int x\sin x\,dx
=
-x\cos x+\sin x+C.
\]
Hence,
\[
A=
\left[-x\cos x+\sin x\right]_0^\pi.
\]
Therefore,
\[
A=\pi.
\]


9.8.4 Visualisation using R: volume of revolution


Code
df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 4, length.out = 800)
)

df$y <- sqrt(df$x)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Region for a volume of revolution",
    subtitle = "Rotating this region about the x-axis gives V = pi int_0^4 x dx.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 9.10: The shaded region under y = sqrt(x) from x = 0 to x = 4.







9.8.5 Worked example: volume of revolution
The region under
\[
y=\sqrt{x}
\]
from \(x=0\) to \(x=4\) is rotated completely about the \(x\)-axis. Find the exact volume.
Using
\[
V=\pi\int_a^b y^2\,dx,
\]
we get
\[
V=\pi\int_0^4 (\sqrt{x})^2\,dx.
\]
So
\[
V=\pi\int_0^4 x\,dx.
\]
Therefore,
\[
V=\pi\left[\dfrac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^4.
\]
Hence,
\[
V=8\pi.
\]


9.8.6 Exercises 8.7

9.8.6.1 Bronze

Find the area under \(y=x\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=3\).
Find the area under \(y=x^2\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=2\).
Find the volume formed when the region under \(y=x\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=2\) is rotated about the \(x\)-axis.
Find the volume formed when the region under \(y=\sqrt{x}\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=4\) is rotated about the \(x\)-axis.
Find the area under \(y=e^{-x}\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=\ln2\).



9.8.6.2 Silver

Find the area under \(y=x\ln x\) from \(x=1\) to \(x=e\).
Find the area under \(y=x\sin x\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=\pi\).
Find the volume formed when the region under \(y=e^{-x}\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=\ln2\) is rotated about the \(x\)-axis.
Find the area between \(y=x\) and \(y=x^2\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=1\).
Find the volume formed when the region under \(y=x^2\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=1\) is rotated about the \(x\)-axis.



9.8.6.3 Gold

Find the area between \(y=\sin x\) and \(y=\cos x\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\).
Find the volume formed when the region under \(y=\sqrt{1+x}\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=3\) is rotated about the \(x\)-axis.
Find the area under \(y=\ln x\) from \(x=1\) to \(x=e^2\).
Find the area under \(y=\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=1\).
Find the volume formed when the region under \(y=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+x}}\) from \(x=0\) to \(x=3\) is rotated about the \(x\)-axis.

title: 9  Further calculus
text:
9.9 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require proof, structure or exact reasoning.

9.9.1 STEP-style Question 1
Use implicit differentiation to prove that
\[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\tan^{-1}x\right)=\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}.
\]


9.9.2 STEP-style Question 2
Prove that
\[
\int_0^1 \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx=\dfrac{\pi}{4}.
\]
Then interpret this result as an area.


9.9.3 STEP-style Question 3
Use integration by parts to prove that
\[
\int_0^1 xe^x\,dx=1.
\]
Explain why this integral is not a direct reverse-chain-rule integral.


9.9.4 STEP-style Question 4
Let
\[
I_n=\int_0^\pi x\sin(nx)\,dx,
\]
where \(n\) is a positive integer.
Find \(I_n\) in terms of \(n\).


9.9.5 STEP-style Question 5
The region under
\[
y=\sqrt{x}
\]
from \(x=0\) to \(x=a\) is rotated completely about the \(x\)-axis.
Show that the volume is
\[
V=\dfrac{\pi a^2}{2}.
\]
Hence find \(a\) if the volume is \(8\pi\).

title: 9  Further calculus
text:
9.10 Reflection questions

Why is the derivative of \(\tan^{-1}x\) connected to \(\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\)?
Why does \(\int \dfrac{1}{x^2+a^2}\,dx\) produce an inverse tangent?
How can you recognise the structure \(\dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)}\)?
Why must every part of an integral be rewritten when using substitution?
Why are partial fractions useful before integrating rational functions?
Why is integration by parts described as the reverse of the product rule?
Why is graphing integration by parts less direct than graphing area or volume?
How does the formula \(V=\pi\int y^2\,dx\) connect a two-dimensional region to a three-dimensional solid?
What can R show clearly about areas and volumes that algebra alone may hide?
What can exact calculus prove that numerical graphing can only suggest?

title: 9  Further calculus
text:
9.11 Answers

9.11.1 Answers to Exercises 8.1

9.11.1.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{3}{1+9x^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2}{1+(2x-1)^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2}{x^2+4}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{1}{1+(1-x)^2}\).



9.11.1.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\tan^{-1}x+\dfrac{x}{1+x^2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{\dfrac{x}{1+x^2}-\tan^{-1}x}{x^2}\).
The tangent is \(y=2x\).
The gradient is \(1\).
For \(x>0\), \[
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)\right)
=
\dfrac{-\dfrac{1}{x^2}}{1+\dfrac{1}{x^2}}
=
-\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}.
\]



9.11.1.3 Gold

The tangent is \[
y-\dfrac{\pi}{4}=\dfrac{1}{2}(x-1).
\]
The derivative is zero for \(x>0\), so the expression is constant. Substituting \(x=1\) gives the constant \(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{x^2}{1+x^2}\), so the only stationary point is \(x=0\). The derivative is non-negative on both sides, so it is not a maximum or minimum.
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2(1-3x^2)}{(1+9x^2)(1+x^2)}\).
The maximum occurs at \(x=1\), and its value is \(\dfrac{\pi}{4}-\dfrac{1}{2}\).




9.11.2 Answers to Exercises 8.2

9.11.2.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)+C\).
\(\dfrac{1}{3}\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{3}\right)+C\).
\(\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{3}\right)+C\).
\(\tan^{-1}(2x)+C\).

\(k=3\).

9.11.2.3 Gold

\(2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)\).
\(a=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt3}\).
\(\tan^{-1}(x+1)+C\).
Since \[
\int \dfrac{1}{4x^2+1}\,dx=\dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}(2x)+C,
\] the result follows by substituting the limits.

\int \dfrac{2x}{(1+x^2)^2}\,dx=-\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}+C,
\] the definite integral is \(1-\dfrac{1}{1+a^2}=\dfrac{a^2}{1+a^2}\).

9.11.7.1 Bronze

9.11.7.2 Silver

9.11.8 Answers to STEP-style extension questions

Let \(y=\tan^{-1}x\). Then \(\tan y=x\). Differentiating implicitly gives \[
\sec^2y\dfrac{dy}{dx}=1.
\] Hence \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{1}{\sec^2y}.
\] Since \(\sec^2y=1+\tan^2y=1+x^2\), we get \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}.
\]
Since \[
\int \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx=\tan^{-1}x+C,
\] we have \[
\int_0^1 \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx
=
\left[\tan^{-1}x\right]_0^1
=
\dfrac{\pi}{4}.
\] This is the exact area under \(y=\dfrac{1}{1+x^2}\) from \(0\) to \(1\).
Using integration by parts, \[
\int_0^1 xe^x\,dx
=
\left[xe^x\right]_0^1-\int_0^1 e^x\,dx
=
e-(e-1)=1.
\] It is not a direct reverse-chain-rule integral because \(x\) is not a constant multiple of the derivative of \(e^x\).
Using integration by parts, \[
I_n=\int_0^\pi x\sin(nx)\,dx
=
\left[-\dfrac{x\cos(nx)}{n}\right]_0^\pi
+
\dfrac{1}{n}\int_0^\pi \cos(nx)\,dx.
\] The second integral is zero. Therefore, \[
I_n=-\dfrac{\pi\cos(n\pi)}{n}
=
\dfrac{(-1)^{n+1}\pi}{n}.
\]
The volume is \[
V=\pi\int_0^a (\sqrt{x})^2\,dx
=
\pi\int_0^a x\,dx
=
\dfrac{\pi a^2}{2}.
\] If \(V=8\pi\), then \[
\dfrac{\pi a^2}{2}=8\pi.
\] Hence \(a^2=16\), and since \(a>0\), \(a=4\).

title: 10  Vectors
text:
10.1 Overview
This chapter develops six connected vector ideas:
The central aim is to understand the meaning of a vector equation of a line and to use scalar products for problem solving. We do not need equations of planes in this chapter.

title: 10  Vectors
10  Vectors
text:
10.2 9.1 Position vectors and displacement vectors

10.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
A position vector gives the position of a point relative to the origin.
If
\[
\overrightarrow{OA}=\mathbf{a}
\]
and
\[
\overrightarrow{OB}=\mathbf{b},
\]
then
\[
\overrightarrow{AB}=\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}.
\]
This is one of the most important ideas in the chapter: to move from \(A\) to \(B\), subtract the position vector of \(A\) from the position vector of \(B\).
For a vector
\[
\mathbf{a}=a_1\mathbf{i}+a_2\mathbf{j}+a_3\mathbf{k},
\]
its magnitude is
\[
|\mathbf{a}|=\sqrt{a_1^2+a_2^2+a_3^2}.
\]
A unit vector in the direction of a non-zero vector \(\mathbf{a}\) is
\[
\dfrac{\mathbf{a}}{|\mathbf{a}|}.
\]


10.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
points_df <- data.frame(
  label = c("O", "A", "B"),
  x = c(0, 2, 5),
  y = c(0, 1, 4)
)

arrows_df <- vector_arrows_2d(
  labels = c("OA", "OB", "AB"),
  starts = matrix(c(0, 0, 0, 0, 2, 1), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE),
  ends = matrix(c(2, 1, 5, 4, 5, 4), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE)
)

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = arrows_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend, linetype = label),
    linewidth = 1.1,
    arrow = arrow_style()
  ) +
  geom_point(data = points_df, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = points_df, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_y = 0.25) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title = "Position and displacement vectors",
    subtitle = "The displacement vector AB is obtained by b - a.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Vector"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 10.1: Position vectors and the displacement vector from A to B.







10.2.3 Worked example
Let
\[
A(2,-1,3)
\]
and
\[
B(-1,5,4).
\]
Find \(\overrightarrow{AB}\) and \(|\overrightarrow{AB}|\).
The position vectors are
\[
\overrightarrow{OA}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}
\]
and
\[
\overrightarrow{OB}=\begin{pmatrix}-1\\5\\4\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\overrightarrow{AB}
=
\overrightarrow{OB}-\overrightarrow{OA}
=
\begin{pmatrix}-1\\5\\4\end{pmatrix}
-
\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}-3\\6\\1\end{pmatrix}.
\]
So
\[
|\overrightarrow{AB}|
=
\sqrt{(-3)^2+6^2+1^2}
=
\sqrt{46}.
\]


10.2.4 Short investigation
Change the coordinates of \(A\) and \(B\) below and observe how \(\overrightarrow{AB}\) changes.

Code
A <- c(2, -1, 3)
B <- c(-1, 5, 4)

AB <- B - A

data.frame(
  component = c("x", "y", "z"),
  A = A,
  B = B,
  AB = AB
)


Code
vector_magnitude(AB)




Table 10.1



  component  A  B AB
1         x  2 -1 -3
2         y -1  5  6
3         z  3  4  1


[1] 6.78233






10.2.5 Exercises 9.1

10.2.5.1 Bronze

If \(A(3,-2)\) and \(B(5,4)\), find \(\overrightarrow{AB}\).
Find the magnitude of \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
If \(A(-1,4,0)\) and \(B(2,1,5)\), find \(\overrightarrow{AB}\).
If \(\overrightarrow{OA}=\mathbf{a}\) and \(\overrightarrow{OB}=\mathbf{b}\), write down \(\overrightarrow{BA}\).
Find the midpoint of \(A(1,2,3)\) and \(B(5,0,-1)\).



10.2.5.2 Silver

If \(A(2,-1,3)\) and \(B(-1,5,4)\), find \(\overrightarrow{AB}\) and \(|\overrightarrow{AB}|\).
If \(\overrightarrow{AB}=\begin{pmatrix}4\\-2\\6\end{pmatrix}\) and \(A(1,3,-2)\), find \(B\).
Find a unit vector in the direction of \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
A point \(P\) divides \(AB\) in the ratio \(AP:PB=1:2\). If \(A(1,0,2)\) and \(B(4,6,-1)\), find \(P\).
Show that \(A(1,2,3)\), \(B(3,0,5)\) and \(C(5,-2,7)\) are collinear.



10.2.5.3 Gold

Show that \(P(3,-1,5)\) is the midpoint of \(A(1,0,2)\) and \(B(5,-2,8)\).
Find \(k\) if \(\left|\begin{pmatrix}k\\2\\1\end{pmatrix}\right|=3\).
Points \(C(1,-1,-3)\), \(A(2,1,-1)\) and \(B(4,5,3)\) are collinear. Find the ratio \(CA:AB\).
Find \(x\) if \(A(1,2,3)\), \(B(4,8,12)\) and \(C(x,4,6)\) are collinear.
Find the distance between \(A(-1,4,2)\) and \(B(3,-2,5)\).

title: 10  Vectors
text:
10.3 9.2 Vector equations of straight lines

10.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
A straight line is determined by:

one point on the line;
one direction vector.

If a line passes through the point with position vector \(\mathbf{a}\) and has direction vector \(\mathbf{d}\), then its vector equation is
\[
\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{d},
\]
where \(t\) is a real parameter.
Each value of \(t\) gives a point on the line.
If the line passes through points with position vectors \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{b}\), then a direction vector is
\[
\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}.
\]
So the line can be written as
\[
\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+t(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}).
\]
The direction vector is not unique. Any non-zero scalar multiple of a direction vector describes the same direction.


10.3.2 Visualisation using R


Code
a <- c(1, 2)
d <- c(2, -1)

line_df <- make_line_2d(a, d, t_min = -2, t_max = 3)
sample_points <- points_on_line_2d(a, d, t_values = c(-1, 0, 1, 2))

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = line_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = sample_points, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = sample_points, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_y = 0.35) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = a[1], y = a[2], xend = a[1] + d[1], yend = a[2] + d[2]),
    linewidth = 1.1,
    arrow = arrow_style()
  ) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title = "Vector equation of a line",
    subtitle = "The parameter t moves the point along the line.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 10.2: The line r = a + td in a two-dimensional projection.







10.3.3 Worked example
Find the vector equation of the line through
\[
A(1,2,-1)
\]
and
\[
B(5,-2,3).
\]
A direction vector is
\[
\overrightarrow{AB}
=
\begin{pmatrix}5\\-2\\3\end{pmatrix}
-
\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-1\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}4\\-4\\4\end{pmatrix}.
\]
We may divide this direction vector by \(4\) and use
\[
\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\\1\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Therefore, one vector equation of the line is
\[
\mathbf{r}
=
\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-1\end{pmatrix}
+t\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\\1\end{pmatrix}.
\]


10.3.4 Short investigation
The table below shows how changing \(t\) moves along the line.

Code
t_values <- -2:4

data.frame(
  t = t_values,
  x = 1 + t_values,
  y = 2 - t_values,
  z = -1 + t_values
)




Table 10.2



   t  x  y  z
1 -2 -1  4 -3
2 -1  0  3 -2
3  0  1  2 -1
4  1  2  1  0
5  2  3  0  1
6  3  4 -1  2
7  4  5 -2  3






10.3.5 Exercises 9.2

10.3.5.1 Bronze

Write down a vector equation of the line through \((1,2,3)\) with direction vector \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\4\end{pmatrix}\).
Find a vector equation of the line through \(A(1,0,2)\) and \(B(3,4,6)\).
Show that \(P(5,-2,11)\) lies on the line \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-2\\4\end{pmatrix}\).
Determine whether \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-4\\6\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}-1\\2\\-3\end{pmatrix}\) are parallel.
Give another vector equation for the same line as \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}\).



10.3.5.2 Silver

Find a vector equation of the line through \(A(-1,2,4)\) and \(B(5,-1,1)\).
Find \(k\) if \(P(k,7,3)\) lies on the line \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\3\\5\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\4\\-2\end{pmatrix}\).
Determine whether the lines \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\\2\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\-1\\5\end{pmatrix}+s\begin{pmatrix}4\\-2\\6\end{pmatrix}\) are the same line.
Find the value of \(\lambda\) for which \((7,-1,10)\) lies on \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-2\end{pmatrix}+\lambda\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\4\end{pmatrix}\).
Show that \(A(2,-1,0)\), \(B(5,5,9)\) and \(C(1,-3,-3)\) are collinear.



10.3.5.3 Gold

Find \(k\) if \(A(1,2,3)\), \(B(4,k,9)\) and \(C(7,8,15)\) are collinear.
The line passes through \(A(2,1,-1)\) and \(B(5,-2,8)\). Show that \(P(8,-5,17)\) lies on the line.
Prove that if \(\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{d}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+s\mathbf{d}\) represent the same point, and \(\mathbf{d}\neq\mathbf{0}\), then \(t=s\).
If \(P=A+\lambda(B-A)\) divides \(AB\) in the ratio \(AP:PB=2:3\), find \(\lambda\).
Show that \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\0\\1\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}1\\-2\\3\end{pmatrix}\) is the same line as the line through \(P(-1,6,-8)\) and \(Q(2,0,1)\).

title: 10  Vectors
text:
10.4 9.3 The scalar product and angles

10.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
The scalar product, or dot product, of two vectors is defined by
\[
\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=|\mathbf{a}|\,|\mathbf{b}|\cos\theta,
\]
where \(\theta\) is the angle between the two vectors.
If
\[
\mathbf{a}=a_1\mathbf{i}+a_2\mathbf{j}+a_3\mathbf{k}
\]
and
\[
\mathbf{b}=b_1\mathbf{i}+b_2\mathbf{j}+b_3\mathbf{k},
\]
then
\[
\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=a_1b_1+a_2b_2+a_3b_3.
\]
Combining the two formulae gives
\[
\cos\theta=
\dfrac{\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}|\,|\mathbf{b}|}.
\]
Two non-zero vectors are perpendicular when
\[
\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=0.
\]


10.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
a_vector <- c(3, 1)
b_vector <- c(1, 3)

vectors_df <- vector_arrows_2d(
  labels = c("a", "b"),
  starts = matrix(c(0, 0, 0, 0), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE),
  ends = rbind(a_vector, b_vector)
)

arc_df <- arc_between_vectors(a_vector, b_vector, radius = 0.8)

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = vectors_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend, linetype = label),
    linewidth = 1.1,
    arrow = arrow_style()
  ) +
  geom_path(data = arc_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.9, y = 0.55, label = "theta") +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-0.5, 3.5), ylim = c(-0.5, 3.5)) +
  labs(
    title = "Angle between vectors",
    subtitle = "The scalar product connects algebraic components with the angle between vectors.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Vector"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 10.3: The angle between two vectors in a two-dimensional projection.







10.4.3 Worked example
Find the angle between
\[
\mathbf{a}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}
\]
and
\[
\mathbf{b}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}.
\]
First compute the scalar product:
\[
\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=2(1)+(-1)(2)+2(2)=4.
\]
Also,
\[
|\mathbf{a}|=\sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+2^2}=3
\]
and
\[
|\mathbf{b}|=\sqrt{1^2+2^2+2^2}=3.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\cos\theta=\dfrac{4}{3\cdot3}=\dfrac{4}{9}.
\]
So
\[
\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{9}\right)
\approx63.6^\circ.
\]


10.4.4 Short investigation
Use R to check angles between several vectors.

Code
vectors <- data.frame(
  pair = c("Pair 1", "Pair 2", "Pair 3"),
  ax = c(2, 1, 1),
  ay = c(-1, 2, 1),
  az = c(2, 2, 0),
  bx = c(1, 2, 1),
  by = c(2, 1, -1),
  bz = c(2, 2, 0)
)

vectors$dot_product <- vectors$ax * vectors$bx + vectors$ay * vectors$by + vectors$az * vectors$bz
vectors$magnitude_a <- sqrt(vectors$ax^2 + vectors$ay^2 + vectors$az^2)
vectors$magnitude_b <- sqrt(vectors$bx^2 + vectors$by^2 + vectors$bz^2)
vectors$angle_degrees <- acos(vectors$dot_product / (vectors$magnitude_a * vectors$magnitude_b)) * 180 / pi

vectors




Table 10.3



    pair ax ay az bx by bz dot_product magnitude_a magnitude_b angle_degrees
1 Pair 1  2 -1  2  1  2  2           4    3.000000    3.000000      63.61220
2 Pair 2  1  2  2  2  1  2           8    3.000000    3.000000      27.26604
3 Pair 3  1  1  0  1 -1  0           0    1.414214    1.414214      90.00000






10.4.5 Exercises 9.3

10.4.5.1 Bronze

Find the magnitude of \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}\).

10.4.5.3 Gold

Find the acute angle between \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}3\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
Find \(k\) if \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\k\\2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\1\end{pmatrix}\) are perpendicular.
Find \(k\) if \(\begin{pmatrix}k\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\k\\1\end{pmatrix}\) are perpendicular.
Starting from \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=|\mathbf{a}|\,|\mathbf{b}|\cos\theta\), derive the formula for \(\cos\theta\).
Find angle \(BAC\) for \(A(1,0,0)\), \(B(3,1,2)\) and \(C(0,2,1)\).

title: 10  Vectors
text:
10.5 9.4 Angles between straight lines

10.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
The angle between two straight lines is the angle between their direction vectors.
If the lines have direction vectors \(\mathbf{d}_1\) and \(\mathbf{d}_2\), then
\[
\cos\theta=
\dfrac{\mathbf{d}_1\cdot\mathbf{d}_2}{|\mathbf{d}_1|\,|\mathbf{d}_2|}.
\]
If the question asks for the acute angle between two lines, use
\[
\cos\theta=
\dfrac{|\mathbf{d}_1\cdot\mathbf{d}_2|}{|\mathbf{d}_1|\,|\mathbf{d}_2|}.
\]
Two lines are parallel if their direction vectors are scalar multiples.
Two lines are perpendicular if their direction vectors have scalar product zero.


10.5.2 Visualisation using R


Code
line_one <- make_line_2d(c(0, 0), c(2, 1), t_min = -2, t_max = 2)
line_two <- make_line_2d(c(0, 0), c(2, -3), t_min = -2, t_max = 2)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = line_one, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = "line 1"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(data = line_two, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = "line 2"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title = "Angle between lines",
    subtitle = "In vector work, use the direction vectors rather than trying to read the angle from the graph.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Line"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 10.4: The angle between two lines is the angle between their direction vectors.







10.5.3 Worked example
Find the acute angle between the lines with direction vectors
\[
\mathbf{d}_1=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-2\end{pmatrix}
\]
and
\[
\mathbf{d}_2=\begin{pmatrix}3\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}.
\]
The scalar product is
\[
\mathbf{d}_1\cdot\mathbf{d}_2=1(3)+2(-1)+(-2)(2)=-3.
\]
The magnitudes are
\[
|\mathbf{d}_1|=\sqrt{1^2+2^2+(-2)^2}=3
\]
and
\[
|\mathbf{d}_2|=\sqrt{3^2+(-1)^2+2^2}=\sqrt{14}.
\]
For the acute angle,
\[
\cos\theta=
\dfrac{|-3|}{3\sqrt{14}}=
\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{14}}.
\]
Hence
\[
\theta\approx74.5^\circ.
\]


10.5.4 Short investigation
Use the direction vectors to compare angles between lines.

Code
line_pairs <- data.frame(
  pair = c("A", "B", "C"),
  d1x = c(1, 1, 3),
  d1y = c(2, 2, -2),
  d1z = c(-2, 2, 6),
  d2x = c(3, 2, 2),
  d2y = c(-1, -1, 4),
  d2z = c(2, 0, 4)
)

line_pairs$dot_product <- line_pairs$d1x * line_pairs$d2x + line_pairs$d1y * line_pairs$d2y + line_pairs$d1z * line_pairs$d2z
line_pairs$magnitude_1 <- sqrt(line_pairs$d1x^2 + line_pairs$d1y^2 + line_pairs$d1z^2)
line_pairs$magnitude_2 <- sqrt(line_pairs$d2x^2 + line_pairs$d2y^2 + line_pairs$d2z^2)
line_pairs$acute_angle_degrees <- acos(abs(line_pairs$dot_product) / (line_pairs$magnitude_1 * line_pairs$magnitude_2)) * 180 / pi

line_pairs




Table 10.4



  pair d1x d1y d1z d2x d2y d2z dot_product magnitude_1 magnitude_2
1    A   1   2  -2   3  -1   2          -3           3    3.741657
2    B   1   2   2   2  -1   0           0           3    2.236068
3    C   3  -2   6   2   4   4          22           7    6.000000
  acute_angle_degrees
1            74.49864
2            90.00000
3            58.41186






10.5.5 Exercises 9.4

10.5.5.1 Bronze

Find the angle between lines with direction vectors \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
Determine whether lines with direction vectors \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-1\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\4\\-2\end{pmatrix}\) are parallel.
Determine whether lines with direction vectors \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\\0\end{pmatrix}\) are perpendicular.
Find the angle between lines with direction vectors \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\0\end{pmatrix}\).
State the direction vector of \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}3\\-1\\4\end{pmatrix}\).



10.5.5.2 Silver

Find the acute angle between \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\\2\end{pmatrix}+\lambda\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}0\\1\\1\end{pmatrix}+\mu\begin{pmatrix}3\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
Find the angle between the line with direction vector \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\2\end{pmatrix}\) and the vector \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\\0\end{pmatrix}\).
Find \(k\) if the line with direction vector \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\k\\2\end{pmatrix}\) is perpendicular to the line with direction vector \(\begin{pmatrix}3\\-2\\1\end{pmatrix}\).
Find \(k\) if \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\k\\1\end{pmatrix}\) is parallel to \(\begin{pmatrix}6\\9\\3\end{pmatrix}\).
Find the angle between lines with direction vectors \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}-1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}\).



10.5.5.3 Gold

Show that lines with direction vectors \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\2\end{pmatrix}\) are perpendicular.
Find \(k>0\) if the angle between \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\k\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\) is \(60^\circ\).
Explain why direction vectors can be used to find the angle between two lines even if the lines do not intersect.
Find the angle between \(AB\) and \(CD\), where \(A(1,2,0)\), \(B(4,0,6)\), \(C(-1,1,2)\) and \(D(1,5,6)\).
Find the acute angle between the line through \((1,2,3)\) and \((4,2,0)\) and the line through \((0,0,0)\) and \((2,1,2)\).

title: 10  Vectors
text:
10.6 9.5 Intersections of straight lines

10.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
To find the point of intersection of two vector lines, write out the parametric equations and solve them simultaneously.
For example, if
\[
\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+\lambda\mathbf{d}
\]
and
\[
\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{b}+\mu\mathbf{e},
\]
then an intersection point must satisfy
\[
\mathbf{a}+\lambda\mathbf{d}=\mathbf{b}+\mu\mathbf{e}.
\]
This gives simultaneous equations in \(\lambda\) and \(\mu\).
In three dimensions, two lines may be:

intersecting;
parallel;
the same line;
skew.

Skew lines are not parallel but do not intersect.


10.6.2 Visualisation using R


Code
line_1 <- make_line_2d(c(1, 2), c(2, -1), t_min = -1, t_max = 3)
line_2 <- make_line_2d(c(5, 0), c(-1, 1), t_min = -2, t_max = 2)

intersection <- data.frame(
  x = 5,
  y = 0,
  label = "intersection"
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = line_1, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = "line 1"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_line(data = line_2, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = "line 2"), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = intersection, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = intersection, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_y = 0.35) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title = "Intersecting vector lines",
    subtitle = "The actual calculation comes from solving the parametric equations.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Line"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 10.5: A projection of two intersecting vector lines.







10.6.3 Worked example
Find the intersection point of
\[
\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}
+\lambda\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}
\]
and
\[
\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}5\\0\\6\end{pmatrix}
+\mu\begin{pmatrix}-1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Equating components gives
\[
1+2\lambda=5-\mu,
\]
\[
2-\lambda=\mu,
\]
and
\[
3\lambda=6.
\]
From the third equation,
\[
\lambda=2.
\]
Then the second equation gives
\[
\mu=2-2=0.
\]
The first equation is also satisfied:
\[
1+2(2)=5-0.
\]
Therefore, the lines intersect at
\[
\begin{pmatrix}5\\0\\6\end{pmatrix}.
\]


10.6.4 Short investigation
Use R to check whether the component equations agree.

Code
lambda <- 2
mu <- 0

line_1_point <- c(1, 2, 0) + lambda * c(2, -1, 3)
line_2_point <- c(5, 0, 6) + mu * c(-1, 1, 0)

data.frame(
  component = c("x", "y", "z"),
  line_1 = line_1_point,
  line_2 = line_2_point
)




Table 10.5



  component line_1 line_2
1         x      5      5
2         y      0      0
3         z      6      6






10.6.5 Exercises 9.5

10.6.5.1 Bronze

Find the point on \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\) when \(t=3\).
Show that \((4,-1,6)\) lies on \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
Find the intersection of \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}5\\2\end{pmatrix}+s\begin{pmatrix}-1\\0\end{pmatrix}\).
Determine whether direction vectors \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\4\\6\end{pmatrix}\) are parallel.
In three dimensions, must two non-parallel lines always intersect?



10.6.5.2 Silver

Find the intersection of \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+\lambda\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}5\\0\\6\end{pmatrix}+\mu\begin{pmatrix}-1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}\).
Show that \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{pmatrix}+s\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-1\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\0\\1\end{pmatrix}\) are skew.
Determine whether \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\\1\end{pmatrix}+\lambda\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\1\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\4\\3\end{pmatrix}+\mu\begin{pmatrix}2\\4\\2\end{pmatrix}\) are the same line.
Find \(k\) if \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}0\\0\\k\end{pmatrix}+s\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\1\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\5\\-1\end{pmatrix}\) intersect.
Find the intersection of \(\mathbf{r}=\lambda\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}+\mu\begin{pmatrix}-2\\1\\3\end{pmatrix}\).



10.6.5.3 Gold

Find \(k\) if \(P(k,4,7)\) lies on \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
Determine whether the lines \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}+s\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\0\\2\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\\1\end{pmatrix}\) intersect.
Explain why inconsistent simultaneous equations imply that two non-parallel lines in three dimensions are skew.
Find the vector equation of the line through the intersection point of \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+\lambda\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}5\\0\\6\end{pmatrix}+\mu\begin{pmatrix}-1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}\), and through the point \((6,2,7)\).
For the two original lines used in Gold Question 4, find the intersection point and the acute angle between them.

title: 10  Vectors
section: 10.7 9.6 Closest points and distances from points to lines
text:
10.7 9.6 Closest points and distances from points to lines

10.7.1 Key mathematical ideas
The scalar product is very useful for finding the closest point from a point to a line.
Let the line be
\[
\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{d},
\]
and let \(P\) have position vector \(\mathbf{p}\).
A general point on the line is
\[
\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{d}.
\]
At the closest point, the vector from the point on the line to \(P\) is perpendicular to the direction vector of the line. Therefore,
\[
(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{x})\cdot\mathbf{d}=0.
\]
Substituting \(\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{d}\) gives
\[
(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a}-t\mathbf{d})\cdot\mathbf{d}=0.
\]
So
\[
t=\dfrac{(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}}{\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d}}.
\]


10.7.2 Visualisation using R


Code
line_point <- c(1, 2)
direction <- c(2, -1)
point_p <- c(4, 0)

line_df <- make_line_2d(line_point, direction, t_min = -1, t_max = 3)
foot <- closest_point_on_line(point_p, line_point, direction)

points_df <- data.frame(
  label = c("P", "foot"),
  x = c(point_p[1], foot[1]),
  y = c(point_p[2], foot[2])
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = line_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = point_p[1], y = point_p[2], xend = foot[1], yend = foot[2]),
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 1.1
  ) +
  geom_point(data = points_df, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = points_df, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_y = 0.35) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title = "Closest point to a line",
    subtitle = "The shortest segment is perpendicular to the line.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 10.6: Closest point from a point to a line in a two-dimensional projection.







10.7.3 Worked example
Find the closest point from
\[
P(4,0,5)
\]
to the line
\[
\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Here,
\[
\mathbf{a}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix},
\quad
\mathbf{d}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix},
\quad
\mathbf{p}=\begin{pmatrix}4\\0\\5\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Using
\[
t=\dfrac{(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}}{\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d}},
\]
we get
\[
\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\-2\\5\end{pmatrix}.
\]
So
\[
(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}=3(2)+(-2)(-1)+5(2)=18,
\]
and
\[
\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d}=2^2+(-1)^2+2^2=9.
\]
Hence
\[
t=\dfrac{18}{9}=2.
\]
The closest point is
\[
\mathbf{x}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+2\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}5\\0\\4\end{pmatrix}.
\]
The distance from \(P\) to the line is
\[
\left|\begin{pmatrix}4\\0\\5\end{pmatrix}-\begin{pmatrix}5\\0\\4\end{pmatrix}\right|
=
\left|\begin{pmatrix}-1\\0\\1\end{pmatrix}\right|
=
\sqrt2.
\]


10.7.4 Short investigation
Use R to check the perpendicular condition.

Code
line_point <- c(1, 2, 0)
direction <- c(2, -1, 2)
point_p <- c(4, 0, 5)

foot <- closest_point_on_line(point_p, line_point, direction)
perpendicular_vector <- point_p - foot

data.frame(
  component = c("x", "y", "z"),
  foot = foot,
  perpendicular_vector = perpendicular_vector,
  direction_vector = direction
)


Code
scalar_product(perpendicular_vector, direction)




Table 10.6



  component foot perpendicular_vector direction_vector
1         x    5                   -1                2
2         y    0                    0               -1
3         z    4                    1                2


[1] 0




The final value is \(0\), confirming that the shortest segment is perpendicular to the line.


10.7.5 Exercises 9.6

10.7.5.1 Bronze

Find the closest point from \(P(3,4,0)\) to the line \(\mathbf{r}=t\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\\0\end{pmatrix}\).
Find the closest point from \(P(4,5,2)\) to the line \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}0\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}\).
If \(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\-2\\5\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{d}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\), find \(\dfrac{(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}}{\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d}}\).
Show that \(\begin{pmatrix}-1\\0\\1\end{pmatrix}\) is perpendicular to \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
Find the distance from \(P(4,5,2)\) to the line in Question 2.



10.7.5.2 Silver

Find the closest point from \(P(4,0,5)\) to \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).

Find the shortest distance from the origin to \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
Find the distance from \(P(1,2,3)\) to the line through \(A(1,0,0)\) and \(B(1,4,0)\).
For the line \(\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{d}\) and point \(P\) with position vector \(\mathbf{p}\), explain why the closest point satisfies \((\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a}-t\mathbf{d})\cdot\mathbf{d}=0\).



10.7.5.3 Gold

Prove that the closest point parameter is \[
t=\dfrac{(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}}{\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d}}.
\]
Find the closest point and distance from \(P(4,0,5)\) to \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
Find the closest point from \(P(0,3,5)\) to \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}\) and find the distance.
Find the point on \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\\2\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\-2\end{pmatrix}\) whose position vector is perpendicular to the direction vector of the line.
Show that the minimum value of \(|\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a}-t\mathbf{d}|^2\) occurs when \(t=\dfrac{(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}}{\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d}}\).

title: 10  Vectors
text:
10.8 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require proof, structure or exact reasoning.

10.8.1 STEP-style Question 1
Let \(A\) and \(B\) have position vectors \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{b}\), where \(\mathbf{a}\neq\mathbf{b}\).
Prove that every point on the line through \(A\) and \(B\) can be written in the form
\[
\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+t(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}).
\]
Explain the meaning of the cases \(t=0\), \(t=1\) and \(0<t<1\).


10.8.2 STEP-style Question 2
Let
\[
\mathbf{a}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-2\end{pmatrix}
\]
and
\[
\mathbf{b}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Find the acute angle between \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{b}\) exactly in terms of an inverse cosine, then give the angle to one decimal place.


10.8.3 STEP-style Question 3
The lines
\[
\ell_1:\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+\lambda\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}
\]
and
\[
\ell_2:\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}5\\0\\6\end{pmatrix}+\mu\begin{pmatrix}-1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}
\]
intersect. Find their point of intersection and the acute angle between them.


10.8.4 STEP-style Question 4
Let
\[
\ell:\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{d}
\]
and let \(P\) have position vector \(\mathbf{p}\).
Prove that the point on \(\ell\) closest to \(P\) has parameter
\[
t=\dfrac{(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}}{\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d}}.
\]
Then apply the formula to find the closest point from \(P(4,0,5)\) to
\[
\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}.
\]


10.8.5 STEP-style Question 5
Find \(k\) such that the two lines
\[
\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}0\\0\\k\end{pmatrix}+s\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\1\end{pmatrix}
\]
and
\[
\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\5\\-1\end{pmatrix}
\]
intersect. Then find the point of intersection.

title: 10  Vectors
text:
10.9 Reflection questions

Why is \(\overrightarrow{AB}=\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}\) rather than \(\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}\)?
What does the parameter \(t\) mean in \(\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+t\mathbf{d}\)?
Why is the direction vector of a line not unique?
How does the scalar product connect algebra and geometry?
Why does \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=0\) mean perpendicular vectors?
Why can two non-parallel lines in three dimensions fail to intersect?
Why does solving line intersections require all components to agree?
Why is the shortest distance from a point to a line perpendicular to the line?
What can R show clearly about vector lines that algebra alone may hide?
What can exact vector reasoning prove that a diagram can only suggest?

title: 10  Vectors
text:
10.10 Answers

10.10.1 Answers to Exercises 9.1

10.10.1.1 Bronze

\(\overrightarrow{AB}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\6\end{pmatrix}\).
\(3\).
\(\overrightarrow{AB}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\-3\\5\end{pmatrix}\).
\(\overrightarrow{BA}=\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}\).
\((3,1,1)\).



10.10.1.2 Silver

\(\overrightarrow{AB}=\begin{pmatrix}-3\\6\\1\end{pmatrix}\) and \(|\overrightarrow{AB}|=\sqrt{46}\).
\(B(5,1,4)\).
\(\dfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
\(P(2,2,1)\).
\(\overrightarrow{AB}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\-2\\2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\overrightarrow{BC}=\begin{pmatrix}2\\-2\\2\end{pmatrix}\), so the points are collinear.



10.10.1.3 Gold

The midpoint of \(A\) and \(B\) is \(\left(\dfrac{1+5}{2},\dfrac{0-2}{2},\dfrac{2+8}{2}\right)=(3,-1,5)\).
\(k=\pm2\).
\(CA:AB=1:2\).
\(x=2\).
\(\sqrt{61}\).




10.10.2 Answers to Exercises 9.2

10.10.2.1 Bronze

\(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\4\end{pmatrix}\).
\(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\\2\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{pmatrix}\).
When \(t=2\), the line gives \(\begin{pmatrix}5\\-2\\11\end{pmatrix}\), so \(P\) lies on the line.
Yes. The second vector is \(-\dfrac{1}{2}\) times the first.
For example, \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}5\\-1\\6\end{pmatrix}+s\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}\).



10.10.2.2 Silver

\(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}-1\\2\\4\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\-1\end{pmatrix}\).
\(k=3\).
They are the same line.
\(\lambda=3\).
\(\overrightarrow{AB}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\6\\9\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\overrightarrow{AC}=\begin{pmatrix}-1\\-2\\-3\end{pmatrix}\), so the points are collinear.



10.10.2.3 Gold

\(k=5\).
\(\overrightarrow{AB}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\-3\\9\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\overrightarrow{AP}=\begin{pmatrix}6\\-6\\18\end{pmatrix}=2\overrightarrow{AB}\), so \(P\) lies on the line.
If the two expressions represent the same point, then \((t-s)\mathbf{d}=\mathbf{0}\). Since \(\mathbf{d}\neq\mathbf{0}\), \(t-s=0\), so \(t=s\).
\(\lambda=\dfrac{2}{5}\).
\(\overrightarrow{PQ}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\-6\\9\end{pmatrix}=3\begin{pmatrix}1\\-2\\3\end{pmatrix}\) and \(Q\) lies on the given line, so the two descriptions give the same line.




10.10.3 Answers to Exercises 9.3

10.10.3.1 Bronze

\(2\).
\(3\).
\(90^\circ\).
Yes, since the scalar product is \(0\).
\(\dfrac{8}{9}\).



10.10.3.2 Silver

\(\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{4}{9}\right)\approx63.6^\circ\).
\(k=\dfrac{5}{2}\).
\(k=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt3}\).
Their scalar product is \(4-4+0=0\), so they are perpendicular.
\(4\sqrt2\).



10.10.3.3 Gold

\(\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{14}}\right)\approx74.5^\circ\).
\(k=4\).
\(k=-\dfrac{1}{2}\).
Dividing both sides by \(|\mathbf{a}|\,|\mathbf{b}|\) gives \(\cos\theta=\dfrac{\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}|\,|\mathbf{b}|}\).
\(\cos\angle BAC=\dfrac{2}{3\sqrt6}\), so \(\angle BAC\approx74.2^\circ\).




10.10.4 Answers to Exercises 9.4

10.10.4.1 Bronze

\(\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{8}{9}\right)\approx27.3^\circ\).
Yes, they are parallel.
Yes, they are perpendicular.
\(90^\circ\).
\(\begin{pmatrix}3\\-1\\4\end{pmatrix}\).



10.10.4.2 Silver

\(\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{14}}\right)\approx74.5^\circ\).
\(\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)\approx48.2^\circ\).
\(k=\dfrac{5}{2}\).
\(k=3\).
\(\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{7}{9}\right)\approx38.9^\circ\).



10.10.4.3 Gold

Their scalar product is \(2+2-4=0\), so the lines are perpendicular.
\(k=\dfrac{3\sqrt{35}}{7}\).
Direction vectors describe orientation. The angle between two lines is defined as the angle between their directions, even if the lines are skew and do not meet.
\(\cos\theta=\dfrac{11}{21}\), so \(\theta\approx58.4^\circ\).
The direction vectors are \(\begin{pmatrix}3\\0\\-3\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\2\end{pmatrix}\). Their scalar product is \(0\), so the angle is \(90^\circ\).




10.10.5 Answers to Exercises 9.5

10.10.5.1 Bronze

\((4,-1,6)\).
When \(t=3\), the line gives \((4,-1,6)\), so the point lies on the line.
The intersection is \((5,2)\).
Yes, they are parallel.
No. In three dimensions, two non-parallel lines may be skew.



10.10.5.2 Silver

The intersection is \((5,0,6)\).
Solving the \(y\)-component gives \(s=-1\). The \(x\)-component then gives \(t=-\dfrac{3}{2}\), but the \(z\)-component is inconsistent. Therefore the lines are skew.
They are the same line.
\(k=2\).
The intersection is \((1,2,3)\).



10.10.5.3 Gold

\(k=5\).
The equations are inconsistent, so the lines do not intersect. Since their directions are not scalar multiples, they are skew.
If the direction vectors are not parallel, the lines cannot be parallel. If the simultaneous equations are inconsistent, there is no common point. Therefore the lines are skew.
The intersection point is \((5,0,6)\). The direction vector to \((6,2,7)\) is \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\1\end{pmatrix}\), so the line is \(\mathbf{r}=\begin{pmatrix}5\\0\\6\end{pmatrix}+t\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\1\end{pmatrix}\).
The intersection point is \((5,0,6)\). The direction vectors are \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}-1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}\), so \(\cos\theta=\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{28}}\). Hence \(\theta\approx55.5^\circ\).




10.10.6 Answers to Exercises 9.6

10.10.6.1 Bronze

\((3,0,0)\).
\((1,5,0)\).
\(2\).
The scalar product is \(-2+0+2=0\), so the vectors are perpendicular.
\(\sqrt{13}\).



10.10.6.2 Silver

The closest point is \((5,0,4)\).
The closest point is \(\left(\dfrac{5}{2},\dfrac{5}{2},1\right)\).
The shortest distance is \(\dfrac{\sqrt{65}}{3}\).
The distance is \(3\).
At the closest point, the vector from the point on the line to \(P\) is perpendicular to the line direction. Hence the scalar product with \(\mathbf{d}\) is \(0\).



10.10.6.3 Gold

At the closest point, \((\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a}-t\mathbf{d})\cdot\mathbf{d}=0\). Expanding gives \((\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}-t(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})=0\), so \(t=\dfrac{(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}}{\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d}}\).
The closest point is \((5,0,4)\) and the distance is \(\sqrt2\).
The closest point is \(\left(\dfrac{34}{9},\dfrac{23}{9},\dfrac{32}{9}\right)\) and the distance is \(\dfrac{\sqrt{149}}{3}\).
The required point is \(\left(\dfrac{13}{9},\dfrac{2}{9},\dfrac{14}{9}\right)\).
Expand \(|\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a}-t\mathbf{d}|^2\) as a quadratic in \(t\). Its derivative is \(-2(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}+2t(\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d})\). Setting this equal to zero gives the required value of \(t\).




10.10.7 Answers to STEP-style extension questions

A point on the line through \(A\) and \(B\) is obtained by starting at \(A\) and moving some scalar multiple of \(\overrightarrow{AB}\). Since \(\overrightarrow{AB}=\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a}\), every point has the form \(\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{a}+t(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a})\). When \(t=0\), the point is \(A\). When \(t=1\), the point is \(B\). When \(0<t<1\), the point lies between \(A\) and \(B\).
The scalar product is \(-3\), and the magnitudes are \(3\) and \(\sqrt{14}\). The acute angle satisfies \[
\cos\theta=\dfrac{3}{3\sqrt{14}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{14}}.
\] Therefore \[
\theta=\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{14}}\right)\approx74.5^\circ.
\]
Equating components gives \(\lambda=2\) and \(\mu=0\), so the intersection point is \((5,0,6)\). The direction vectors are \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}-1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}\). Hence \[
\cos\theta=\dfrac{|2(-1)+(-1)(1)+3(0)|}{\sqrt{14}\sqrt2}=\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{28}}.
\] Therefore \(\theta\approx55.5^\circ\).
At the closest point, the vector from the line to the point is perpendicular to the direction vector. Therefore \[
(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a}-t\mathbf{d})\cdot\mathbf{d}=0.
\] Solving gives \[
t=\dfrac{(\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}}{\mathbf{d}\cdot\mathbf{d}}.
\] For the given point and line, \(t=2\), so the closest point is \((5,0,4)\).
Equating components gives \[
s=1+2t,
\] \[
2s=2+5t,
\] and \[
k+s=3-t.
\] From the first two equations, \(t=0\) and \(s=1\). Substituting into the third equation gives \(k+1=3\), so \(k=2\). The point of intersection is \((1,2,3)\).

title: 11  Differential equations
text:
11.1 Overview
This chapter develops six connected differential-equation ideas:
The central technique in this chapter is separating the variables. We will not use integrating factors, second-order differential equations, or stochastic differential equations as solution techniques.

title: 11  Differential equations
11  Differential equations
section: 11.2 Inspiration: differential equations behind real-world data
text:
11.2 Inspiration: differential equations behind real-world data
Differential equations are not just symbolic exercises. They are one of the main languages used to describe change in the real world.
In this chapter, our technique is deliberately limited to separating the variables. However, the same basic idea sits underneath much more sophisticated models used in weather forecasting, finance, population modelling, epidemics, cooling, chemical reactions and environmental science.
The guiding question is:

If we know how fast something changes, can we predict what happens next?

Real applications usually begin with data. For example:

weather observations from New Zealand and Vietnam;
temperature, rainfall, wind and sunshine summaries;
exchange-rate data such as NZD/VND or USD/VND;
population or environmental indices;
school-based data such as attendance, enrolment, revision completion or assessment readiness.

The full real-world models may be complicated. But in this chapter we study simplified models that still capture the same central idea: a rate of change determines a future state.

11.2.1 From real-world systems to differential equations









Context
Real-world data students can recognise
More advanced differential-equation model
Separable model used in this chapter




Weather forecasting
Temperature, rainfall, wind speed, pressure
Coupled nonlinear partial differential equations from fluid dynamics and thermodynamics
\(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A)\)


Cooling and heating
Tea cooling in a room, classroom temperature, outdoor/indoor comparison
Heat equation or energy-balance models
\(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A)\)


Population growth
Population index, school roll, bacteria growth
Logistic or multi-compartment systems
\(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP\left(1-\dfrac{P}{M}\right)\)


Finance
Exchange rates, investment indices, price series
Stochastic differential equations with random noise
\(\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS\)


Epidemics
Number infected, recovered or susceptible
Coupled SIR-type differential equations
\(\dfrac{dI}{dt}=kI\) or \(\dfrac{dI}{dt}=kI(M-I)\)


Environmental recovery
Pollution level, rainfall deficit, concentration decay
Transport-diffusion-reaction equations
\(\dfrac{dC}{dt}=-kC\)



The important point is that our simple models are not the whole story. They are entry points into the same mathematical way of thinking.


11.2.2 Example 1: weather and climate
Modern weather forecasting does not use one simple equation. A full numerical weather model may involve velocity, pressure, temperature, moisture, radiation, surface effects and the rotation of the Earth.
A simplified momentum equation may contain terms such as
\[
\dfrac{D\mathbf{u}}{Dt}
+
f\mathbf{k}\times\mathbf{u}
=
-\dfrac{1}{\rho}\nabla p
+
\mathbf{g}
+
\nu\nabla^2\mathbf{u}.
\]
This is far beyond our course. It is a partial differential equation involving vectors, pressure gradients, rotation and diffusion.
But a simple classroom version might ask:

A hot surface cools towards the surrounding air temperature. How does its temperature change?

That leads to the separable model
\[
\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A),
\]
where \(A\) is the ambient temperature.
This simple equation already captures an important real-world behaviour: the further \(T\) is from \(A\), the faster it changes.


11.2.3 Example 2: finance and exchange rates
Financial data such as NZD/VND or USD/VND exchange rates do not move smoothly. They respond to interest rates, trade, policy, news, expectations and market shocks.
A more advanced stochastic model may be written as
\[
dS_t=\mu S_t\,dt+\sigma S_t\,dW_t.
\]
Here, the term
\[
\mu S_t\,dt
\]
represents the deterministic trend, while
\[
\sigma S_t\,dW_t
\]
represents random fluctuation.
Stochastic differential equations are not part of this chapter. However, if we ignore the random term temporarily, we get the separable deterministic model
\[
\dfrac{dS}{dt}=\mu S.
\]
Solving this gives
\[
S=S_0e^{\mu t}.
\]
So the model in this chapter is a simplified backbone for a much more complex financial idea.


11.2.4 Example 3: epidemics and saturation
A simple early-stage epidemic model may assume that the number of infected individuals grows at a rate proportional to the current number infected:
\[
\dfrac{dI}{dt}=kI.
\]
This gives exponential growth:
\[
I=I_0e^{kt}.
\]
But unlimited exponential growth is not realistic. In a fixed population, growth slows down as the number of susceptible people decreases.
A more realistic model may involve several coupled equations, such as a susceptible-infected-recovered system. A simplified version in this chapter is logistic growth:
\[
\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP\left(1-\dfrac{P}{M}\right).
\]
This is still separable, but it captures a better idea: growth is fast at first, then slows as the quantity approaches a maximum capacity \(M\).


11.2.5 Visualisation using R
The graph below compares three simplified model shapes. The values are scaled so that the shapes can be compared on one plot.


Code
time_values <- seq(0, 12, length.out = 600)

inspiration_df <- data.frame(
  time = rep(time_values, 3),
  context = rep(
    c(
      "cooling towards ambient",
      "deterministic financial growth",
      "logistic saturation"
    ),
    each = length(time_values)
  )
)

cooling_values <- 26 + (80 - 26) * exp(-0.25 * time_values)
finance_values <- 15000 * exp(0.04 * time_values)
logistic_values <- 1000 / (1 + 9 * exp(-0.7 * time_values))

inspiration_df$model_value <- c(
  cooling_values,
  finance_values,
  logistic_values
)

inspiration_df$scaled_value <- ave(
  inspiration_df$model_value,
  inspiration_df$context,
  FUN = function(values) {
    (values - min(values)) / (max(values) - min(values))
  }
)

ggplot(inspiration_df, aes(x = time, y = scaled_value, linetype = context)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Differential equations model different types of change",
    subtitle = "The values are scaled so the shapes can be compared.",
    x = "time",
    y = "scaled model value",
    linetype = "Context"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.1: Three simplified differential-equation model shapes.







11.2.6 Real-world example: using a differential equation to model an NZ bank-related share price
Heartland Group Holdings Limited is listed on the NZX under the ticker HGH. Heartland describes its story as one involving specialist finance and banking products, so this gives us a useful New Zealand financial context for a differential-equations model.
The NZX page for HGH gives the following delayed market data:

Code
hgh_data <- data.frame(
  source = "NZX delayed market data",
  ticker = "HGH",
  company = "Heartland Group Holdings Limited",
  data_timestamp = "27/06/2026, 16:31 NZST",
  current_price_nzd = 1.2050,
  change_52_week_nzd = 0.4350,
  change_52_week_percent = 56.49
)

hgh_data




Table 11.1



                   source ticker                          company
1 NZX delayed market data    HGH Heartland Group Holdings Limited
          data_timestamp current_price_nzd change_52_week_nzd
1 27/06/2026, 16:31 NZST             1.205              0.435
  change_52_week_percent
1                  56.49




This gives us two real data points for a simple classroom model.
The current price is approximately
\[
S(1)=1.205.
\]
The 52-week change is approximately
\[
0.435.
\]
So the approximate price one year earlier was
\[
S(0)=1.205-0.435=0.770.
\]
We now model the share price \(S\) using the separable differential equation
\[
\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS,
\]
where \(t\) is measured in years from approximately 52 weeks ago.
Separating the variables gives
\[
\dfrac{1}{S}\dfrac{dS}{dt}=r.
\]
So
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{S}\,dS=\int r\,dt.
\]
Hence
\[
\ln S=rt+C.
\]
Therefore,
\[
S=S_0e^{rt}.
\]
Using the real NZX data,
\[
S(0)=0.770
\]
and
\[
S(1)=1.205.
\]
So
\[
1.205=0.770e^r.
\]
Therefore,
\[
e^r=\dfrac{1.205}{0.770}.
\]
Taking logarithms gives
\[
r=\ln\left(\dfrac{1.205}{0.770}\right).
\]
Thus
\[
r\approx0.448.
\]
The fitted differential-equation model is
\[
S(t)=0.770e^{0.448t}.
\]






Warning



This is not investment advice and not a genuine financial forecast. It is a mathematical modelling example using two real NZX data points. Real share prices are affected by earnings, interest rates, dividends, credit conditions, market sentiment, news and random shocks.




11.2.7 Visualisation using R: fitting the model to real NZX data


Code
price_current <- 1.2050
change_52_week <- 0.4350
price_one_year_ago <- price_current - change_52_week

growth_rate <- log(price_current / price_one_year_ago)

observed_prices <- data.frame(
  t = c(0, 1),
  price = c(price_one_year_ago, price_current),
  label = c("approx. 52 weeks ago", "current NZX price")
)

fitted_curve <- data.frame(
  t = seq(0, 1, length.out = 400)
)

fitted_curve$price <- price_one_year_ago * exp(growth_rate * fitted_curve$t)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = fitted_curve, aes(x = t, y = price), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = observed_prices, aes(x = t, y = price), size = 3) +
  geom_text(
    data = observed_prices,
    aes(x = t, y = price, label = label),
    nudge_y = 0.05
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Fitting dS/dt = rS to real NZX HGH data",
    subtitle = paste0("Estimated continuous annual rate r = ", round(growth_rate, 3)),
    x = "time in years from approximately 52 weeks ago",
    y = "share price in NZD"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.2: A separable differential-equation model fitted to real NZX HGH data.







11.2.8 A light prediction
A full one-year extrapolation would be too aggressive for a classroom model based on only two data points. Instead, we make a light 13-week projection.
Since
\[
13\text{ weeks}=\dfrac{13}{52}\text{ years}=0.25\text{ years},
\]
we estimate
\[
S(1.25)=1.205e^{0.448(0.25)}.
\]
This gives
\[
S(1.25)\approx1.348.
\]
So the simple model gives a light 13-week projection of approximately
\[
\$1.35.
\]
This should be interpreted as a mathematical projection, not a financial prediction.

Code
prediction_weeks <- c(4, 8, 13)
prediction_years <- prediction_weeks / 52

prediction_table <- data.frame(
  weeks_after_current_price = prediction_weeks,
  years_after_current_price = round(prediction_years, 3),
  predicted_price_nzd = round(price_current * exp(growth_rate * prediction_years), 3)
)

prediction_table




Table 11.2



  weeks_after_current_price years_after_current_price predicted_price_nzd
1                         4                     0.077               1.247
2                         8                     0.154               1.291
3                        13                     0.250               1.348






11.2.9 Visualisation using R: light prediction region


Code
prediction_curve <- data.frame(
  t = seq(0, 1.25, length.out = 600)
)

prediction_curve$price <- price_one_year_ago * exp(growth_rate * prediction_curve$t)
prediction_curve$interval <- ifelse(
  prediction_curve$t <= 1,
  "observed 52-week interval",
  "13-week projection"
)

future_point <- data.frame(
  t = 1.25,
  price = price_current * exp(growth_rate * 0.25),
  label = "13-week projection"
)

ggplot() +
  annotate(
    "rect",
    xmin = 1,
    xmax = 1.25,
    ymin = -Inf,
    ymax = Inf,
    alpha = 0.12
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = prediction_curve,
    aes(x = t, y = price, linetype = interval),
    linewidth = 1.1
  ) +
  geom_point(data = observed_prices, aes(x = t, y = price), size = 3) +
  geom_point(data = future_point, aes(x = t, y = price), size = 3) +
  geom_text(
    data = future_point,
    aes(x = t, y = price, label = label),
    nudge_y = 0.05
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Light projection from dS/dt = rS",
    subtitle = "The shaded region shows the 13-week extrapolation beyond the real NZX data.",
    x = "time in years from approximately 52 weeks ago",
    y = "share price in NZD",
    linetype = "Interval"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.3: A light 13-week projection from the fitted differential-equation model.







11.2.10 Scenario check
Because the fitted value
\[
r\approx0.448
\]
comes from a strong 52-week movement, it is risky to assume that this rate continues. A more honest modelling approach is to compare scenarios.


Code
future_time <- seq(0, 0.25, length.out = 300)

scenario_df <- expand.grid(
  years_after_current_price = future_time,
  scenario = c(
    "same fitted rate",
    "half fitted rate",
    "zero growth",
    "negative half fitted rate"
  )
)

scenario_df$rate <- ifelse(
  scenario_df$scenario == "same fitted rate",
  growth_rate,
  ifelse(
    scenario_df$scenario == "half fitted rate",
    growth_rate / 2,
    ifelse(
      scenario_df$scenario == "zero growth",
      0,
      -growth_rate / 2
    )
  )
)

scenario_df$predicted_price <- price_current * exp(
  scenario_df$rate * scenario_df$years_after_current_price
)

ggplot(scenario_df, aes(x = years_after_current_price, y = predicted_price, linetype = scenario)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = price_current, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Scenario check for a 13-week projection",
    subtitle = "The model is sensitive to the assumed continuous rate r.",
    x = "years after current NZX price",
    y = "projected share price in NZD",
    linetype = "Scenario"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.4: Scenario comparison after the current NZX price.







11.2.11 Interpretation
This example follows the modelling cycle:
\[
\text{real NZX data}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
S=S_0e^{rt}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{fit }r
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{make a cautious projection}.
\]
The strength of the differential equation is that the assumption is explicit:

The price changes at a rate proportional to the current price.

Once that assumption is made, the mathematics forces an exponential model. The final step is not just calculation, but critique: we ask whether the assumption is reasonable.


11.2.12 Why we still study simple separable models
A simple separable differential equation is useful because it teaches the core modelling cycle:

identify the changing quantity;
describe its rate of change;
write a differential equation;
separate the variables;
integrate;
use an initial condition;
interpret the solution in context.

The models in this chapter are simplified, but they are not artificial. They are the first layer of the same mathematical thinking used in larger models.
For example,
\[
\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A)
\]
is not a full weather model, but it captures relaxation towards an ambient value.
Similarly,
\[
\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS
\]
is not a full financial model, but it captures the deterministic backbone of continuous growth or decay.
And
\[
\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP\left(1-\dfrac{P}{M}\right)
\]
is not a full population or epidemic model, but it captures the idea that growth slows as a limiting capacity is approached.
This chapter therefore acts as a bridge:
\[
\text{data}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{rate of change}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{differential equation}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{solution}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{interpretation}.
\]

title: 11  Differential equations
section: 11.3 10.1 Forming differential equations from rates of change
text:
11.3 10.1 Forming differential equations from rates of change

11.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
A differential equation describes how a quantity changes.
If a quantity \(y\) changes with respect to \(x\), then its rate of change is
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}.
\]
Many modelling statements translate directly into differential equations.
If \(y\) increases at a rate proportional to \(y\), then
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=ky,
\]
where \(k>0\).
If \(y\) decreases at a rate proportional to \(y\), then
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-ky,
\]
where \(k>0\).
If a temperature \(T\) approaches an ambient temperature \(A\), then a common model is
\[
\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A),
\]
where \(k>0\).
This means the rate of temperature change depends on the difference between the current temperature and the ambient temperature.


11.3.2 Visualisation using R


Code
field_df <- slope_field(
  x_min = 0,
  x_max = 4,
  y_min = 0,
  y_max = 6,
  derivative_function = function(x_value, y_value) 0.5 * y_value
)

curve_df <- data.frame(
  x = seq(0, 4, length.out = 400)
)

curve_df$y <- 2 * exp(0.5 * curve_df$x)

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = field_df,
    aes(x = xstart, y = ystart, xend = xend, yend = yend),
    alpha = 0.55
  ) +
  geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "A rate proportional to the current amount",
    subtitle = "When dy/dx = 0.5y, larger y-values have steeper gradients.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.5: Slope field for dy/dx = 0.5y.







11.3.3 Worked example
A cup of tea has temperature \(T\) degrees Celsius after \(t\) minutes. The surrounding air temperature is \(22^\circ\text{C}\). The tea cools at a rate proportional to how much hotter it is than the surrounding air.
Form a differential equation.
The temperature difference is
\[
T-22.
\]
Since the tea is cooling, the rate of change is negative when \(T>22\).
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-22),
\quad k>0.
\]
This equation also has the sensible equilibrium
\[
T=22,
\]
because when \(T=22\),
\[
\dfrac{dT}{dt}=0.
\]


11.3.4 Short investigation
The following table translates modelling language into differential equations.

Code
data.frame(
  statement = c(
    "growth proportional to amount",
    "decay proportional to amount",
    "cooling towards ambient A",
    "saturation towards capacity M",
    "logistic growth with capacity M"
  ),
  differential_equation = c(
    "dy/dt = ky",
    "dy/dt = -ky",
    "dT/dt = -k(T - A)",
    "dW/dt = k(M - W)",
    "dP/dt = kP(1 - P/M)"
  )
)




Table 11.3



                        statement differential_equation
1   growth proportional to amount            dy/dt = ky
2    decay proportional to amount           dy/dt = -ky
3       cooling towards ambient A     dT/dt = -k(T - A)
4   saturation towards capacity M      dW/dt = k(M - W)
5 logistic growth with capacity M   dP/dt = kP(1 - P/M)






11.3.5 Exercises 10.1

11.3.5.1 Bronze

A quantity \(y\) increases at a rate proportional to \(y\). Write a differential equation.
A quantity \(y\) decreases at a rate proportional to \(y\). Write a differential equation.
A population \(P\) decreases at \(5\%\) per hour. Write a differential equation.
A speed \(v\) decreases at a rate proportional to \(v^2\). Write a differential equation.
A water level \(W\) rises towards a maximum level \(M\) at a rate proportional to \(M-W\). Write a differential equation.



11.3.5.2 Silver

A drink of temperature \(T\) cools in a room of temperature \(22^\circ\text{C}\). Its rate of cooling is proportional to \(T-22\). Write a differential equation.
A number of infected individuals \(I\) grows at a rate proportional to \(I(1000-I)\). Write a differential equation.
A deterministic exchange-rate model assumes a value \(S\) changes at a rate proportional to \(S\). Write a differential equation.

11.3.5.3 Gold

A cold object warms towards ambient temperature \(30^\circ\text{C}\) at a rate proportional to the temperature difference. Write a differential equation.
