The rate of decrease of \(y\) with respect to \(x\) is proportional to \(\dfrac{y}{1+x}\). Write a differential equation.
A population \(P\) grows logistically with carrying capacity \(5000\). Write a suitable differential equation.
Explain why \(T=A\) is an equilibrium solution of \(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A)\).

title: 11  Differential equations
text:
11.4 10.2 Solving separable differential equations

11.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
A first-order differential equation is separable if it can be rearranged so that all the \(y\) terms are on one side and all the \(x\) terms are on the other.
For example,
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=xy
\]
can be rearranged as
\[
\dfrac{1}{y}\dfrac{dy}{dx}=x.
\]
This leads to
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{y}\,dy=\int x\,dx.
\]
So
\[
\ln|y|=\dfrac{x^2}{2}+C.
\]
A common final form is
\[
y=Ae^{\dfrac{x^2}{2}},
\]
where \(A\) is a non-zero constant.


11.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
x_values <- seq(-2, 2, length.out = 600)

solution_df <- expand.grid(
  x = x_values,
  initial_value = c(1, 2, 4)
)

solution_df$y <- solution_df$initial_value * exp(solution_df$x^2)
solution_df$curve_label <- paste0("y(0) = ", solution_df$initial_value)

ggplot(solution_df, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = curve_label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 25)) +
  labs(
    title = "Solution family for dy/dx = 2xy",
    subtitle = "Different initial conditions give different curves.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Initial condition"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.6: A family of solutions to dy/dx = 2xy.







11.4.3 Worked example
Solve
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2xy.
\]
Separate the variables:
\[
\dfrac{1}{y}\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2x.
\]
Integrate both sides:
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{y}\,dy
=
\int 2x\,dx.
\]
So
\[
\ln|y|=x^2+C.
\]
Exponentiating gives
\[
|y|=e^{x^2+C}=e^C e^{x^2}.
\]
So the general solution may be written as
\[
y=Ae^{x^2},
\]
where \(A\) is a constant.


11.4.4 Short investigation
Check the solution numerically by differentiating the expression in R.

Code
x_values <- c(-1, 0, 1, 2)
y_values <- 3 * exp(x_values^2)
dy_dx_values <- 6 * x_values * exp(x_values^2)
right_side_values <- 2 * x_values * y_values

data.frame(
  x = x_values,
  y = round(y_values, 5),
  derivative = round(dy_dx_values, 5),
  right_side = round(right_side_values, 5)
)




Table 11.4



   x         y derivative right_side
1 -1   8.15485  -16.30969  -16.30969
2  0   3.00000    0.00000    0.00000
3  1   8.15485   16.30969   16.30969
4  2 163.79445  655.17780  655.17780






11.4.5 Exercises 10.2

11.4.5.1 Bronze

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3x^2\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2xy\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=y\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{x}{y}\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=4xy^2\).



11.4.5.2 Silver

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=(x+1)y\), given that \(y=2\) when \(x=0\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{3x}{y}\), given that \(y=4\) when \(x=0\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{y}{1+x}\), given that \(y=5\) when \(x=0\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2x(1+y)\), given that \(y=0\) when \(x=0\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{6x}{y+1}\), given that \(y=2\) when \(x=0\).



11.4.5.3 Gold

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=xy^2\), given that \(y=1\) when \(x=0\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=y\cos x\), given that \(y=3\) when \(x=0\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{1+x}{y}\), given that \(y=1\) when \(x=0\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=e^xy^2\), given that \(y=2\) when \(x=0\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=(2x+1)(y-4)\), given that \(y=5\) when \(x=0\).

title: 11  Differential equations
text:
11.5 10.3 Particular solutions and interpretation

11.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
A general solution contains an arbitrary constant.
A particular solution uses extra information, usually an initial condition, to find that constant.
For example, the general solution
\[
y=Ae^{x^2}
\]
becomes a particular solution if we know the value of \(y\) at one point.
If
\[
y(0)=7,
\]
then
\[
7=Ae^0=A.
\]
So the particular solution is
\[
y=7e^{x^2}.
\]


11.5.2 Visualisation using R


Code
x_values <- seq(-1.5, 1.5, length.out = 600)

curve_df <- data.frame(
  x = x_values,
  y = 7 * exp(x_values^2)
)

point_df <- data.frame(
  x = 0,
  y = 7,
  label = "(0, 7)"
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = point_df, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = point_df, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_y = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Particular solution",
    subtitle = "The initial condition selects one curve from the family.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.7: The solution y = 7e(x2) passes through (0, 7).







11.5.3 Worked example
Solve
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{3y}{1+x},
\quad y(0)=2.
\]
Separate the variables:
\[
\dfrac{1}{y}\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{3}{1+x}.
\]
Integrate:
\[
\int \dfrac{1}{y}\,dy
=
\int \dfrac{3}{1+x}\,dx.
\]
So
\[
\ln|y|=3\ln|1+x|+C.
\]
For the solution through \(x=0\), we work on the interval
\[
x>-1.
\]
Thus
\[
\ln|y|=\ln(1+x)^3+C.
\]
Hence
\[
y=A(1+x)^3.
\]
Using \(y(0)=2\) gives
\[
2=A.
\]
Therefore,
\[
y=2(1+x)^3,
\quad x>-1.
\]


11.5.4 Short investigation
Use R to compare several particular solutions.


Code
x_values <- seq(-0.9, 2, length.out = 600)

solution_df <- expand.grid(
  x = x_values,
  initial_value = c(1, 2, 4)
)

solution_df$y <- solution_df$initial_value * (1 + solution_df$x)^3
solution_df$curve_label <- paste0("y(0) = ", solution_df$initial_value)

ggplot(solution_df, aes(x = x, y = y, linetype = curve_label)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Initial conditions change the constant",
    subtitle = "The shape is controlled by the differential equation; the starting value selects the curve.",
    x = "x",
    y = "y",
    linetype = "Initial condition"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.8: Particular solutions of dy/dx = 3y/(1+x).







11.5.5 Exercises 10.3

11.5.5.1 Bronze

The general solution is \(y=Ae^{2x}\). Find \(A\) if \(y(0)=5\).
The general solution is \(y=A(1+x)^3\). Find \(A\) if \(y(0)=4\).
The general solution is \(y^2=x^2+C\). Find \(C\) if \(y(0)=3\).
The general solution is \(\ln y=x+C\). Find \(y\) if \(y(0)=2\).
The general solution is \(y=Ae^{-3t}\). Find \(A\) if \(y(0)=80\).



11.5.5.2 Silver

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=4y\), given \(y(0)=3\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=xy\), given \(y(0)=2\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2y}{1+x}\), given \(y(0)=5\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{4x}{y}\), given \(y(0)=1\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=x(y+2)\), given \(y(0)=0\).



11.5.5.3 Gold

Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=3x^2(y-1)\), given \(y(0)=2\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=\dfrac{2x+1}{y}\), given \(y(0)=3\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=y\tan x\), given \(y(0)=4\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=x(y-3)^2\), given \(y(0)=4\).
Find the solution of \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=ky\), \(y(0)=100\), if \(y(5)=200\).

title: 11  Differential equations
text:
11.6 10.4 Growth, decay and saturation models

11.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
The simplest growth model is
\[
\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP,
\quad k>0.
\]
Separating variables gives
\[
\dfrac{1}{P}\dfrac{dP}{dt}=k.
\]
So
\[
\ln P=kt+C,
\]
and therefore
\[
P=P_0e^{kt}.
\]
The simplest decay model is
\[
\dfrac{dP}{dt}=-kP,
\quad k>0,
\]
with solution
\[
P=P_0e^{-kt}.
\]
Growth proportional to current amount is a useful first model, but it cannot grow forever in a restricted environment. A saturation model has the form
\[
\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP\left(1-\dfrac{P}{M}\right),
\]
where \(M\) is the carrying capacity.


11.6.2 Visualisation using R


Code
t_values <- seq(0, 12, length.out = 600)

model_df <- data.frame(
  t = rep(t_values, 3),
  model = rep(c("growth", "decay", "saturation"), each = length(t_values))
)

model_df$value <- c(
  growth_solution(t_values, initial_value = 100, rate = 0.18),
  growth_solution(t_values, initial_value = 100, rate = -0.18),
  logistic_solution(t_values, capacity = 1000, initial_value = 100, rate = 0.45)
)

ggplot(model_df, aes(x = t, y = value, linetype = model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Three model shapes",
    subtitle = "Growth, decay and saturation have distinct long-term behaviour.",
    x = "time",
    y = "quantity",
    linetype = "Model"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.9: Exponential growth, exponential decay and logistic saturation.







11.6.3 Worked example
A quantity \(P\) grows at a rate proportional to \(P\). Initially,
\[
P(0)=500.
\]
After \(10\) years,
\[
P(10)=700.
\]
Find a model for \(P\).
Since
\[
\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP,
\]
we have
\[
P=500e^{kt}.
\]
Using \(P(10)=700\) gives
\[
700=500e^{10k}.
\]
Thus
\[
e^{10k}=\dfrac{7}{5}.
\]
So
\[
10k=\ln\left(\dfrac{7}{5}\right),
\]
and
\[
k=\dfrac{1}{10}\ln\left(\dfrac{7}{5}\right).
\]
Therefore,
\[
P=500e^{\dfrac{t}{10}\ln(\dfrac{7}{5})}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
P=500\left(\dfrac{7}{5}\right)^{t/10}.
\]


11.6.4 Short investigation: NZ and Vietnam context
The following small dataset is a classroom-style structure for comparing a deterministic model with updated observations. Replace the values with current official values before using the activity live.


Code
context_df <- data.frame(
  year = 0:5,
  index_value = c(100, 106, 113, 121, 128, 137)
)

rate_estimate <- log(context_df$index_value[6] / context_df$index_value[1]) / 5

curve_df <- data.frame(
  year = seq(0, 5, length.out = 400)
)

curve_df$model <- context_df$index_value[1] * exp(rate_estimate * curve_df$year)

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = context_df, aes(x = year, y = index_value), size = 3) +
  geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = year, y = model), linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Fitting a simple exponential model",
    subtitle = "This structure can be reused with updated NZ, Vietnam or school data.",
    x = "years from start",
    y = "index"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.10: A deterministic curve compared with a small classroom dataset.







11.6.5 Exercises 10.4

11.6.5.1 Bronze

Solve \(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0.2P\), given \(P(0)=50\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=-0.3P\), given \(P(0)=80\).
A quantity satisfies \(P=100e^{0.05t}\). Find \(\dfrac{dP}{dt}\) in terms of \(P\).

11.6.5.2 Silver

A bacteria culture satisfies \(\dfrac{dN}{dt}=0.4N\), \(N(0)=200\). Find \(N(5)\).
A pollutant level satisfies \(\dfrac{dC}{dt}=-0.25C\), \(C(0)=60\). Find the time when \(C=10\).
A quantity doubles every \(8\) years. Find the exponential growth constant.



11.6.5.3 Gold

A quantity satisfies \(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP\) and triples in \(12\) years. Find \(k\).
A quantity satisfies \(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=-kP\) and halves in \(6\) years. Find \(k\).

For the logistic model in Question 3, find the time when \(P=800\).
Show that the logistic model \(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP\left(1-\dfrac{P}{M}\right)\) has equilibrium solutions \(P=0\) and \(P=M\).

title: 11  Differential equations
text:
11.7 10.5 Cooling and weather-type relaxation models

11.7.1 Key mathematical ideas
Newton’s law of cooling has the form
\[
\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A),
\]
where:

\(T\) is the object’s temperature;
\(A\) is the ambient temperature;
\(k>0\) is the cooling constant.

This model is separable:
\[
\dfrac{1}{T-A}\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k.
\]
So
\[
\ln|T-A|=-kt+C.
\]
Therefore,
\[
T=A+Ce^{-kt}.
\]
If \(T(0)=T_0\), then
\[
T=A+(T_0-A)e^{-kt}.
\]
This form also models many relaxation processes: a quantity moves gradually towards a long-term level.


11.7.2 Visualisation using R


Code
time_values <- seq(0, 40, length.out = 600)

cooling_df <- data.frame(
  time = time_values,
  temperature = cooling_solution(
    time_values,
    ambient = 26,
    initial_value = 80,
    cooling_constant = log(9 / 4) / 10
  )
)

ggplot(cooling_df, aes(x = time, y = temperature)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 26, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Newton cooling model",
    subtitle = "The temperature approaches the ambient level without crossing it.",
    x = "time in minutes",
    y = "temperature in degrees Celsius"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.11: Cooling towards ambient temperature.







11.7.3 Worked example
A hot drink is placed in a room at temperature
\[
26^\circ\text{C}.
\]
Initially, the drink is at
\[
80^\circ\text{C}.
\]
After \(10\) minutes, its temperature is
\[
50^\circ\text{C}.
\]
Assume
\[
\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-26).
\]
Find \(T\) in terms of \(t\).
The general solution is
\[
T=26+Ce^{-kt}.
\]
Using \(T(0)=80\) gives
\[
80=26+C,
\]
so
\[
C=54.
\]
Thus
\[
T=26+54e^{-kt}.
\]
Using \(T(10)=50\) gives
\[
50=26+54e^{-10k}.
\]
So
\[
24=54e^{-10k},
\]
and
\[
e^{-10k}=\dfrac{4}{9}.
\]
Taking logarithms,
\[
-10k=\ln\left(\dfrac{4}{9}\right).
\]
Hence
\[
k=\dfrac{1}{10}\ln\left(\dfrac{9}{4}\right).
\]
Therefore,
\[
T=26+54e^{-\dfrac{t}{10}\ln(\dfrac{9}{4})}.
\]


11.7.4 Short investigation: comparing ambient settings
The same drink cools differently in different ambient conditions.


Code
time_values <- seq(0, 45, length.out = 600)

ambient_df <- expand.grid(
  time = time_values,
  place = c("cooler ambient", "warmer ambient")
)

ambient_df$ambient <- ifelse(ambient_df$place == "cooler ambient", 16, 30)
ambient_df$temperature <- cooling_solution(
  ambient_df$time,
  ambient = ambient_df$ambient,
  initial_value = 80,
  cooling_constant = 0.08
)

ggplot(ambient_df, aes(x = time, y = temperature, linetype = place)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title = "Same cooling constant, different ambient temperatures",
    subtitle = "This is a useful classroom link to weather data from NZ and Vietnam.",
    x = "time in minutes",
    y = "temperature in degrees Celsius",
    linetype = "Context"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.12: Cooling curves under two illustrative ambient temperatures.







11.7.5 Exercises 10.5

11.7.5.1 Bronze

Solve \(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-0.2(T-20)\), given \(T(0)=80\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-25)\), given \(T(0)=70\).

11.7.5.2 Silver

For Question 1, find the time when \(T=30\).

11.7.5.3 Gold

A hot drink cools in ambient temperature \(26^\circ\text{C}\). Initially \(T=80\), and after \(10\) minutes \(T=50\). Find \(T(t)\).
For Question 1, find the time when \(T=30\).
A cold object warms towards \(32^\circ\text{C}\). Initially it is \(12^\circ\text{C}\) and after \(5\) minutes it is \(22^\circ\text{C}\). Find the model.
Show that if \(T=A+(T_0-A)e^{-kt}\), then it satisfies \(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A)\).
In the model \(T=A+Ce^{-kt}\) with \(k>0\), explain why the curve approaches \(A\) but never reaches it in finite time unless \(C=0\).

title: 11  Differential equations
section: 11.8 10.6 Deterministic finance models and stochastic context
text:
11.8 10.6 Deterministic finance models and stochastic context

11.8.1 Key mathematical ideas
A simple deterministic finance model assumes a value \(S\) changes at a rate proportional to itself:
\[
\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS.
\]
Separating variables gives
\[
\dfrac{1}{S}\dfrac{dS}{dt}=r.
\]
Thus
\[
\ln S=rt+C,
\]
and
\[
S=S_0e^{rt}.
\]
This is the deterministic backbone behind many compound-growth models.
Real financial data are not smooth. Exchange rates, asset prices and interest-rate-sensitive values include random shocks. A stochastic model may add a random component, but stochastic differential equations are beyond this chapter.
In this chapter, we use only the deterministic separable model.


11.8.2 Visualisation using R


Code
set.seed(9709)

time_values <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 252)
initial_rate <- 15000
annual_drift <- 0.02
annual_volatility <- 0.08

deterministic_df <- data.frame(
  time = time_values,
  rate_value = growth_solution(time_values, initial_rate, annual_drift),
  path = "deterministic"
)

simulated_paths <- do.call(
  rbind,
  lapply(1:6, function(path_id) {
    random_steps <- rnorm(length(time_values) - 1, mean = 0, sd = sqrt(1 / 252))
    log_values <- numeric(length(time_values))
    log_values[1] <- log(initial_rate)
    
    for (i in 2:length(time_values)) {
      log_values[i] <- log_values[i - 1] +
        (annual_drift - 0.5 * annual_volatility^2) / 252 +
        annual_volatility * random_steps[i - 1]
    }
    
    data.frame(
      time = time_values,
      rate_value = exp(log_values),
      path = paste0("simulated path ", path_id)
    )
  })
)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = simulated_paths,
    aes(x = time, y = rate_value, group = path),
    alpha = 0.35
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = deterministic_df,
    aes(x = time, y = rate_value),
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Deterministic model and stochastic-looking variation",
    subtitle = "Only the smooth deterministic model is solved in this chapter.",
    x = "time in years",
    y = "index value"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 11.13: A deterministic exponential curve with simulated noisy paths.







11.8.3 Worked example
A deterministic exchange-rate index \(S\) satisfies
\[
\dfrac{dS}{dt}=0.02S,
\]
where \(t\) is measured in years.
Suppose
\[
S(0)=15000.
\]
Find \(S(t)\) and estimate \(S(0.5)\).
Since
\[
\dfrac{dS}{dt}=0.02S,
\]
we have
\[
S=15000e^{0.02t}.
\]
At \(t=0.5\),
\[
S(0.5)=15000e^{0.01}.
\]
Thus
\[
S(0.5)\approx15150.75.
\]
This is not a forecast. It is a simple deterministic model for practising separable differential equations.


11.8.4 Short investigation
Use the deterministic formula to compare different annual rates.

Code
rates <- c(-0.03, 0, 0.02, 0.05)

data.frame(
  annual_rate = rates,
  value_after_half_year = round(15000 * exp(rates * 0.5), 2),
  value_after_one_year = round(15000 * exp(rates), 2)
)




Table 11.5



  annual_rate value_after_half_year value_after_one_year
1       -0.03              14776.68             14556.68
2        0.00              15000.00             15000.00
3        0.02              15150.75             15303.02
4        0.05              15379.73             15769.07






11.8.5 Exercises 10.6

11.8.5.1 Bronze

Solve \(\dfrac{dS}{dt}=0.04S\), given \(S(0)=1000\).
Solve \(\dfrac{dS}{dt}=-0.02S\), given \(S(0)=5000\).
If \(S=15000e^{0.02t}\), find \(S(1)\).
If \(S=100e^{-0.1t}\), find the percentage rate of continuous decrease.
Explain why \(S=S_0e^{rt}\) is deterministic.



11.8.5.2 Silver

A value grows continuously at \(3\%\) per year from \(2000\). Find its value after \(5\) years.
A value decreases continuously at \(4\%\) per year from \(8000\). Find its value after \(2\) years.
A value changes from \(15000\) to \(15150.75\) in half a year under \(\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS\). Find \(r\).
Find the time for \(S=1000e^{0.06t}\) to reach \(1500\).
Find the time for \(S=2000e^{-0.08t}\) to fall to \(1200\).



11.8.5.3 Gold

A deterministic index satisfies \(\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS\), \(S(0)=S_0\). Prove that \(S=S_0e^{rt}\).
A value doubles in \(3\) years under continuous growth. Find \(r\).
A value loses \(20\%\) in \(4\) years under continuous decay. Find the decay constant \(k\).
Explain why a deterministic exponential model may be a poor forecast for daily exchange rates.
A model satisfies \(\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS\) and \(S(1)=18000\), \(S(3)=22000\). Find \(r\) and \(S(0)\).

title: 11  Differential equations
text:
11.9 STEP-style extension questions
These questions are designed to require proof, structure or exact reasoning.

11.9.1 STEP-style Question 1
Solve
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=xy,
\quad y(0)=2.
\]
Then show directly by differentiation that your answer satisfies the differential equation.


11.9.2 STEP-style Question 2
Solve
\[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=x(y-1)^2,
\quad y(0)=2.
\]
State the largest interval containing \(0\) on which your solution is defined.


11.9.3 STEP-style Question 3
A quantity \(P\) satisfies the logistic differential equation
\[
\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP\left(1-\dfrac{P}{M}\right),
\]
where \(k>0\) and \(M>0\).
Show that the general solution may be written as
\[
P=\dfrac{M}{1+Ae^{-kt}}.
\]


11.9.4 STEP-style Question 4
A hot object cools according to
\[
\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A).
\]
Given \(T(0)=T_0\), prove that
\[
T=A+(T_0-A)e^{-kt}.
\]
Then explain why the model predicts approach to ambient temperature rather than reaching it in finite time.


11.9.5 STEP-style Question 5
A deterministic financial index satisfies
\[
\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS.
\]
Suppose \(S(1)=18000\) and \(S(3)=22000\).
Find \(r\) and \(S(0)\) exactly in logarithmic form.

title: 11  Differential equations
text:
11.10 Reflection questions

What does a differential equation describe?
What does it mean to separate the variables?
Why do we usually need an initial condition?
Why can the same general solution represent infinitely many curves?
Why does \(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP\) lead to exponential growth?
Why does \(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-A)\) approach an ambient value?
Why is a logistic model more realistic than unlimited exponential growth in some contexts?
Why is stochastic finance beyond the technique of this chapter?
What can R show clearly about solution families that algebra alone may hide?
What can exact separation of variables prove that a graph can only suggest?

title: 11  Differential equations
text:
11.11 Answers

11.11.1 Answers to Exercises 10.1

11.11.1.1 Bronze

\(\dfrac{dy}{dt}=ky\), where \(k>0\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dt}=-ky\), where \(k>0\).
\(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=-0.05P\).
\(\dfrac{dv}{dt}=-kv^2\), where \(k>0\).
\(\dfrac{dW}{dt}=k(M-W)\), where \(k>0\).



11.11.1.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T-22)\), where \(k>0\).
\(\dfrac{dI}{dt}=kI(1000-I)\), where \(k>0\).
\(\dfrac{dS}{dt}=rS\).
\(\dfrac{dD}{dt}=-kD\), where \(k>0\).
\(N(0)=200\).



11.11.1.3 Gold

\(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=k(30-T)\), where \(k>0\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=kxy\), where \(k>0\).
\(\dfrac{dy}{dx}=-\dfrac{ky}{1+x}\), where \(k>0\).
For example, \(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=kP\left(1-\dfrac{P}{5000}\right)\).
If \(T=A\), then \(T-A=0\), so \(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=0\). Therefore \(T=A\) is an equilibrium solution.




11.11.2 Answers to Exercises 10.2

11.11.2.1 Bronze

\(y=x^3+C\).
\(y=Ae^{x^2}\).
\(y=Ae^x\).
\(y^2=x^2+C\).
\(y=\dfrac{1}{C-2x^2}\).



11.11.2.2 Silver

\(y=2e^{\dfrac{x^2}{2}+x}\).
\(y^2=3x^2+16\).
\(y=5(1+x)\), on the interval \(x>-1\).
\(y=e^{x^2}-1\).
\(y=-1+\sqrt{6x^2+9}\).



11.11.2.3 Gold

\(y=\dfrac{1}{1-\dfrac{x^2}{2}}\).
\(y=3e^{\sin x}\).
\(y=x+1\) on the interval \(x>-1\).
\(y=\dfrac{2}{3-2e^x}\).
\(y=4+e^{x^2+x}\).




11.11.3 Answers to Exercises 10.3

11.11.3.1 Bronze

\(A=5\).
\(A=4\).
\(C=9\).
\(y=2e^x\).
\(A=80\).



11.11.3.2 Silver

\(y=3e^{4x}\).
\(y=2e^{\dfrac{x^2}{2}}\).
\(y=5(1+x)^2\), on the interval \(x>-1\).
\(y=\sqrt{4x^2+1}\) near \(x=0\).
\(y=2e^{\dfrac{x^2}{2}}-2\).



11.11.3.3 Gold

\(y=1+e^{x^3}\).
\(y=\sqrt{2x^2+2x+9}\) near \(x=0\).
\(y=4\sec x\) on an interval containing \(0\).
\(y=3+\dfrac{1}{1-\dfrac{x^2}{2}}\).
\(k=\dfrac{1}{5}\ln2\), so \(y=100e^{\dfrac{x}{5}\ln2}\).




11.11.4 Answers to Exercises 10.4

11.11.4.1 Bronze

\(P=50e^{0.2t}\).
\(P=80e^{-0.3t}\).
\(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0.05P\).
The half-life is \(\dfrac{\ln2}{0.1}=10\ln2\).
\(k>0\) means growth; \(k<0\) means decay.



11.11.4.2 Silver

\(k=\dfrac{1}{10}\ln\left(\dfrac{7}{5}\right)\).
\(k=\dfrac{1}{4}\ln\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)\).
\(N(5)=200e^2\).
\(t=4\ln6\).
\(k=\dfrac{\ln2}{8}\).



11.11.4.3 Gold

\(k=\dfrac{\ln3}{12}\).
\(k=\dfrac{\ln2}{6}\).
\(P=\dfrac{1000}{1+9e^{-0.4t}}\).
\(t=\dfrac{\ln36}{0.4}=2.5\ln36\approx8.96\).
If \(P=0\) or \(P=M\), then \(\dfrac{dP}{dt}=0\), so both are equilibrium solutions.




11.11.5 Answers to Exercises 10.5

11.11.5.1 Bronze

\(T=20+60e^{-0.2t}\).
\(T=25+45e^{-kt}\).
\(T(0)=80\).
The long-term temperature is \(20^\circ\text{C}\).
If \(T=A\), then \(T-A=0\), so the derivative is \(0\).



11.11.5.2 Silver

\(k=\dfrac{1}{5}\ln\left(\dfrac{7}{4}\right)\).
\(t=\dfrac{\ln7}{k}=\dfrac{5\ln7}{\ln(7/4)}\).
\(T=18+10e^{-0.1t}\).
\(T=30-20e^{-0.2t}\).
\(W=100-30e^{-0.3t}\).



11.11.5.3 Gold

\(T=26+54e^{-\dfrac{t}{10}\ln(\dfrac{9}{4})}\).
\(t=\dfrac{10\ln(27/2)}{\ln(9/4)}\approx32.1\) minutes.
\(T=32-20e^{-kt}\), where \(k=\dfrac{1}{5}\ln2\).
Differentiating gives \(\dfrac{dT}{dt}=-k(T_0-A)e^{-kt}=-k(T-A)\).
If \(C\neq0\), then \(e^{-kt}\) is positive for every finite \(t\), so \(T-A=Ce^{-kt}\) is never \(0\).




11.11.6 Answers to Exercises 10.6

11.11.6.1 Bronze

\(S=1000e^{0.04t}\).
\(S=5000e^{-0.02t}\).
\(S(1)=15000e^{0.02}\approx15303.02\).
It decreases continuously at \(10\%\) per unit time.
It has no random term, so each value of \(t\) gives exactly one predicted value.



11.11.6.2 Silver

The value is \(2000e^{0.15}\approx2323.67\).
The value is \(8000e^{-0.08}\approx7384.93\).
\(r=0.02\).
\(t=\dfrac{\ln(3/2)}{0.06}\approx6.76\).
\(t=\dfrac{\ln(5/3)}{0.08}\approx6.39\).



11.11.6.3 Gold

Separating variables gives \(\ln S=rt+C\), so \(S=Ae^{rt}\). Since \(S(0)=S_0\), \(A=S_0\).
\(r=\dfrac{\ln2}{3}\).
\(k=\dfrac{1}{4}\ln\left(\dfrac{5}{4}\right)\).
Daily exchange rates have shocks, interventions, liquidity effects and news effects, so a smooth deterministic exponential curve is only a simplified backbone.
\(r=\dfrac{1}{2}\ln\left(\dfrac{11}{9}\right)\) and \(S(0)=18000e^{-r}\).




11.11.7 Answers to STEP-style extension questions

Separating variables gives \[
\int \dfrac{1}{y}\,dy=\int x\,dx.
\] Hence \[
\ln|y|=\dfrac{x^2}{2}+C.
\] Since \(y(0)=2\), \[
y=2e^{x^2/2}.
\] Differentiating gives \[
\dfrac{dy}{dx}=2e^{x^2/2}\cdot x=xy.
\]
Separate variables: \[
\int \dfrac{1}{(y-1)^2}\,dy=\int x\,dx.
\] Thus \[
-\dfrac{1}{y-1}=\dfrac{x^2}{2}+C.
\] Since \(y(0)=2\), we get \(C=-1\). Hence \[
\dfrac{1}{y-1}=1-\dfrac{x^2}{2}.
\] Therefore, \[
y=1+\dfrac{1}{1-\dfrac{x^2}{2}}.
\] The largest interval containing \(0\) is \[
-\sqrt2<x<\sqrt2.
\]
Separate variables: \[
\dfrac{dP}{P(1-P/M)}=k\,dt.
\] Since \[
\dfrac{1}{P(1-P/M)}=\dfrac{M}{P(M-P)}
=
\dfrac{1}{P}+\dfrac{1}{M-P},
\] integration gives \[
\ln|P|-\ln|M-P|=kt+C.
\] Hence \[
\ln\left|\dfrac{P}{M-P}\right|=kt+C.
\] Therefore \[
\dfrac{P}{M-P}=Be^{kt}.
\] Rearranging gives \[
P=\dfrac{M}{1+Ae^{-kt}}.
\]
Separating variables gives \[
\int \dfrac{1}{T-A}\,dT=\int -k\,dt.
\] Hence \[
\ln|T-A|=-kt+C.
\] Therefore \[
T=A+Ce^{-kt}.
\] Using \(T(0)=T_0\) gives \(C=T_0-A\), so \[
T=A+(T_0-A)e^{-kt}.
\] Since \(e^{-kt}>0\) for finite \(t\), the model approaches \(A\) but does not reach it in finite time unless \(T_0=A\).
Since \[
S=S_0e^{rt},
\] the two conditions give \[
18000=S_0e^r
\] and \[
22000=S_0e^{3r}.
\] Dividing gives \[
\dfrac{11}{9}=e^{2r}.
\] Hence \[
r=\dfrac{1}{2}\ln\left(\dfrac{11}{9}\right).
\] Also \[
S_0=18000e^{-r}
=
18000\sqrt{\dfrac{9}{11}}.
\]

title: 12  Complex numbers
text:
12.1 Overview
This chapter develops six connected ideas:
The central aim is to make complex numbers both algebraic and geometric. A complex number
\[
z=x+iy
\]
is also the point
\[
(x,y)
\]
on an Argand diagram. This link between algebra and geometry is especially important when solving loci questions.

title: 12  Complex numbers
12  Complex numbers
text:
12.2 12.1 Imaginary numbers and Cartesian form

12.2.1 Key mathematical ideas
The imaginary unit \(i\) is defined by
\[
i^2=-1.
\]
A complex number has Cartesian form
\[
z=x+iy,
\]
where \(x\) and \(y\) are real numbers.
The real part is
\[
\operatorname{Re}(z)=x,
\]
and the imaginary part is
\[
\operatorname{Im}(z)=y.
\]
Two complex numbers are equal if and only if their real and imaginary parts are equal. Therefore, if
\[
a+bi=c+di,
\]
then
\[
a=c
\quad\text{and}\quad
b=d.
\]


12.2.2 Visualisation using R


Code
points_df <- argand_points(
  z_values = c(3 + 2i, -2 + 4i, -3 - 1i, 2 - 3i),
  labels = c("3 + 2i", "-2 + 4i", "-3 - i", "2 - 3i")
)

ggplot(points_df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), nudge_y = 0.35) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-5, 5), ylim = c(-5, 5)) +
  labs(
    title = "The complex plane",
    subtitle = "The real part is horizontal; the imaginary part is vertical.",
    x = "Re(z)",
    y = "Im(z)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 12.1: Complex numbers represented as points on an Argand diagram.







12.2.3 Worked example
Given
\[
3+2i+a+bi=7-i,
\]
find \(a\) and \(b\).
The left-hand side is
\[
(3+a)+(2+b)i.
\]
Comparing real and imaginary parts gives
\[
3+a=7
\]
and
\[
2+b=-1.
\]
Therefore,
\[
a=4
\quad\text{and}\quad
b=-3.
\]


12.2.4 Short investigation
Use R to separate real and imaginary parts.

Code
z_values <- c(3 + 2i, -2 + 4i, -3 - 1i, 2 - 3i)

data.frame(
  z = as.character(z_values),
  real_part = Re(z_values),
  imaginary_part = Im(z_values)
)




Table 12.1



      z real_part imaginary_part
1  3+2i         3              2
2 -2+4i        -2              4
3 -3-1i        -3             -1
4  2-3i         2             -3






12.2.5 Exercises 12.1

12.2.5.1 Bronze

Simplify \(i^2+i^4\).
Write down the real and imaginary parts of \(5-3i\).
If \(a+2i=7+bi\), find \(a\) and \(b\).
Simplify \((2+3i)+(4-i)\).
Simplify \((7-2i)-(3+5i)\).



12.2.5.2 Silver

If \((a+1)+(b-2)i=6+3i\), find \(a\) and \(b\).
Simplify \(i^{17}\).
Simplify \((3+2i)(1-4i)\).
Find real numbers \(a\) and \(b\) such that \((2+i)+(a+bi)=5-4i\).
Show that \((1+i)^2=2i\).



12.2.5.3 Gold

Find positive real numbers \(a\) and \(b\) if \((a+bi)^2=7+24i\).
Find all real \(x\) such that \((x+2i)^2\) is real.
Find all real \(y\) such that \((3+yi)^2\) is purely imaginary.
If \(z=x+iy\) and \(z+\bar z=10\), find \(x\).
If \(z=x+iy\) and \(z-\bar z=8i\), find \(y\).

title: 12  Complex numbers
text:
12.3 12.2 Conjugates, modulus, argument and division

12.3.1 Key mathematical ideas
If
\[
z=x+iy,
\]
then the conjugate of \(z\) is
\[
\bar z=x-iy.
\]
The modulus of \(z\) is
\[
|z|=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}.
\]
The argument of \(z\) is the angle measured from the positive real axis to the line from the origin to the point representing \(z\).
The key identity is
\[
z\bar z=(x+iy)(x-iy)=x^2+y^2=|z|^2.
\]
This is why conjugates are useful for division.


12.3.2 Visualisation using R


Code
points_df <- argand_points(
  z_values = c(3 + 4i, 3 - 4i),
  labels = c("z = 3 + 4i", "conjugate = 3 - 4i")
)

segments_df <- data.frame(
  x = c(0, 0),
  y = c(0, 0),
  xend = points_df$x,
  yend = points_df$y,
  label = points_df$label
)

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = segments_df,
    aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend, linetype = label),
    linewidth = 1.1
  ) +
  geom_point(data = points_df, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = points_df, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_x = 0.25) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-1, 5), ylim = c(-5, 5)) +
  labs(
    title = "Complex conjugates",
    subtitle = "The real part is unchanged; the imaginary part changes sign.",
    x = "Re(z)",
    y = "Im(z)",
    linetype = "Point"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 12.2: Conjugation reflects a complex number in the real axis.







12.3.3 Worked example
Calculate
\[
\dfrac{5+2i}{1-3i}.
\]
Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:
\[
\dfrac{5+2i}{1-3i}
=
\dfrac{(5+2i)(1+3i)}{(1-3i)(1+3i)}.
\]
The numerator is
\[
(5+2i)(1+3i)=5+15i+2i+6i^2=-1+17i.
\]
The denominator is
\[
1^2+3^2=10.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\dfrac{5+2i}{1-3i}
=
\dfrac{-1+17i}{10}
=
-\dfrac{1}{10}+\dfrac{17}{10}i.
\]


12.3.4 Short investigation
R can check moduli and principal arguments.

Code
z_values <- c(3 + 4i, -3 + 4i, -3 - 4i, 3 - 4i)

data.frame(
  z = as.character(z_values),
  modulus = Mod(z_values),
  argument_radians = round(Arg(z_values), 4),
  argument_degrees = round(Arg(z_values) * 180 / pi, 1)
)




Table 12.2



      z modulus argument_radians argument_degrees
1  3+4i       5           0.9273             53.1
2 -3+4i       5           2.2143            126.9
3 -3-4i       5          -2.2143           -126.9
4  3-4i       5          -0.9273            -53.1






12.3.5 Exercises 12.2

12.3.5.1 Bronze

Find the conjugate of \(4-7i\).
Find \(|3+4i|\).
Find \(|1-i|\).
Find \((2+3i)(2-3i)\).
Find the argument of \(1+i\).



12.3.5.2 Silver

Find \(\dfrac{5-2i}{2+i}\).
Find \(|2-5i|\).
Find the principal argument of \(-1+\sqrt3 i\).
Show that \(\dfrac{1}{3-4i}=\dfrac{3}{25}+\dfrac{4}{25}i\).



12.3.5.3 Gold

Find \(z\) if \(z+\bar z=6\) and \(z\bar z=25\), with \(\operatorname{Im}(z)>0\).
Find \(z\) if \(z-\bar z=10i\) and \(|z|=13\), with \(\operatorname{Re}(z)>0\).
Find all complex numbers \(z=x+iy\) such that \(z^2\) is real.
Find all complex numbers \(z=x+iy\) such that \(z^2\) is purely imaginary.
Find \(\arg\left(\dfrac{1+i}{\sqrt3+i}\right)\) in exact form.

title: 12  Complex numbers
text:
12.4 12.3 Polar form, multiplication and transformations

12.4.1 Key mathematical ideas
A complex number can be written in Cartesian form as
\[
z=x+iy.
\]
It can also be written in polar form as
\[
z=r(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta),
\]
where
\[
r=|z|
\]
and
\[
\theta=\arg z.
\]
If
\[
z_1=r_1(\cos\theta_1+i\sin\theta_1)
\]
and
\[
z_2=r_2(\cos\theta_2+i\sin\theta_2),
\]
then
\[
z_1z_2
=
r_1r_2\left(\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)+i\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right).
\]
Multiplication multiplies moduli and adds arguments. Division divides moduli and subtracts arguments:
\[
\dfrac{z_1}{z_2}
=
\dfrac{r_1}{r_2}\left(\cos(\theta_1-\theta_2)+i\sin(\theta_1-\theta_2)\right).
\]


12.4.2 Visualisation using R


Code
original <- c(1 + 0i, 0 + 1i, -1 + 0i, 0 - 1i)
multiplier <- 1 + 1i
transformed <- multiplier * original

points_df <- rbind(
  data.frame(x = Re(original), y = Im(original), set = "original"),
  data.frame(x = Re(transformed), y = Im(transformed), set = "after multiplying by 1 + i")
)

segments_df <- data.frame(
  x = Re(original),
  y = Im(original),
  xend = Re(transformed),
  yend = Im(transformed)
)

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(data = segments_df, aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend), alpha = 0.55) +
  geom_point(data = points_df, aes(x = x, y = y, shape = set), size = 3) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-2, 2), ylim = c(-2, 2)) +
  labs(
    title = "Complex multiplication as a transformation",
    subtitle = "Multiplication by 1 + i scales by sqrt(2) and rotates by pi/4.",
    x = "Re(z)",
    y = "Im(z)",
    shape = "Point set"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 12.3: Multiplication by 1 + i rotates by pi/4 and scales by sqrt(2).







12.4.3 Worked example
Write
\[
z=-1+\sqrt3 i
\]
in polar form.
First,
\[
r=\sqrt{(-1)^2+(\sqrt3)^2}=2.
\]
The point lies in Quadrant II, with reference angle
\[
\dfrac{\pi}{3}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\arg z=\dfrac{2\pi}{3}.
\]
Hence
\[
z=2\left(\cos\dfrac{2\pi}{3}+i\sin\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right).
\]


12.4.4 Worked example
Let
\[
z=\sqrt3+i
\]
and
\[
w=1+i.
\]
Find \(zw\) in polar form.
We have
\[
z=2\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi}{6}+i\sin\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)
\]
and
\[
w=\sqrt2\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi}{4}+i\sin\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right).
\]
Therefore,
\[
zw=2\sqrt2\left(\cos\dfrac{5\pi}{12}+i\sin\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right).
\]


12.4.5 Exercises 12.3

12.4.5.1 Bronze

Write \(1+i\) in polar form.
Write \(\sqrt3+i\) in polar form.
Write \(-1+i\) in polar form.
Convert \(2\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi}{3}+i\sin\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\) into Cartesian form.
Describe the effect of multiplying by \(i\).



12.4.5.2 Silver

Write \(-1+\sqrt3i\) in polar form.
Write \(-\sqrt3-i\) in polar form using the principal argument.
Find the polar form of \((1+i)(\sqrt3+i)\).
Find the polar form of \(\dfrac{\sqrt3+i}{1+i}\).
Describe the transformation caused by multiplying by \(2i\).



12.4.5.3 Gold

Find the polar form of \(-3+3\sqrt3i\).
Find the polar form of \(2-2\sqrt3i\) using the principal argument.
If \(z=4(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)\) and \(\operatorname{Re}(z)=2\), find possible values of \(\theta\) in \((-\pi,\pi]\).
If multiplying by \(a+bi\) rotates every point by \(\dfrac{\pi}{3}\) and scales every distance by \(2\), find \(a+bi\).
Show that \(|zw|=|z||w|\) when \(z\) and \(w\) are written in polar form.

title: 12  Complex numbers
text:
12.5 12.4 Equations with complex roots

12.5.1 Key mathematical ideas
Complex numbers allow equations such as
\[
x^2+1=0
\]
to have solutions:
\[
x=\pm i.
\]
For polynomials with real coefficients, non-real roots occur in conjugate pairs. Therefore, if
\[
2+3i
\]
is a root of a polynomial with real coefficients, then
\[
2-3i
\]
is also a root.
If a quadratic has roots
\[
2+3i
\quad\text{and}\quad
2-3i,
\]
then the quadratic is
\[
(x-(2+3i))(x-(2-3i)).
\]
This simplifies to
\[
(x-2)^2+9=x^2-4x+13.
\]


12.5.2 Visualisation using R


Code
root_points <- argand_points(
  z_values = c(2 + 3i, 2 - 3i),
  labels = c("2 + 3i", "2 - 3i")
)

ggplot(root_points, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), nudge_x = 0.25) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-1, 5), ylim = c(-4, 4)) +
  labs(
    title = "Conjugate pair of roots",
    subtitle = "For real coefficients, non-real roots occur in conjugate pairs.",
    x = "Re(z)",
    y = "Im(z)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 12.4: Complex conjugate roots are reflected in the real axis.







12.5.3 Worked example
Find the two square roots of
\[
3+4i.
\]
Let
\[
(a+bi)^2=3+4i.
\]
Expanding gives
\[
a^2-b^2+2abi=3+4i.
\]
Therefore,
\[
a^2-b^2=3
\]
and
\[
2ab=4.
\]
Also, taking moduli gives
\[
a^2+b^2=|3+4i|=5.
\]
Adding
\[
a^2-b^2=3
\]
and
\[
a^2+b^2=5
\]
gives
\[
2a^2=8,
\]
so
\[
a=\pm2.
\]
Since \(ab=2\), the roots are
\[
2+i
\quad\text{and}\quad
-2-i.
\]
Therefore,
\[
z=\pm(2+i).
\]


12.5.4 Exercises 12.4

12.5.4.1 Bronze

Solve \(x^2+9=0\).
Solve \(x^2-4x+13=0\).
If \(3+2i\) is a root of a real polynomial, write down another root.
Form the quadratic with roots \(1\pm2i\).
Find the sum and product of the roots \(4\pm i\).



12.5.4.2 Silver

Solve \(x^2+2x+10=0\).
Form the quadratic with roots \(-3\pm4i\).
Find the square roots of \(3+4i\).
Find the square roots of \(5-12i\).
A cubic with real coefficients has roots \(1\) and \(2+i\). Find the cubic with leading coefficient \(1\).



12.5.4.3 Gold

Find the square roots of \(-7+24i\).
A quartic with real coefficients has roots \(1+i\) and \(2+3i\). Form a quartic with leading coefficient \(1\).
Find \(a\) and \(b\) if \(x^2+ax+b=0\) has root \(3-2i\).
If \(z^2=8i\), find the roots in polar and Cartesian form.
Show that if a real quadratic has non-real roots, then its discriminant is negative.

title: 12  Complex numbers
section: 12.6 12.5 Loci and shaded regions in the complex plane
text:
12.6 12.5 Loci and shaded regions in the complex plane

12.6.1 Key mathematical ideas
Let
\[
z=x+iy.
\]
Then complex equations and inequalities can describe loci in the plane.
A circle locus has the form
\[
|z-(a+bi)|=r.
\]
This means the point \(z\) is distance \(r\) from the fixed point \(a+bi\). In Cartesian form,
\[
(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2=r^2.
\]
A perpendicular-bisector locus has the form
\[
|z-a|=|z-b|.
\]
This means the point \(z\) is equidistant from two fixed points.
An argument locus has the form
\[
\arg(z-a)=\theta.
\]
This is a ray starting from the point \(a\), at angle \(\theta\) to the positive real axis. The starting point is excluded because the argument is undefined there.


12.6.2 Visualisation using R: shaded circle region


Code
region_df <- region_grid(-2, 6, -3, 5)
region_df$inside <- (region_df$x - 2)^2 + (region_df$y - 1)^2 <= 9

circle_df <- circle_locus(2 + 1i, 3)
centre_df <- argand_points(2 + 1i, "2 + i")

ggplot() +
  geom_raster(data = subset(region_df, inside), aes(x = x, y = y), alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_path(data = circle_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = centre_df, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = centre_df, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_y = 0.35) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-2, 6), ylim = c(-3, 5)) +
  labs(
    title = "A modulus inequality gives a disk",
    subtitle = "|z - (2 + i)| <= 3 means within 3 units of 2 + i.",
    x = "Re(z)",
    y = "Im(z)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 12.5: The shaded region |z - (2 + i)| <= 3.







12.6.3 Visualisation using R: shaded perpendicular-bisector region


Code
region_df <- region_grid(-1, 7, -3, 6)
region_df$inside <- (region_df$x - 1)^2 + region_df$y^2 <=
  (region_df$x - 5)^2 + (region_df$y - 2)^2

line_df <- data.frame(x = seq(-1, 7, length.out = 400))
line_df$y <- 7 - 2 * line_df$x

fixed_points <- argand_points(
  z_values = c(1 + 0i, 5 + 2i),
  labels = c("1", "5 + 2i")
)

ggplot() +
  geom_raster(data = subset(region_df, inside), aes(x = x, y = y), alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_line(data = line_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = fixed_points, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = fixed_points, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_y = 0.35) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-1, 7), ylim = c(-3, 6)) +
  labs(
    title = "A distance comparison gives a half-plane",
    subtitle = "The boundary is the perpendicular bisector of 1 and 5 + 2i.",
    x = "Re(z)",
    y = "Im(z)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 12.6: The shaded region |z - 1| <= |z - (5 + 2i)|.







12.6.4 Visualisation using R: shaded argument region


Code
region_df <- region_grid(-1, 7, -1, 6)
region_df$dx <- region_df$x - 1
region_df$dy <- region_df$y - 1
region_df$inside <- region_df$dx > 0 & region_df$dy >= 0 & region_df$dy <= region_df$dx

ray_1 <- ray_locus(1 + 1i, 0, length_value = 7)
ray_2 <- ray_locus(1 + 1i, pi / 4, length_value = 7)
start_df <- argand_points(1 + 1i, "1 + i")

ggplot() +
  geom_raster(data = subset(region_df, inside), aes(x = x, y = y), alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_path(data = ray_1, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_path(data = ray_2, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = start_df, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3, shape = 1) +
  geom_text(data = start_df, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_y = 0.35) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-1, 7), ylim = c(-1, 6)) +
  labs(
    title = "An argument inequality gives an angular region",
    subtitle = "The starting point is excluded because the argument is undefined.",
    x = "Re(z)",
    y = "Im(z)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 12.7: The shaded region 0 <= arg(z - (1 + i)) <= pi/4.







12.6.5 Worked example
Find the Cartesian equation of
\[
|z-(2+i)|=3.
\]
Let
\[
z=x+iy.
\]
Then
\[
z-(2+i)=(x-2)+i(y-1).
\]
So
\[
|z-(2+i)|=\sqrt{(x-2)^2+(y-1)^2}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\sqrt{(x-2)^2+(y-1)^2}=3.
\]
Squaring gives
\[
(x-2)^2+(y-1)^2=9.
\]


12.6.6 Exercises 12.5

12.6.6.1 Bronze

Write the Cartesian equation of \(|z-(2+i)|=3\).
Sketch and describe \(|z|=4\).
Sketch and describe \(\operatorname{Im}(z)=2\).
Sketch and describe \(\operatorname{Re}(z)=-1\).
Describe the locus \(\arg z=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\).



12.6.6.2 Silver

Write the Cartesian equation of \(|z-(3-2i)|=5\).
Find the Cartesian equation of \(|z-1|=|z-(5+2i)|\).
Shade the region \(|z-(2+i)|\leq3\).
Shade the region \(|z-1|\leq|z-(5+2i)|\).
Describe the region \(0\leq\arg(z-(1+i))\leq\dfrac{\pi}{4}\).



12.6.6.3 Gold

Find the intersection points of \(|z-(3+2i)|=2\) and \(\arg(z-1)=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\).
Find the least and greatest possible values of \(|z|\) if \(|z-(8+6i)|=5\).
Find the greatest and least possible values of \(\arg z\) if \(|z-(5+5i)|=5\).
Explain geometrically why \(|z-a|=|z-b|\) is a perpendicular bisector.
Find the Cartesian inequality represented by \(|z-1|<|z-(5+2i)|\).

title: 12  Complex numbers
text:
12.7 12.6 Mixed problem solving

12.7.1 Worked example: intersection of loci
Find all complex numbers satisfying
\[
|z-(3+2i)|=2
\]
and
\[
\arg(z-1)=\dfrac{\pi}{4}.
\]
Let
\[
z=x+iy.
\]
The circle is
\[
(x-3)^2+(y-2)^2=4.
\]
The argument condition means the ray from \(1\) at angle \(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\), so
\[
y=x-1,
\quad x>1.
\]
Substitute into the circle:
\[
(x-3)^2+(x-3)^2=4.
\]
Thus
\[
2(x-3)^2=4,
\]
so
\[
x=3\pm\sqrt2.
\]
Since both values satisfy \(x>1\),
\[
y=2\pm\sqrt2.
\]
Therefore,
\[
z=(3+\sqrt2)+(2+\sqrt2)i
\]
or
\[
z=(3-\sqrt2)+(2-\sqrt2)i.
\]


12.7.2 Visualisation using R


Code
circle_df <- circle_locus(3 + 2i, 2)
ray_df <- ray_locus(1 + 0i, pi / 4, length_value = 7)
intersection_df <- data.frame(
  x = c(3 - sqrt(2), 3 + sqrt(2)),
  y = c(2 - sqrt(2), 2 + sqrt(2)),
  label = c("lower point", "upper point")
)

ggplot() +
  geom_path(data = circle_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_path(data = ray_df, aes(x = x, y = y), linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(data = intersection_df, aes(x = x, y = y), size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = intersection_df, aes(x = x, y = y, label = label), nudge_y = 0.35) +
  axis_layers() +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-1, 7), ylim = c(-1, 6)) +
  labs(
    title = "Intersecting complex loci",
    subtitle = "The algebra confirms the points suggested by the Argand diagram.",
    x = "Re(z)",
    y = "Im(z)"
  ) +
  book_theme()








Figure 12.8: Intersections of |z - (3 + 2i)| = 2 and arg(z - 1) = pi/4.







12.7.3 Mixed exercises

Find all roots of \(z^2=3+4i\).
Find the quadratic with real coefficients whose roots are \(4\pm3i\).
Write \(-2+2\sqrt3i\) in polar form.
Describe the transformation caused by multiplication by \(-2i\).
Find the Cartesian equation of \(|z+2-i|=4\).
Find the Cartesian inequality represented by \(|z-(1+i)|\leq2\).
Find the Cartesian equation of \(|z-2|=|z-(4+4i)|\).
Find the exact values of \(z\) satisfying \(|z-(3+2i)|=2\) and \(\arg(z-1)=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\).
Find the least and greatest possible values of \(|z|\) if \(|z-(8+6i)|=5\).
Find the greatest and least possible values of \(\arg z\) if \(|z-(5+5i)|=5\).

title: 12  Complex numbers
text:
12.8 STEP-style extension questions

12.8.1 STEP-style Question 1
Let
\[
z=x+iy.
\]
Prove that
\[
z\bar z=|z|^2.
\]
Use this result to derive the division formula for
\[
\dfrac{a+bi}{c+di}.
\]


12.8.2 STEP-style Question 2
Find all complex numbers satisfying
\[
z^2=3+4i.
\]
Represent the roots on an Argand diagram and explain why the roots are opposite points.


12.8.3 STEP-style Question 3
Let
\[
z=r(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta).
\]
Show that multiplication by \(z\) maps any complex number \(w\) to a point obtained by rotating \(w\) through angle \(\theta\) and scaling its distance from the origin by \(r\).


12.8.4 STEP-style Question 4
The complex number \(z\) satisfies
\[
|z-(3+2i)|=2
\]
and
\[
\arg(z-1)=\dfrac{\pi}{4}.
\]
Find all possible values of \(z\) exactly.


12.8.5 STEP-style Question 5
Let \(z=x+iy\). The locus of \(z\) is
\[
|z-(5+5i)|=5.
\]
Find the greatest and least possible values of \(\arg z\), explaining your reasoning geometrically.

title: 12  Complex numbers
text:
12.9 Reflection questions

Why do we need the number \(i\)?
What does it mean to compare real and imaginary parts?
Why is the conjugate useful for division?
Why is \(|z|\) interpreted as a distance?
Why must arguments be handled carefully in different quadrants?
Why does multiplication by \(i\) rotate a point by \(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\)?
Why do non-real roots of real polynomials occur in conjugate pairs?
Why does \(|z-a|=r\) represent a circle?
Why does \(|z-a|=|z-b|\) represent a perpendicular bisector?
What can an Argand diagram show that algebra alone may hide?

title: 12  Complex numbers
text:
12.10 Answers

12.10.1 Answers to Exercises 12.1

12.10.1.1 Bronze

\(i^2+i^4=-1+1=0\).
Real part \(5\), imaginary part \(-3\).
\(a=7\) and \(b=2\).
\(6+2i\).
\(4-7i\).



12.10.1.2 Silver

\(a=5\) and \(b=5\).
\(i^{17}=i\).
\((3+2i)(1-4i)=11-10i\).
\(a=3\) and \(b=-5\).
\((1+i)^2=1+2i+i^2=2i\).



12.10.1.3 Gold

\((4+3i)^2=7+24i\), so \(a=4\) and \(b=3\).
\((x+2i)^2=x^2-4+4xi\), so \(x=0\).
\((3+yi)^2=9-y^2+6yi\). For this to be purely imaginary, \(9-y^2=0\), so \(y=\pm3\).
\(z+\bar z=2x=10\), so \(x=5\).
\(z-\bar z=2iy=8i\), so \(y=4\).




12.10.2 Answers to Exercises 12.2

12.10.2.1 Bronze

\(4+7i\).
\(5\).
\(\sqrt2\).
\(13\).
\(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\).



12.10.2.2 Silver

\(\dfrac{3+4i}{1+i}=\dfrac{7+i}{2}\).
\(\dfrac{5-2i}{2+i}=\dfrac{8-9i}{5}\).
\(\sqrt{29}\).
\(\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\).
Multiplying by the conjugate gives \(\dfrac{1}{3-4i}=\dfrac{3+4i}{25}\).



12.10.2.3 Gold

\(z+\bar z=6\) gives \(x=3\). Since \(z\bar z=|z|^2=25\), we have \(9+y^2=25\). Since \(\operatorname{Im}(z)>0\), \(y=4\), so \(z=3+4i\).
\(z-\bar z=10i\) gives \(y=5\). Since \(|z|=13\), \(x^2+25=169\). Since \(\operatorname{Re}(z)>0\), \(x=12\), so \(z=12+5i\).
\(z^2=x^2-y^2+2xyi\) is real when \(2xy=0\), so \(x=0\) or \(y=0\).
\(z^2=x^2-y^2+2xyi\) is purely imaginary when \(x^2-y^2=0\), so \(y=\pm x\).
\(\arg(1+i)-\arg(\sqrt3+i)=\dfrac{\pi}{4}-\dfrac{\pi}{6}=\dfrac{\pi}{12}\).




12.10.3 Answers to Exercises 12.3

12.10.3.1 Bronze

\(\sqrt2\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi}{4}+i\sin\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)\).
\(2\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi}{6}+i\sin\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)\).
\(\sqrt2\left(\cos\dfrac{3\pi}{4}+i\sin\dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right)\).
\(1+\sqrt3 i\).
Multiplication by \(i\) is rotation by \(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\) anticlockwise about the origin.



12.10.3.2 Silver

\(2\left(\cos\dfrac{2\pi}{3}+i\sin\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)\).
\(2\left(\cos\left(-\dfrac{5\pi}{6}\right)+i\sin\left(-\dfrac{5\pi}{6}\right)\right)\).
\(2\sqrt2\left(\cos\dfrac{5\pi}{12}+i\sin\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)\).
\(\sqrt2\left(\cos\left(-\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)+i\sin\left(-\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)\right)\).
Multiplication by \(2i\) is scale factor \(2\) and rotation by \(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\) anticlockwise.



12.10.3.3 Gold

\(6\left(\cos\dfrac{2\pi}{3}+i\sin\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)\).
\(4\left(\cos\left(-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)+i\sin\left(-\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\right)\).
\(4\cos\theta=2\), so \(\cos\theta=\dfrac{1}{2}\). Hence \(\theta=\pm\dfrac{\pi}{3}\).
\(a+bi=2\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi}{3}+i\sin\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)=1+\sqrt3 i\).
If \(z=r(\cos\alpha+i\sin\alpha)\) and \(w=s(\cos\beta+i\sin\beta)\), then \(zw=rs(\cos(\alpha+\beta)+i\sin(\alpha+\beta))\), so \(|zw|=rs=|z||w|\).




12.10.4 Answers to Exercises 12.4

12.10.4.1 Bronze

\(x=\pm3i\).
\(x=2\pm3i\).
\(3-2i\).
\((x-(1+2i))(x-(1-2i))=(x-1)^2+4=x^2-2x+5\).
Sum \(8\), product \(17\).



12.10.4.2 Silver

\(x=-1\pm3i\).
\((x+3)^2+16=x^2+6x+25\).
\(\pm(2+i)\).
\(\pm(3-2i)\).
The cubic is \((x-1)((x-2)^2+1)=x^3-5x^2+9x-5\).



12.10.4.3 Gold

The square roots of \(-7+24i\) are \(\pm(3+4i)\).
The roots are \(1\pm i\) and \(2\pm3i\). The quartic is \(((x-1)^2+1)((x-2)^2+9)=x^4-6x^3+23x^2-34x+26\).
The other root is \(3+2i\). The sum is \(6\) and the product is \(13\). Since the quadratic is \(x^2+ax+b=0\), \(a=-6\) and \(b=13\).
\(8i=8\left(\cos\dfrac{\pi}{2}+i\sin\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\). The square roots have modulus \(2\sqrt2\) and arguments \(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\) and \(\dfrac{5\pi}{4}\), giving \(2+2i\) and \(-2-2i\).
A real quadratic with non-real roots has no real roots, so its graph does not cross the real axis and its discriminant is negative.




12.10.5 Answers to Exercises 12.5

12.10.5.1 Bronze

\((x-2)^2+(y-1)^2=9\).
A circle centred at the origin with radius \(4\).
The horizontal line \(y=2\).
The vertical line \(x=-1\).
A ray from the origin at angle \(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\), excluding the origin.



12.10.5.2 Silver

\((x-3)^2+(y+2)^2=25\).
\((x-1)^2+y^2=(x-5)^2+(y-2)^2\), which simplifies to \(2x+y=7\).
The closed disk centred at \((2,1)\) with radius \(3\).
The half-plane \(2x+y\leq7\).
The sector starting at \((1,1)\) between the positive real direction and the line of gradient \(1\), excluding the starting point.



12.10.5.3 Gold

\(z=(3+\sqrt2)+(2+\sqrt2)i\) or \(z=(3-\sqrt2)+(2-\sqrt2)i\).
The centre \(8+6i\) has distance \(10\) from the origin and the circle has radius \(5\), so the least value is \(5\) and the greatest value is \(15\).
The centre has argument \(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\) and distance \(5\sqrt2\) from the origin. The tangent angle satisfies \(\sin\alpha=\dfrac{5}{5\sqrt2}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt2}\), so \(\alpha=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\). Hence the least argument is \(0\) and the greatest argument is \(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\).
The locus is the set of points equidistant from \(a\) and \(b\), so it is the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining \(a\) and \(b\).
Expanding gives \((x-1)^2+y^2<(x-5)^2+(y-2)^2\), so \(2x+y<7\).




12.10.6 Answers to Mixed exercises

\(z=2+i\) or \(z=-2-i\).
\((x-4)^2+9=x^2-8x+25\).
\(4\left(\cos\dfrac{2\pi}{3}+i\sin\dfrac{2\pi}{3}\right)\).
Multiplication by \(-2i\) is scale factor \(2\) and rotation by \(-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\), or equivalently \(\dfrac{3\pi}{2}\).
Since \(z+2-i=z-(-2+i)\), the equation is \((x+2)^2+(y-1)^2=16\).
\((x-1)^2+(y-1)^2\leq4\).
\((x-2)^2+y^2=(x-4)^2+(y-4)^2\), which simplifies to \(x+2y=7\).
\(z=(3+\sqrt2)+(2+\sqrt2)i\) or \(z=(3-\sqrt2)+(2-\sqrt2)i\).
Least value \(5\), greatest value \(15\).
Least argument \(0\), greatest argument \(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\).



12.10.7 Answers to STEP-style extension questions

If \(z=x+iy\), then \(\bar z=x-iy\). Hence \[
z\bar z=(x+iy)(x-iy)=x^2+y^2=|z|^2.
\] Therefore, \[
\dfrac{a+bi}{c+di}
=
\dfrac{(a+bi)(c-di)}{c^2+d^2}
=
\dfrac{ac+bd}{c^2+d^2}+\dfrac{bc-ad}{c^2+d^2}i.
\]
Let \(z=a+bi\). Then \(a^2-b^2=3\) and \(2ab=4\). Also \(a^2+b^2=5\). Hence \(a^2=4\) and \(ab=2\), giving \(z=2+i\) or \(z=-2-i\). These are opposite points on the Argand diagram because one is the negative of the other.
Write \(w=s(\cos\phi+i\sin\phi)\). Then \[
zw=rs(\cos(\theta+\phi)+i\sin(\theta+\phi)).
\] So the modulus is multiplied by \(r\) and the argument is increased by \(\theta\).
The ray gives \(y=x-1\) with \(x>1\). Substitution into \((x-3)^2+(y-2)^2=4\) gives \(2(x-3)^2=4\), so \(x=3\pm\sqrt2\). Therefore \[
z=(3+\sqrt2)+(2+\sqrt2)i
\] or \[
z=(3-\sqrt2)+(2-\sqrt2)i.
\]
The circle has centre \((5,5)\) and radius \(5\). The line from the origin to the centre has argument \(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\) and length \(5\sqrt2\). The tangent angle satisfies \[
\sin\alpha=\dfrac{5}{5\sqrt2}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt2},
\] so \(\alpha=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\). Hence the least argument is \(0\) and the greatest argument is \(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\).
